host-lifecycle, bump the host-template pointer, re-pin <code>.host-software</code>, record the receipts and a <code>call/</code> decision that supersedes the scope of <code>call/0026</code> and carries <code>call/0027</code> to completion, and bump the CI install pins. The released binary gates green and the whole suite is green.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #validate-4b</li> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h3 id="retire-shim"><a class="header" href="#retire-shim">Retire the legacy per-member marker</a></h3> <p>Retire the deprecate-then-retire shim: a surviving per-member <code>front-door = true</code> or <code>entrance = true</code> becomes a loud problem in every consumer, never the entrance. Add the spine retirement doctrine and an adopter <code>UPGRADING</code> entry, gated so the live spine no longer teaches the legacy marker. Ship a host-lifecycle release, then re-pin and adopt.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #release-and-re-pin</li> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h2 id="risks-3"><a class="header" href="#risks-3">Risks</a></h2> <ul> <li>The milestone touches the spine, the tool, and the agentic-host declaration at once. The tool gains the stanza before the spine documents it and agentic-host moves to it, and the shim accepts the legacy marker throughout, so no state is half-migrated.</li> <li>The declaration adds a surface every adopter could meet, so it stays one stanza: a member, a document path, a closed concept set with a <code>true</code> sentinel, validated loudly, a single entrance per project.</li> <li>The generality is built ahead of a second real entrance. The honest scope keeps the claim true to what the tool reads, so the machinery is ready without over-claiming a coverage it cannot yet exercise.</li> </ul> <h2 id="status-4"><a class="header" href="#status-4">Status</a></h2> <p>complete, released as host-lifecycle v0.29.0 (2026-06-25; <code>dbeb3db</code>, artifact <code>01171b92</code>). The entrance check reads a singleton <code>[entrance]</code> stanza in <code>.host-software</code> that names the member and the document within it (default <code>README.md</code>), held complete against the concepts it <code>restates</code>, reaching any document by path, the standalone sibling of reconcile. A three-reviewer adversarial design review returned a re-scope verdict; the operator re-cut it to expand the milestone, folding the review's blocking findings in as design requirements. A three-reviewer adversarial code review (<code>code-review.md</code>) then found and fixed two blocking defects before release: the parse <code>problems</code> reached only the entrance command, so a typo'd member re-demoted the front door through reconcile; and an empty <code>restates</code> checked nothing yet reported clean. A Qwen-3.5-4B run validated the declaration form and the stanza ergonomics (<code>gather-data.md</code>). host-template <code>ba86125</code> carries the doctrine and the adopter <code>UPGRADING</code> entry; agentic-host migrated its <code>.host-software</code> to the stanza and adopted the revision. Supersedes the agentic-host-local scope of plan/0040 and the scope half of <code>call/0026</code>, carries <code>call/0027</code> to completion, and is recorded in <code>call/0028</code>. The legacy per-member marker was then retired (host-lifecycle v0.30.0, host-template <code>de8a517</code>): a surviving one is a loud problem in every consumer, which completes the deprecate-then-retire path. The whole suite is green.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="plan0044-lexicon-masking-reaches-the-prose-lane"><a class="header" href="#plan0044-lexicon-masking-reaches-the-prose-lane">plan/0044: LEXICON masking reaches the prose lane</a></h1> <p>The LEXICON allowlist (host-lint issue #13) masks a sanctioned phrase before the naming lane classifies a line, so a legitimate tell-shaped token a project declares stops flagging. The prose lane, the host-grammar density engine behind <code>--prose</code> and <code>--docs</code>, never consulted that allowlist, so a legitimate domain word the prose grammar reads as an ai-diction trope had no sanctioned escape. host-lint issue #16 reports the divergence. This milestone extends the existing pre-detection mask to the prose lane, so one declaration serves both lanes.</p> <h2 id="problem-4"><a class="header" href="#problem-4">Problem</a></h2> <p>The two lanes take different paths. The naming lane masks each line with the sanctioned phrases before it classifies; the prose lane hands the raw document to the host-grammar engine and never sees the allowlist. So a project that legitimately uses a domain noun the grammar over-reads (the reporter's case is the noun <code>harness</code>, the name of a <code>rehost harness</code> used identically across its codebase) is left with two options, rename the term or carry the warning, while the naming lane would have honoured a declaration.</p> <p>This contradicts the living-grammar doctrine, which names the per-project LEXICON as the home for a token the shared grammar flags but the project judges legitimate. For a prose trope that home did not exist: the only inline-source escape is the block-level <code>host-lint:ignore</code> fence, and there is no per-project prose allowlist. The README also overclaims. It says a declared phrase never flags "in any mode", though the enumerated modes are the naming modes alone.</p> <h2 id="decision-operator-1"><a class="header" href="#decision-operator-1">Decision (operator)</a></h2> <p>Of the three options the issue lists, the operator chose the first, extend the mask, and set aside a separate prose allowlist and documenting the gap as intentional. One declaration in the existing LEXICON serves both lanes; the prose lane reuses the same pre-detection blank-out the naming lane already performs. The decision and its alternatives are recorded in <code>call/0029</code>, validated at the weak-agent bar in <code>gather-data.md</code>.</p> <h2 id="the-design-1"><a class="header" href="#the-design-1">The design</a></h2> <h3 id="the-mask-before-both-the-per-tell-scan-and-the-density-score"><a class="header" href="#the-mask-before-both-the-per-tell-scan-and-the-density-score">The mask, before both the per-tell scan and the density score</a></h3> <p><code>scan_prose_text</code> masks the whole document with <code>mask_allowed</code>, the naming lane's blank-out (byte-length-preserving, word-boundaried, case-insensitive), before the host-grammar pass. The masked text feeds both the per-tell scan and the document density score, so a declared phrase contributes to neither. Because the mask preserves byte length, the offsets that locate each surviving tell still index the original document, and the reported excerpt stays the author's own text.</p> <h3 id="surgical-not-blanket"><a class="header" href="#surgical-not-blanket">Surgical, not blanket</a></h3> <p>A declared multi-word phrase clears the trope only within that phrase; a standalone occurrence of the same word still flags. Declaring <code>rehost harness</code> clears <code>harness</code> inside that phrase and leaves a later standalone <code>harness</code> reported, the same word-boundary specificity the naming lane already carries.</p> <h3 id="no-new-abuse-surface"><a class="header" href="#no-new-abuse-surface">No new abuse surface</a></h3> <p>The prose lane reuses the same provenance-gated entries the naming lane masks with: an entry must hold a letter, may not launder a flag-tier tell, and a tracker reference must carry a URL. So the prose lane grants no muting power the naming lane lacked. The density gate divides the weighted tell count by the sentence count, so masking a phrase removes only its own weight from the numerator and cannot inflate the denominator to push other, undeclared tells under the threshold. A weak-agent run confirmed this reading: handed honest options, including holding for a separate stricter allowlist, the model judged the reuse adequately bounded on the no-new-power argument.</p> <h3 id="the-readme-reconciliation"><a class="header" href="#the-readme-reconciliation">The README reconciliation</a></h3> <p>The masked-modes wording adds <code>--prose</code> and <code>--docs</code>, so the "in any mode" claim becomes true rather than narrowed to the naming modes.</p> <h3 id="no-spine-change"><a class="header" href="#no-spine-change">No spine change</a></h3> <p>Extending the mask makes the existing doctrine, that a legitimate tell-shaped token stays in the per-project LEXICON, hold for the prose lane. The doctrine is met, not changed, so host-template carries no new UPGRADING entry; an adopter reads the corrected behaviour on the next host-lint bump.</p> <h2 id="build-sequence-4"><a class="header" href="#build-sequence-4">Build sequence</a></h2> <h3 id="settle-by-data"><a class="header" href="#settle-by-data">Settle the approach with data</a></h3> <p>Settle the lane-consistency reading, the surgical word-boundary semantics, and the abuse question with a recorded Qwen-3.5-4B run, in <code>gather-data.md</code>, run direct against rope with a real system prompt and non-contaminated options.</p> <ul> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h3 id="extend-mask"><a class="header" href="#extend-mask">Extend the mask to the prose lane</a></h3> <p>Thread the sanctioned phrases into <code>scan_prose_text</code> and <code>run_docs</code>, mask the document before the per-tell scan and the density score, and report each surviving tell from the original text. Regression tests pin the behaviour: a declared phrase clears the trope within the phrase, a standalone occurrence of the same word still flags, an empty allowlist leaves the scan unchanged, and the density score drops by the masked phrase alone.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #settle-by-data</li> <li>verify: cd software/host-lint/main && cargo test</li> </ul> <h3 id="readme-claim"><a class="header" href="#readme-claim">Reconcile the README claim</a></h3> <p>Add <code>--prose</code> and <code>--docs</code> to the masked-modes wording so the "in any mode" claim holds. host-lint prose stays clean.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #extend-mask</li> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h3 id="release-and-close"><a class="header" href="#release-and-close">Release, re-pin, and close</a></h3> <p>Release host-lint with the change class that removes findings, re-pin <code>.host-software</code>, bump the CI install revisions, and close issue #16. The released binary gates green and the whole suite is green.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #readme-claim</li> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h2 id="risks-4"><a class="header" href="#risks-4">Risks</a></h2> <ul> <li>The mask now feeds the density engine, so a mis-scoped entry could hide a real trope. The provenance guards and the per-occurrence word boundary keep the mask specific, and the sentence-count denominator stops a mask from diluting the other tells.</li> <li>The change removes prose findings when a phrase is declared, so it ships under the removes-findings change class, and an adopter reads the masked-modes wording to learn the new reach.</li> </ul> <h2 id="status-5"><a class="header" href="#status-5">Status</a></h2> <p>complete, released as host-lint v0.10.1 (2026-06-25; <code>0c2bfc3</code>, artifact <code>941126c9</code>). The <code>--prose</code> and <code>--docs</code> lanes consult the per-repo LEXICON: <code>scan_prose_text</code> masks a declared phrase with the naming lane's <code>mask_allowed</code> before both the per-tell scan and the document density score, so a declared <code>rehost harness</code> clears the trope on <code>harness</code> within that phrase and a standalone occurrence still flags. The README masked-modes wording adds <code>--prose</code> and <code>--docs</code>. Validated at the Qwen-3.5-4B bar (<code>gather-data.md</code>): lane consistency, the surgical word boundary, and the abuse-bounding confirmed, with the dissent option available and not chosen. The first abuse-guard probe was contaminated by an option asserting a multi-word rule the tool does not enforce; it was recorded and discarded, then re-run with honest options. <code>call/0029</code> records the decision. No spine change, since the doctrine is met rather than changed. The <code>src/lib.rs</code> edit staled two Kani-backed obligations (<code>DetectInternalCodeAsName</code>), which host-lint's own obligations lane caught after the first tag; the feature shipped as v0.10.0, then v0.10.1 re-derived the obligation digests through host-prove and is the pinned release. host-lint issue #16 is closed. The whole suite is green.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="plan0045-the-embedded-prose-engine-catches-up"><a class="header" href="#plan0045-the-embedded-prose-engine-catches-up">plan/0045: The embedded prose engine catches up</a></h1> <p>plan/0044 taught host-lint's prose lane to honor the per-repo LEXICON, so a legitimate domain noun a project declares stops reading as an ai-diction trope. host-lifecycle runs that same lane in-process for its verify-phase prose recheck (<code>host-lifecycle prose</code>, the <code>--docs</code> engine via the linked <code>host_lint</code> crate), so the gate needs no host-lint on PATH. But host-lifecycle embedded the crate at an older revision, before the mask existed, so the recheck ignored the LEXICON the standalone binary already honored. connollydavid/host-lifecycle#2 reports the divergence. This milestone shares host-lint's docs engine into its library, so the binary and the embedded copy run one engine and cannot drift, then bumps host-lifecycle onto it.</p> <h2 id="problem-5"><a class="header" href="#problem-5">Problem</a></h2> <p>The prose mask (<code>mask_allowed</code>) lived in the <code>host_lint</code> library and was current. But host-lifecycle's <code>prose_audit</code> reimplemented the <code>--docs</code> walk and called <code>scan_prose_text</code> at the crate revision it pinned, host-lint v0.8.1, whose signature had no allowlist parameter. The embedded engine could not consult the LEXICON even in principle: it received no phrases to mask. Because <code>host-lifecycle prose</code> exits non-zero on any warning, an adopter with a legitimate flagged domain noun on a governed surface, the case the issue names, had a permanently failing verify recheck and no remedy, since the sanctioned escape (declare the phrase in LEXICON) was the one path the embedded engine never took.</p> <p>The loader (<code>load_lexicon</code>) and the walk (<code>run_docs</code>) lived in host-lint's binary; the library held only the parsing primitives, so host-lifecycle had no shared entry point to call. The reimplemented walk was itself the drift surface: even with the crate bumped, an embedder that re-derives the loader and walk by hand stays one edit away from lagging again.</p> <h2 id="decision-operator-2"><a class="header" href="#decision-operator-2">Decision (operator)</a></h2> <p>Share the engine into the library, rather than replicate the loader inside host-lifecycle. <code>Lexicon</code>, <code>load_lexicon</code>, and <code>run_docs</code> move into the <code>host_lint</code> library as public functions; host-lint's binary calls them; host-lifecycle calls the same functions. The embedded prose recheck becomes a thin call into the shared engine, so a future host-lint change to the loader, the walk, or the mask reaches the embedder on the next bump with no hand re-derivation. The operator chose the shared-library path over the one-release replicate-in-host-lifecycle path precisely because the bug is an instance of embedded-engine drift, and the fix should close the class, not the instance.</p> <h2 id="the-design-2"><a class="header" href="#the-design-2">The design</a></h2> <h3 id="loader-and-walk-in-the-shared-library"><a class="header" href="#loader-and-walk-in-the-shared-library">Loader and walk in the shared library</a></h3> <p><code>Lexicon</code> plus <code>load_lexicon</code> and <code>run_docs</code> become public in <code>host_lint</code>'s lib. <code>run_docs</code> returns a <code>Result</code> instead of exiting the process, so host-lint's binary prints the error and exits while an in-process embedder surfaces it as it chooses. host-lint's CLI behavior is unchanged, confirmed against the fixture: a LEXICON-declared phrase clears the trope (exit 0), an undeclared occurrence still warns (exit 3), and the git-failure message and exit code are byte-identical to before.</p> <h3 id="host-lifecycle-calls-the-engine"><a class="header" href="#host-lifecycle-calls-the-engine">host-lifecycle calls the engine</a></h3> <p><code>prose_audit</code> shrinks to <code>host_lint::load_lexicon(root).phrases_lc</code> plus <code>host_lint::run_docs(root, &allow, &ignore)</code>. The reimplemented walk is gone, so the walk, the loader, and the mask are all shared with the standalone binary. A regression test pins the fix: a declared phrase clears the trope in the in-process audit, and an undeclared occurrence of the same word still warns.</p> <h3 id="the-moved-dependency-needs-a-fresh-bundle"><a class="header" href="#the-moved-dependency-needs-a-fresh-bundle">The moved dependency needs a fresh bundle</a></h3> <p>host-lifecycle pins host-lint by git revision and builds offline from a hash-pinned vendor bundle (plan/0032). Bumping the revision is the first dependency move since the bundle was cut, so the shared bundle is regenerated as <code>vendor-v2</code>, covering host-lint v0.10.2's source, and host-lifecycle's <code>deps-bundle</code> is re-pinned in <code>.host-software</code> and <code>deps-bundle.lock</code>. host-lint stays on <code>vendor-v1</code>: its own closure is unchanged, and its build ignores the host-lint-crate entry the bundle also carries.</p> <h3 id="no-spine-change-1"><a class="header" href="#no-spine-change-1">No spine change</a></h3> <p>The doctrine, that a legitimate tell-shaped token stays in the per-project LEXICON for both lanes, is met rather than changed, so host-template carries no new UPGRADING entry; an adopter reads the corrected behaviour on the next host-lifecycle bump. The meta-repo prose CI pins (host-template, host) move to v0.30.1 so their own prose gate runs the same engine.</p> <h2 id="build-sequence-5"><a class="header" href="#build-sequence-5">Build sequence</a></h2> <h3 id="reproduce"><a class="header" href="#reproduce">Diagnose and reproduce</a></h3> <p>Build a minimal fixture (a tracked doc whose ai-diction noun is declared in a sibling LEXICON) and confirm the divergence: standalone <code>host-lint --docs</code> masks the phrase and exits clean, while <code>host-lifecycle prose</code> warns and exits non-zero on the same repo. Trace the root cause to the embedded crate revision whose <code>scan_prose_text</code> takes no allowlist.</p> <ul> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h3 id="share-engine"><a class="header" href="#share-engine">Share host-lint's docs engine into the library</a></h3> <p>Move <code>Lexicon</code>, <code>load_lexicon</code>, and <code>run_docs</code> into the <code>host_lint</code> library as public items; have the binary call them; make <code>run_docs</code> return a <code>Result</code>. Regression tests pin the loader and the masked walk. host-lint's CLI behavior stays identical, and clippy is clean.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #reproduce</li> <li>verify: cd software/host-lint/main && cargo test</li> </ul> <h3 id="consume-engine"><a class="header" href="#consume-engine">host-lifecycle consumes the shared engine</a></h3> <p>Bump the host-lint dependency to v0.10.2, rewrite <code>prose_audit</code> to call the shared loader and walk, and add the LEXICON-masking regression test. The rebuilt binary clears the fixture: a declared phrase exits clean, an undeclared occurrence still warns.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #share-engine</li> <li>verify: cd software/host-lifecycle/main && cargo test</li> </ul> <h3 id="vendor-v2"><a class="header" href="#vendor-v2">Regenerate the vendor bundle for the moved dependency</a></h3> <p>Produce <code>vendor-v2</code> from host-lint's manifest synced with host-lifecycle's, covering host-lint v0.10.2's source, publish it, and re-pin host-lifecycle's <code>deps-bundle</code>. The offline <code>--network none</code> release build reproduces the canonical hash.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #consume-engine</li> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h3 id="release-and-close"><a class="header" href="#release-and-close">Re-pin, bump CI, and close</a></h3> <p>Release host-lint v0.10.2 and host-lifecycle v0.30.1, re-pin <code>.host-software</code>, bump the five CI install revisions, record the release receipts, and close the issue. <code>software --check</code> is clean and the whole suite is green.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #vendor-v2</li> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h2 id="risks-5"><a class="header" href="#risks-5">Risks</a></h2> <ul> <li>The loader and walk now live in the library, so an embedder and the CLI share one code path. A bug in <code>run_docs</code> would reach both, but a single shared path is the point: it removes the drift this milestone fixes, and the path is covered by tests on both sides.</li> <li>Regenerating the vendor bundle is the documented cost of moving a pinned dependency. The new bundle is hash-pinned and its offline build is verified by the release gate, so a wrong bundle fails the gate rather than shipping.</li> </ul> <h2 id="status-6"><a class="header" href="#status-6">Status</a></h2> <p>complete, released as host-lint v0.10.3 (<code>63348a6</code>, artifact <code>753ac4f6</code>) and host-lifecycle v0.30.1 (<code>46d481c</code>, artifact <code>23c27bff</code>, on <code>vendor-v2</code> sha <code>4e49536b</code>); host-lifecycle embeds the host-lint library at v0.10.2 (<code>5a9d2c5</code>), which is engine-identical to v0.10.3. host-lint's <code>Lexicon</code>, <code>load_lexicon</code>, and <code>run_docs</code> are public library items; its binary and host-lifecycle's <code>prose_audit</code> call the same engine, so the embedded prose recheck cannot lag a future host-lint change. The fixture confirms the fix end to end: with the LEXICON the recheck exits clean, without it the trope still warns. host-lint's standalone <code>--docs</code> behavior is byte-identical across the refactor. The shared bundle was regenerated as <code>vendor-v2</code> so the offline release build sees host-lint v0.10.2; host-lint stays on <code>vendor-v1</code>. The five CI install pins (agentic-host reproducible-build and mdbook, host-template and host prose) move to v0.30.1. No spine change, since the doctrine is met rather than changed. <code>software --check</code> and the fixture are green; connollydavid/host-lifecycle#2 is closed.</p> <p>host-lint was first tagged v0.10.2 at <code>5a9d2c5</code>, but the <code>src/lib.rs</code> edit staled its two kani obligation digests (<code>DetectInternalCodeAsName</code>), so that tag's CI failed the obligations staleness lint (the kani proofs and all six binaries passed). The digests were re-derived by fingerprinting the new source (<code>git hash-object src/lib.rs</code> into <code>host-lint.obligations.digests</code>), which is artifact-preserving because the ledger is not compiled, and promoted into a clean v0.10.3 tag, the pinned release. v0.10.2 stays a superseded tag. host-lifecycle keeps embedding the host-lint library at <code>5a9d2c5</code> (engine-identical to v0.10.3), so no vendor regeneration or host-lifecycle re-release was needed.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="plan0046-the-generated-book-moves-to-mdbook-freeing-docs"><a class="header" href="#plan0046-the-generated-book-moves-to-mdbook-freeing-docs">plan/0046: the generated book moves to mdBook/, freeing docs/</a></h1> <p><code>host-lifecycle book</code> generates a browsable mdBook site from a project's rooms. It writes the generated mdBook source into a top-level <code>docs/</code> folder and clears that folder on every run. connollydavid/host-lifecycle#3 reported this as a destructive <code>book</code> bug. The real defect is narrower and deeper: <code>docs/</code> is a generic name, and a project brought under the methodology that already keeps hand-written documentation in <code>docs/</code> loses it on the first <code>book</code> run. This milestone moves the generated source and built HTML under a tool-named <code>mdBook/</code> folder, which frees <code>docs/</code> for authored content and removes the collision at its root.</p> <h2 id="problem-6"><a class="header" href="#problem-6">Problem</a></h2> <p>The generator reserves <code>docs/</code> as its output and wipes it each run (<code>fs::remove_dir_all</code>). For a greenfield project adopted from the template that is safe, because <code>docs/</code> is gitignored generated output from the start. For a migration it is a footgun: <code>classify</code> and <code>adopt</code> never inspect a pre-existing <code>docs/</code>, so a project that keeps a tracked research record or hand-written guides in <code>docs/</code> has them deleted the first time the doc site is generated. The reporter's suggested fixes (stop defaulting to <code>docs/</code>, make the source dir configurable) point at the generator, but the underlying choice is a location decision: where should generated output live so it never collides with the one folder name projects most commonly use for authored docs.</p> <h2 id="decision-settled-with-data-and-the-cast"><a class="header" href="#decision-settled-with-data-and-the-cast">Decision, settled with data and the cast</a></h2> <p>The generated mdBook source and built HTML move under one gitignored top-level folder named <code>mdBook/</code>: <code>mdBook/src/</code> for the generated source and <code>mdBook/out/</code> for the built HTML. The config <code>book.toml</code> stays at the repository root, so mdBook is still invoked exactly as before (<code>mdbook build</code> from the root, no arguments). <code>docs/</code> is freed for authored content.</p> <pre><code class="language-toml"># book.toml (repo root, gitignored) [book] src = "mdBook/src" [build] build-dir = "mdBook/out" </code></pre> <p>This holds to mdBook's own conventions. <code>src</code> and <code>build-dir</code> are first-class config keys that accept any relative path, and the generator already set a non-default <code>src = "docs"</code>, so re-pointing them is ordinary use of the tool rather than a fight with it. The folder name <code>mdBook/</code> makes it self-evident that the tree is the tool's generated workspace, so a migrating project never mistakes it for authored content.</p> <h3 id="the-data-fen-the-real-qwen35-4b"><a class="header" href="#the-data-fen-the-real-qwen35-4b">The data (Fen, the real qwen3.5-4b)</a></h3> <p>The decision was settled against the project's weak-agent persona, driven directly through the rope API with a true system prompt. A first probe fed the model mdBook's own idiom and it preferred the standard source-folder name 4 of 5 times. A fair head-to-head that also stated the ensemble's conventions favoured consolidating everything under one folder. The deciding fact is that mdBook's <code>src</code> is configurable, so the consolidated layout keeps the root-level invocation while tucking the generated trees under <code>mdBook/</code>. The locked layout then passed an acceptance test: handed the same scenario of a project with hand-written <code>docs/</code>, the real model judged <code>docs/</code> safe under the new layout 5 of 5 times, and judged the old <code>docs/</code>-as-output layout dangerous 3 of 3 times. The weak agent perceives the footgun, and perceives it gone.</p> <h3 id="the-cast"><a class="header" href="#the-cast">The cast</a></h3> <p>Consulting the five personas confirmed the layout and added four requirements for shipping it. Mara and Wren are served by a loud generated-versus-authored boundary that even a small model reads correctly. Fen requires the migration to stay a single tool-driven command rather than a hand-edit. Bly, the adopter several revisions behind, requires the change to apply as an independent ledger entry and the cleanup of the old generated trees to fail safe, so a skipped step re-lists rather than hides. Orin, the maintainer, requires the ledger entry to be version-gated on the new host-lifecycle and safe when followed literally on old infrastructure.</p> <h2 id="the-design-3"><a class="header" href="#the-design-3">The design</a></h2> <h3 id="the-generator"><a class="header" href="#the-generator">The generator</a></h3> <p><code>write_book</code> writes the SUMMARY and room pages into <code>mdBook/src/</code> instead of <code>docs/</code>. <code>book_toml</code> sets <code>src = "mdBook/src"</code> and adds <code>build-dir = "mdBook/out"</code>. The user-facing strings and the tests follow the rename. The build still runs from the root.</p> <h3 id="the-spine-and-the-gitignore"><a class="header" href="#the-spine-and-the-gitignore">The spine and the gitignore</a></h3> <p>The generated trees are gitignored as one entry. <code>host-template/.gitignore</code> and agentic-host's <code>.gitignore</code> carry <code>/book.toml</code> and <code>/mdBook/</code> in place of <code>/docs/</code> and <code>/book/</code>. The doctrine in <code>host-template/STRUCTURE.md</code> states that generated output lives under <code>mdBook/</code> and that <code>docs/</code> is available for authored content, which retires the earlier reserved-<code>docs/</code> reading. <code>call/0014</code> is already superseded by the spine, so this is a spine change propagated through <code>UPGRADING</code>, not a new project decision.</p> <h3 id="the-adopter-ledger"><a class="header" href="#the-adopter-ledger">The adopter ledger</a></h3> <p><code>UPGRADING.md</code> gains one entry that is independent (it needs no earlier unrelated migration), declares <code>requires</code> the new host-lifecycle version (an adopter on the old binary still writes <code>docs/</code>), and carries a machine-checkable verify. The migration swaps the two gitignore lines and removes the stale generated <code>docs/</code> and <code>book/</code> trees. An adopter records it with <code>host-lifecycle upgrade --record</code>, so the step is tool-carried and the stamp is never hand-edited.</p> <h2 id="build-sequence-6"><a class="header" href="#build-sequence-6">Build sequence</a></h2> <h3 id="decide-layout"><a class="header" href="#decide-layout">Decide the layout with data and the cast</a></h3> <p>Settle where generated output lives, against the real qwen3.5-4b (rope, true system prompt) and the five personas. The acceptance test confirms the weak agent sees <code>docs/</code> safe under the new layout and endangered under the old one.</p> <ul> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h3 id="book-generator"><a class="header" href="#book-generator">Move the book generator to mdBook/</a></h3> <p>Re-point <code>write_book</code> and <code>book_toml</code> to <code>mdBook/src</code> and <code>mdBook/out</code>, and follow the rename through the user-facing strings and the tests. The build runs from the root unchanged.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #decide-layout</li> <li>verify: cd software/host-lifecycle/main && cargo test</li> </ul> <h3 id="spine-and-ledger"><a class="header" href="#spine-and-ledger">Update the spine and the adopter ledger</a></h3> <p>Update <code>host-template/STRUCTURE.md</code> doctrine and <code>.gitignore</code>, and add the independent, version-gated, fail-safe <code>UPGRADING</code> entry with a machine-checkable verify.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #book-generator</li> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h3 id="repoint-agentic-host"><a class="header" href="#repoint-agentic-host">Re-point agentic-host and clean the old trees</a></h3> <p>Swap agentic-host's <code>.gitignore</code> to <code>/book.toml</code> and <code>/mdBook/</code>, point <code>mdbook.yml</code> publishing at <code>mdBook/out</code>, remove the stale generated <code>docs/</code> and <code>book/</code>, and regenerate the site locally to confirm it builds.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #book-generator</li> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h3 id="release-and-verify"><a class="header" href="#release-and-verify">Release, adopt, and verify the whole suite</a></h3> <p>Release host-lifecycle, re-pin <code>.host-software</code>, record the release receipt, bump the CI install pins, adopt the new spine revision on agentic-host through the ledger, and confirm the whole suite is green and that the Site workflow serves <code>mdBook/out</code>.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #spine-and-ledger</li> <li>depends: #repoint-agentic-host</li> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h2 id="risks-6"><a class="header" href="#risks-6">Risks</a></h2> <ul> <li>An adopter that runs the new host-lifecycle without applying the gitignore swap would see the old generated <code>docs/</code> and <code>book/</code> linger as untracked clutter. The ledger entry handles the swap and the cleanup, and is version-gated so it pairs with the binary that changes the output.</li> <li>A migrating project that already tracks content in <code>docs/</code> is now safe by construction, since the generator no longer writes there. The classify-time guard discussed under #3 is no longer needed, because the reserved name is gone.</li> </ul> <h2 id="status-7"><a class="header" href="#status-7">Status</a></h2> <p>complete, released as host-lifecycle v0.30.2 (<code>f2b4607</code>, artifact <code>3a9e59ce</code>). <code>host-lifecycle book</code> writes its generated source to <code>mdBook/src/</code> and the built HTML to <code>mdBook/out/</code>, with <code>book.toml</code> kept at the repo root, so <code>mdbook build</code> runs from the root unchanged. <code>docs/</code> is freed for authored content, which closes connollydavid/host-lifecycle#3 at its root: the generator no longer reserves <code>docs/</code>, so the migration footgun cannot recur and the classify-time guard is unnecessary. The spine carries the doctrine and the gitignore (host-template <code>e068828</code>) plus an independent, version-gated, fail-safe <code>UPGRADING</code> entry (<code>e068828</code>, requires host-lifecycle v0.30.2); agentic-host adopted it through the ledger (<code>upgrade --record e068828</code>). The decision was settled against the real qwen3.5-4b, which read <code>docs/</code> as out of harm's way under the new layout in every run and reproduced the footgun on the old layout in every run, and the five cast personas confirmed it. A regression test pins the generator to <code>mdBook/src</code> and proves an authored <code>docs/</code> is left intact. <code>software --check</code> is clean and the Site workflow serves <code>mdBook/out</code>. The whole suite is green.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="plan0047-the-prose-lane-audits-the-authored-working-tree-not-just-the-tracked-subset"><a class="header" href="#plan0047-the-prose-lane-audits-the-authored-working-tree-not-just-the-tracked-subset">plan/0047: the prose lane audits the authored working tree, not just the tracked subset</a></h1> <p><code>host-lint --docs</code> and the <code>host-lifecycle prose</code> recheck that embeds it audit authored markdown by walking <code>git ls-files</code>, which lists tracked and staged files only. A brand-new authored doc that has not been staged yet is silently skipped, so a pre-commit <code>prose</code> run reads clean over it and then the same doc fails the <code>verify</code> recheck once committed, with no content change. connollydavid/host-lint#17 reports it. This milestone widens the walk to the authored working tree, so a new doc is audited before it is staged and a clean result is never silently partial.</p> <h2 id="problem-and-investigation"><a class="header" href="#problem-and-investigation">Problem and investigation</a></h2> <p>The skip is real and partly intentional. The walk uses <code>git ls-files</code> precisely to exclude gitignored output, vendored deps, un-materialized worktrees, and submodules, which is why the prose lane never trips over generated content. Skipping an untracked authored file is a side effect of that choice. Two facts narrow the gap: staging closes it, because <code>git ls-files</code> includes staged files (a <code>git add</code> makes the new doc scanned), so the window is only the moment between creating a file and staging it; and the workaround (stage before checking) is documented. So the question is whether a pre-commit check should cover the unstaged case at all.</p> <p>The cast answers it. A <code>prose: clean</code> that silently skipped a new file is the record that <strong>overstates completeness</strong> Bly is built to catch, the <strong>silent trap</strong> Fen fumbles into (clean pre-commit, HAZARD post-commit), and the instruction that <strong>fails unsafe when followed literally</strong> that Orin guards against. By the methodology's own fail-safe principles a silent partial-clean is a defect, even where the scan scope is intentional. The operator chose to widen the walk.</p> <h2 id="the-design-4"><a class="header" href="#the-design-4">The design</a></h2> <p><code>run_docs</code>, the shared engine (host-lint library, used by both <code>host-lint --docs</code> and <code>host-lifecycle prose</code> since plan/0045), walks two sets: <code>git ls-files</code> (tracked and staged) and <code>git ls-files --others --exclude-standard</code> (untracked files git would offer to add). The two sets are disjoint, so no dedup is needed. <code>--exclude-standard</code> keeps gitignored output, vendored deps, and un-materialized worktrees out, exactly as the bare <code>git ls-files</code> walk did, so only authored markdown that is or is about to be in the repo is audited. The per-file logic is unchanged: <code>.md</code> only, <code>.host-lintignore</code> honored, symlinks skipped.</p> <p>The walk scope is a host-lint implementation detail, not spine doctrine (the spine never states the mechanism), so this milestone needs no doctrine change and no adopter <code>UPGRADING</code> entry. A fresh CI checkout carries no untracked authored files, so CI behaviour is unchanged; only a local pre-commit or working-tree run gains the new coverage.</p> <h2 id="validation"><a class="header" href="#validation">Validation</a></h2> <p>Confirmed end to end before release. The <code>#17</code> reproduction now flags an untracked authored doc while the gitignored generated tree stays excluded. On agentic-host, <code>git ls-files --others --exclude-standard</code> finds no markdown (the generated <code>mdBook/</code> and <code>book.toml</code> are gitignored), so <code>--docs</code> stays clean and the verify recheck is unchanged. A unit test pins all three cases: a tracked doc scanned, a new untracked doc scanned, a gitignored generated doc excluded.</p> <p>The real qwen3.5-4b judged the fix at the weak-agent bar (rope, true system prompt): handed a new unstaged doc with an error, it said the new working-tree walk catches it in every run, and the old tracked-only walk misses it in every run. The five cast personas confirmed the change serves them.</p> <h2 id="build-sequence-7"><a class="header" href="#build-sequence-7">Build sequence</a></h2> <h3 id="investigate-decide"><a class="header" href="#investigate-decide">Investigate and decide</a></h3> <p>Reproduce the skip, confirm it is intentional and that staging closes it, and weigh fix against the documented workaround through the cast. Operator chose to widen the walk.</p> <ul> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h3 id="scan-working-tree"><a class="header" href="#scan-working-tree">Scan the authored working tree</a></h3> <p>Widen <code>run_docs</code> to <code>git ls-files</code> plus <code>git ls-files --others --exclude-standard</code>; the per-file logic and the gitignore exclusion are unchanged. A unit test pins the three cases.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #investigate-decide</li> <li>verify: cd software/host-lint/main && cargo test</li> </ul> <h3 id="validate"><a class="header" href="#validate">Validate with Fen and the cast</a></h3> <p>Confirm the fix end to end (untracked caught, generated excluded, CI unchanged) and at the weak-agent bar with the real qwen3.5-4b, and consult the cast.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #scan-working-tree</li> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h3 id="release-cascade"><a class="header" href="#release-cascade">Release host-lint and cascade host-lifecycle</a></h3> <p>Re-derive the kani digests before tagging (the <code>src/lib.rs</code> edit stales them), release host-lint, bump host-lifecycle's host-lint dependency, regenerate the offline bundle as <code>vendor-v3</code>, release host-lifecycle, and re-pin <code>.host-software</code>.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #validate</li> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h3 id="close-and-verify"><a class="header" href="#close-and-verify">Re-pin, close, and verify the suite</a></h3> <p>Bump the CI install pins, record the receipts, close the issue, and confirm the whole suite is green.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #release-cascade</li> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h2 id="risks-7"><a class="header" href="#risks-7">Risks</a></h2> <ul> <li>The walk now reads untracked authored markdown, so a working-tree draft a developer has not chosen to commit is audited. That is the intended coverage, and a genuine scratch file is gitignored, which the <code>--exclude-standard</code> filter excludes.</li> <li>The change is in the shared <code>run_docs</code>, so it cascades through a host-lint release and a host-lifecycle release with a regenerated bundle, the same shape as plan/0045.</li> </ul> <h2 id="status-8"><a class="header" href="#status-8">Status</a></h2> <p>complete, released as host-lint v0.10.4 (<code>ce683be</code>, artifact <code>a9ef0865</code>) and host-lifecycle v0.30.3 (<code>0de2843</code>, artifact <code>cf59cc16</code>, on <code>vendor-v3</code> sha <code>817a71d9</code>). The shared <code>run_docs</code> walks <code>git ls-files</code> (tracked and staged) plus <code>git ls-files --others --exclude-standard</code> (untracked non-ignored), so a new authored doc is audited before it is staged and a pre-commit run is never silently clean over a file it skipped. <code>--exclude-standard</code> holds generated output, vendored deps, and un-materialized worktrees out, so a fresh CI checkout is unchanged. connollydavid/host-lint#17 is closed. Validated end to end and at the weak-agent bar: the real qwen3.5-4b judged the new walk catches a new unstaged doc in every run and judged the old walk misses it in every run; a unit test pins a tracked doc scanned, an untracked doc scanned, a gitignored generated doc excluded; on agentic-host the new walk finds no untracked markdown, so the verify recheck stays clean. No spine change, since the walk scope is a host-lint implementation detail rather than spine doctrine. The <code>src/lib.rs</code> edit staled the kani obligation digests, which were re-derived before tagging (the plan/0045 lesson applied first try, so the v0.10.4 tag is green). <code>software --check</code> is clean and the whole suite is green.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="plan0048-a-declared-rungs-re-deriver-must-be-runnable-and-its-digest-earned"><a class="header" href="#plan0048-a-declared-rungs-re-deriver-must-be-runnable-and-its-digest-earned">plan/0048: a declared rung's re-deriver must be runnable, and its digest earned</a></h1> <p>A host-lint <code>src/lib.rs</code> change stales the kani obligation digests, and the tag is born red on the obligations staleness lint while the proof itself is fine. This kept recurring, and the standing workaround was to hand-edit the digest ledger. This milestone traces that to its root, which is not a release-tooling decision at all, and fixes it as a methodology migration rather than a patch to one host.</p> <h2 id="how-the-scope-arrived-here"><a class="header" href="#how-the-scope-arrived-here">How the scope arrived here</a></h2> <p>The milestone opened as a release-time guard: should <code>host-lifecycle release</code> block, auto-record, or attest when it finds a stale digest. Driving the real qwen3.5-4b cold (no leading framing) showed the weak agent cannot reason about the underlying soundness, conflating "recompute the fingerprint" with "re-run the proof," so a preference poll on that guard was the wrong instrument. Consulting the cast reframed it: the fingerprint is a proxy for "is the proof current," and the born-red exists because the proxy is refreshed by hand, no longer tied to the proof it claims to track. The principle the cast settled on: a cheap proxy may stand in for an expensive check only while it stays mechanically coupled to it, refreshed by the thing that runs the real proof and by nothing else.</p> <p>Asking why the coupled path was abandoned uncovered the actual defect. The sanctioned <code>host-lifecycle obligations <spec> --rederive --record-digests</code> re-runs the proof through host-prove and records the digest only on a pass. It had been recorded in memory as failing with <code>ENOENT</code> on the <code>/mnt/c</code> WSL mount, a host-prove invocation gap blamed on the filesystem. That diagnosis was wrong. The proof runs fine on <code>/mnt/c</code>. The real causes were that <strong>host-prove was never installed on PATH</strong> by the local setup (host-lint's CI installs it; the fresh-clone discipline never did), and that it was handed <code>src/</code> rather than the crate root. Once host-prove is installed and pointed at the crate root, the full chain re-derives both rungs and exits clean, locally, on <code>/mnt/c</code>.</p> <p>So the re-deriver was referenced and materialized, its CI lane present, and yet not runnable on the box that needed it. It was available but never discharged, the very <code>call/0018</code> distinction, turned on the re-deriver itself. Nobody noticed for two weeks because the kani proof is verified by another path (CI runs <code>cargo kani</code> directly and re-derives there), so the local re-derivation's brokenness was invisible, and a <code>command not found</code> reads as a filesystem fault, so it was filed as a WSL problem and never re-opened.</p> <h2 id="the-decision-express-it-as-a-migration-not-a-host-fix"><a class="header" href="#the-decision-express-it-as-a-migration-not-a-host-fix">The decision: express it as a migration, not a host fix</a></h2> <p>A one-off <code>cargo install host-prove</code> in this host's setup would re-bury the gap for the next adopter. The fix generalizes an invariant the spine already has. The spine enforces "declare a deeper rung, oblige its lane": <code>software --check</code> HAZARDs a <code>kani:</code>/<code>apalache:</code>/<code>tlaps:</code> obligation with no matching CI workflow. But "the workflow is present in CI config" is weaker than "the re-derivation actually runs," and the gap between them is exactly where this hid. The migration is one clause longer:</p> <blockquote> <p>A declared rung obliges its lane to be runnable, not merely present. Re-derivation that cannot run is not discharged. Available is not present-in-CI is not runnable is not discharged.</p> </blockquote> <h2 id="the-validated-design-fen-hard-evidence-non-leading"><a class="header" href="#the-validated-design-fen-hard-evidence-non-leading">The validated design (Fen, hard evidence, non-leading)</a></h2> <p>Two questions were put to the real qwen3.5-4b with neutral framing, after an earlier leading round was discarded.</p> <p>A check that the re-deriver resolves on PATH falls short on its own. Asked cold whether a gate that confirms the tool is on PATH could still pass while the verification does not work, six of eight runs said yes, naming version mismatch, wrong arguments, missing dependencies, and a stand-in executable that returns success. So a PATH probe alone is recognized as too weak even at the weak-agent bar.</p> <p>Asked which check best ensures the claim is real, given the gate must stay cheap, eight of eight runs chose a recorded result showing the proof was run on the current code and passed, over both an on-PATH check and a one-off smoke run. That recorded-pass-on-current-code is the digest ledger earned by a passing re-derivation, with the staleness check guaranteeing it is current. The weak agent reconstructed <code>call/0018</code> from first principles.</p> <p>So the gate does not gain a PATH probe. It gains two coupled things:</p> <ul> <li>A <strong>runnability</strong> check that the re-deriver <strong>executes</strong> (a cheap version probe), not merely resolves, since on-PATH is not works.</li> <li>The digest stays <strong>earned</strong>: recorded only by <code>--rederive --record-digests</code> (now runnable), never hand-edited, so the existing staleness check soundly means a passing re-derivation on the current code.</li> </ul> <h2 id="the-fix"><a class="header" href="#the-fix">The fix</a></h2> <h3 id="host-lifecycle"><a class="header" href="#host-lifecycle">host-lifecycle</a></h3> <p><code>software --check</code>, for a materialized component that declares a deeper rung, probes that the rung's re-deriver executes (host-prove runs, the verifier is reachable) and HAZARDs when it cannot, beside the existing "no CI lane" HAZARD. The probe is cheap and never runs the proof; the real re-derivation stays the pluggable <code>call/0018</code> enforcement.</p> <h3 id="host-template-doctrine-and-the-ledger"><a class="header" href="#host-template-doctrine-and-the-ledger">host-template doctrine and the ledger</a></h3> <p>The "Mandatory when used" and <code>call/0018</code> text widens the "obliges its CI lane" rule into "obliges a runnable lane driver," with the available-is-not-runnable-is-not-discharged line and the earn-the-digest rule (discharge through <code>--rederive --record-digests</code>, never a hand edit). One independent, version-gated <code>UPGRADING</code> entry carries it; every adopter who upgrades gets the runnability gate, so the first time their own declared rung is not runnable, the gate says so.</p> <h3 id="agentic-host-adopts-it"><a class="header" href="#agentic-host-adopts-it">agentic-host adopts it</a></h3> <p>Like any adopter: record the ledger entry, install host-prove in the fresh-clone setup and in the CI that runs <code>software --check</code>, and the gate goes green by being runnable. The CLAUDE.md setup line is the project-local instance of the spine rule, not the fix.</p> <h3 id="the-record-is-corrected"><a class="header" href="#the-record-is-corrected">the record is corrected</a></h3> <p>The WSL-ENOENT entries in MEMORY were a misdiagnosis and are struck with a correcting entry: the re-derivation runs on <code>/mnt/c</code>; the causes were host-prove not on PATH and the wrong directory. This stays local, since it is this host's own record, not methodology.</p> <h2 id="build-sequence-8"><a class="header" href="#build-sequence-8">Build sequence</a></h2> <h3 id="diagnose-audit"><a class="header" href="#diagnose-audit">Diagnose, audit, and decide</a></h3> <p>Reproduce the false ENOENT, prove the local re-derivation runs once host-prove is on PATH and pointed at the crate root, audit the missed install step and the misdiagnosis, and settle the migration shape with the cast.</p> <ul> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h3 id="validate-strictness"><a class="header" href="#validate-strictness">Validate the gate strictness with Fen</a></h3> <p>Put the gate check to the real qwen3.5-4b with neutral framing: a PATH probe is insufficient (six of eight), a recorded pass on the current code is best (eight of eight). The gate checks runnability that executes plus an earned, current digest.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #diagnose-audit</li> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h3 id="runnability-gate"><a class="header" href="#runnability-gate">The runnability gate in host-lifecycle</a></h3> <p><code>software --check</code> probes that a declared rung's re-deriver executes and HAZARDs when it cannot. Unit tests cover a declared rung with a runnable driver, with a missing driver, and with no declared rung.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #validate-strictness</li> <li>verify: cd software/host-lifecycle/main && cargo test</li> </ul> <h3 id="spine-and-release"><a class="header" href="#spine-and-release">Spine doctrine, ledger, and release</a></h3> <p>Generalize the host-template doctrine and add the independent, version-gated <code>UPGRADING</code> entry. Release host-lifecycle, re-pin <code>.host-software</code>, advance the host-template pointer so the entry is PENDING, and correct the memory. The migration is now built and published; adopting it on this host is its own step.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #runnability-gate</li> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h3 id="adopt"><a class="header" href="#adopt">agentic-host adopts the entry</a></h3> <p>On the operator's trigger, "Read and follow https://github.com/connollydavid/host to keep this repository an agentic project," adopt like any project: record the ledger entry, install host-prove in the fresh-clone setup and the CI that runs <code>software --check</code>, bump the CI host-lifecycle pins, and confirm the whole suite is green. The gate goes green by being runnable.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #spine-and-release</li> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h2 id="risks-8"><a class="header" href="#risks-8">Risks</a></h2> <ul> <li>The runnability probe runs in every <code>software --check</code>, including CI, so the CI that runs the gate must install the re-deriver. That install is part of the adoption, and its absence is precisely the HAZARD the migration adds.</li> <li>A probe that the re-deriver executes is stronger than on-PATH but still not a proof that the rung passes; the earned digest and the pluggable re-derivation carry that, as <code>call/0018</code> intends.</li> </ul> <h2 id="status-9"><a class="header" href="#status-9">Status</a></h2> <p>complete. The runnability gate ships in host-lifecycle v0.31.0 (<code>software --check</code> HAZARDs a declared rung whose re-deriver does not execute). The spine doctrine and the version-gated <code>UPGRADING</code> entry <code>6174996</code> ("a re-deriver that runs") are pushed; <code>.host-software</code> is re-pinned to v0.31.0. The memory misdiagnosis is corrected. On the operator's trigger, "Read and follow https://github.com/connollydavid/host to keep this repository an agentic project," agentic-host adopted the entry the case-(c) way: <code>upgrade --record 6174996</code> passed its verify and recorded the applied claim, host-prove is installed in the fresh-clone setup and in the CI that runs <code>software --check</code>, and both CI host-lifecycle pins are at v0.31.0. <code>software --check</code> is green (the gate reports the re-deriver runnable for both declaring components), and the whole suite is green across agentic-host, host-lifecycle, and host-template.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="plan0049-external-reference-corpus"><a class="header" href="#plan0049-external-reference-corpus">plan/0049: external reference corpus</a></h1> <p>This milestone adds a new component, <code>host-reference</code>, that reads external documentation an agentic project depends on but does not author, and normalises it into a token-lean form an agent can interpret in context. The material arrives in many shapes, such as Markdown, HTML, PDF, Office documents, images, structured data, electronic design files, and mechanical CAD. <code>host-reference</code> reads each shape and produces a normalised representation chosen for low token cost and faithful meaning.</p> <p>The milestone is in design. This README records the direction and the open questions; the settled decisions land in <code>call/0030</code> and its siblings once the weak-agent probes and the cast review return. The probe set and its adversarial review live in <code>gather-data.md</code>.</p> <h2 id="why-9"><a class="header" href="#why-9">Why</a></h2> <p>An agentic project already applies rigour to what it produces. The build is reproducible, the authored prose is linted, the specifications are checked. The material it consumes has none of that treatment. External reference knowledge enters an agent's context raw and unpinned. It is heavy in tokens, and nothing records where it came from. The <code>reference</code> memory kind records only a pointer to the content. There is no inbound counterpart to the outbound discipline, and <code>host-reference</code> is that counterpart.</p> <p>The primary goal is in-context interpretation: let an agent understand external material inside its context window at low token cost, and act on it and cite it with a provenance trail. Token reduction is the means; correct interpretation is the end. The impact is measured rather than asserted, since each ingest records the raw token count against the normalised token count, so the saving is a number a reader can check.</p> <h2 id="direction"><a class="header" href="#direction">Direction</a></h2> <p><code>host-reference</code> is a reference compiler with a two-layer model.</p> <ul> <li>The immutable normalised layer holds the normalised corpus, a semantic skeleton, and a content-addressed source map. It is re-derived from the source bytes in a pinned toolchain, so it is reproducible and attestable the same way a binary is.</li> <li>The mutable overlay layer holds annotations, edits, and agent notes. It is collaborative and anchored into the immutable layer by standard selectors, so it survives a re-derivation.</li> </ul> <p>Four properties carry the design:</p> <ul> <li>Semantic normalisation to tokens. Each content kind maps to its token-optimal target, and the semantic structure that aids interpretation (headings, reading order, tables, entities, cross references) is preserved rather than discarded.</li> <li>Deterministic re-derivation and attestation. The immutable layer is a reproducible artifact that <code>software --check</code> and host-prove verify.</li> <li>Bidirectional provenance and editing. The source map resolves both directions, round-trip fidelity is a declared per-kind property, an editable view writes back where a well-behaved lens exists, and a hard-to-edit kind such as PDF carries edits in a standard sidecar rather than a mutated original.</li> <li>Tiered, range-addressable views. A cheap skeleton is always resident; a full slice is fetched on demand by page range, section, offset, or token budget, so a large document costs only the slice a task needs.</li> </ul> <h2 id="format-coverage"><a class="header" href="#format-coverage">Format coverage</a></h2> <p>The specification is closed over the superset of content kinds; the build lands one kind at a time, each with a conformance fixture that re-derives byte for byte. The modality families are prose, structured data, office documents, fixed-layout documents, raster images, vector graphics, electronic mail, electronic design, engineering geometry, and audio-visual media. Each family holds several kinds. Prose covers Markdown and HTML and the documentation markups, the TeX family and reStructuredText and AsciiDoc and Org-mode among them, along with RTF and EPUB and the manual page. Structured data covers JSON and CSV and YAML and XML, the config and tabular kinds (TOML, the INI family, JSON Lines, the calendar and contact kinds, the Jupyter notebook), and the columnar Parquet and Arrow. Electronic design covers the SPICE netlist alongside KiCad and Gerber and Eagle. Engineering geometry covers mechanical CAD and the 3D-printing kinds (mesh, the container kinds, the toolpath kind, and the parametric source kind).</p> <p>Many kinds share a reader, so the breadth collapses to a small set of mechanisms: a zip reader for the Office and EPUB and 3D-printing containers, a compound-file reader for legacy Office and Outlook mail, an XML reader for vector graphics and several design kinds, fixed-layout extraction, optical character recognition, columnar decoding, media-container metadata, and the engineering parsers.</p> <p>Audio-visual media follows the recognition split of <code>call/0030</code>. The attested layer holds what re-derives deterministically, the container metadata such as duration and codec and stream layout. The transcript and the description are machine-learning output, so they live in the overlay through the provider-agnostic adapter, the same way the text inside an image does. The columnar kinds re-derive deterministically and stay in the attested layer, since a schema and its statistics are a parse rather than an inference.</p> <p>The component's remit is external reference material, not code. Source code and the interface and schema definition kinds, an API description or a protocol schema, are software; they belong to the <em>Where</em> room and the <code>host-*</code> tooling rather than to a reference reader, and they are out of scope here. Archives are out of scope as well, since a container of arbitrary files is a packaging concern rather than a content kind. The pluggable model of <code>call/0033</code> carries the superset as the menu, and an adopting project enables the subset its corpus needs.</p> <h2 id="decisions"><a class="header" href="#decisions">Decisions</a></h2> <p>The design is settled across four records.</p> <ul> <li><code>call/0030</code> fixes the component shape and these mechanism choices: the overlay is a Loro document, recognition is hybrid (a deterministic engine for the attested layer and a provider-agnostic vision adapter for the overlay), the semantic layer is a sidecar in JSON-LD bound to the <code>plan/0039</code> vocabulary, the round-trip law is a per-kind lens law in the property-based lane, and an undeclared capability defaults to the most restrictive setting.</li> <li><code>call/0031</code> fixes the untrusted-input threat model: ingested material is untrusted, met by defensive parsing with hard bounds, a legible untrusted boundary, and a fail-safe recorded refusal.</li> <li><code>call/0032</code> fixes the engineering-geometry token target, taken to the cast since the summary target has no settled industry answer: a deterministic structure-and-metadata summary keyed on the model tree, with a parsed-against-computed reproducibility split and an extensible per-format default.</li> <li><code>call/0033</code> fixes the reader architecture: each format is a pluggable Cargo feature, every reader is pure-Rust with no C dependency, the best reader per format is chosen on merit, the dependency licences are per plugin with AGPL banned and GPL flagged, and the in-scope set is the adopting project's choice.</li> </ul> <p>The canonicalisation rules, the reference tokenizer the token accounting reports against, and the query surface a running project calls are build-time details settled inside the milestone.</p> <h2 id="readers-and-dependencies"><a class="header" href="#readers-and-dependencies">Readers and dependencies</a></h2> <p>Each kind binds the best maintained pure-Rust reader, pinned with its version and licence in <a href="plan/0049-external-reference-corpus/readers.html"><code>readers.md</code></a>, which also carries the licence watch-list and the pure-Rust feature discipline. Almost every pick is permissive. The GPL <code>openscad-rs</code> runs out-of-process under <code>call/0033</code>, which doubles as the first real test of the plugin API, since the OpenSCAD plugin drives that helper through the same <code>Normalizer</code> interface as every in-process reader. The manual page and TeX and IGES have no pure-Rust library, so they are deferred rather than hand-rolled or bound to a C library. A <code>cargo-deny</code> lane denies AGPL and flags GPL.</p> <h2 id="validation-1"><a class="header" href="#validation-1">Validation</a></h2> <p>The agent-facing surfaces are validated at the weak-agent bar before the design locks, the way plan/0042 and plan/0039 were. The probe set, its adversarial review, and the recorded run live in <code>gather-data.md</code>. A design judgement with no settled answer, such as the engineering-geometry summary fields, goes to the cast rather than a pass rate.</p> <h2 id="build-sequence-9"><a class="header" href="#build-sequence-9">Build sequence</a></h2> <p>The build runs as an anchored task graph (plan/0042): each task carries a receipt in <code>.host-task-receipts</code>, and a downstream task stays pending until its prerequisites carry receipts. The order lives in the <code>depends</code> edges rather than the names. The scaffold and the embed are done.</p> <h3 id="scaffold-workspace"><a class="header" href="#scaffold-workspace">Scaffold the workspace</a></h3> <p>Author the Cargo workspace on the pinned toolchain. The <code>host-reference-core</code> library carries the <code>Normalizer</code> trait and the two-layer types, and a thin CLI sits over it.</p> <ul> <li>verify: cd software/host-reference/main && cargo test</li> </ul> <h3 id="embed-component"><a class="header" href="#embed-component">Embed the component</a></h3> <p>Create the public repo and push the scaffold. Pin it source-only in <code>.host-software</code> and materialize the bare store and worktree. Record the embed and release receipts.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #scaffold-workspace</li> <li>verify: attested operator</li> </ul> <h3 id="text-cheap-kinds"><a class="header" href="#text-cheap-kinds">The text-cheap kinds</a></h3> <p>Land the text-cheap normalisers that <code>call/0030</code> groups under the text and data and XML mechanisms, each with a conformance fixture that re-derives byte for byte.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #embed-component</li> <li>verify: cd software/host-reference/main && cargo test</li> </ul> <h3 id="pluggable-foundation"><a class="header" href="#pluggable-foundation">The pluggable foundation</a></h3> <p>Retrofit the text-cheap readers behind Cargo features in the CLI, the text-cheap set on by default and the heavier readers reserved for their own opt-in features (call/0033). Add the <code>cargo-deny</code> lane that denies AGPL and flags GPL. A build with no features compiles to an empty registry.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #text-cheap-kinds</li> <li>verify: cd software/host-reference/main && cargo test && cargo deny check licenses advisories</li> </ul> <h3 id="expand-prose-and-structured-readers"><a class="header" href="#expand-prose-and-structured-readers">The expanded prose and structured readers</a></h3> <p>Land the documentation markups and the extra config and tabular kinds behind their features, each with a conformance fixture. The prose readers are reStructuredText and AsciiDoc and Org-mode and RTF and EPUB and BibTeX; the structured readers are TOML and the INI family and JSON Lines and the calendar and contact kinds and the Jupyter notebook and the columnar Parquet and Arrow. The pins live in <code>readers.md</code>.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #pluggable-foundation</li> <li>verify: cd software/host-reference/main && cargo test</li> </ul> <h3 id="office-mail-fixed-layout"><a class="header" href="#office-mail-fixed-layout">Office and mail and fixed-layout</a></h3> <p>Land the office and mail normalisers over the shared container readers, and add the born-digital PDF. Each carries its fixture.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #pluggable-foundation</li> <li>verify: cd software/host-reference/main && cargo test</li> </ul> <h3 id="recognition-and-engineering"><a class="header" href="#recognition-and-engineering">Recognition and engineering</a></h3> <p>Land the recognition path for scanned PDF and image and audio-visual media. Add the EDA layout normalisers and the engineering geometry of <code>call/0032</code>. Each carries its fixture.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #office-mail-fixed-layout</li> <li>verify: cd software/host-reference/main && cargo test</li> </ul> <h3 id="overlay"><a class="header" href="#overlay">The overlay</a></h3> <p>Land the Loro overlay and the write-back path over the W3C Web Annotation selectors, with the round-trip law proptested per kind.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #recognition-and-engineering</li> <li>verify: cd software/host-reference/main && cargo test</li> </ul> <h3 id="spec-and-release"><a class="header" href="#spec-and-release">The spec and the release</a></h3> <p>Add the <code>.allium</code> spec with its lanes and obligations, and wire the CI. Establish the reproducible build with its deps-bundle, and release through <code>host-lifecycle</code>.</p> <ul> <li>depends: #overlay</li> <li>verify: host-lifecycle software --verify-build .</li> </ul> <h2 id="status-10"><a class="header" href="#status-10">Status</a></h2> <p>The design is settled and the build is under way. The scaffold and the embed are done, and so is the text-cheap-kinds task: its normalisers and conformance fixtures have landed behind the <code>host-reference-core</code> contract and the CLI (prose, structured data, markup, vector, and the SPICE netlist). The component is pinned source-only in <code>.host-software</code>, and <code>software --check</code> is green. The build sequence above is an anchored receipted task graph (plan/0042). The pluggable foundation and the expanded prose and structured readers have landed, each reader a feature-gated crate, and the per-kind pins live in <code>readers.md</code>; the library-less kinds stay deferred. Office and mail and fixed-layout have landed (DOCX, PPTX, XLSX over undoc; EML over mail-parser; born-digital PDF over lopdf and pdf-extract), and so has recognition-and-engineering: the attested deterministic readers of the recognition split, image and audio-visual container metadata alongside the EDA and engineering-geometry parsers. OCR is delivered too, as the first out-of-process plugin (<code>call/0034</code>): a helper binary carries the ocrs engine and its CC-BY-SA-4.0 models, the permissive plugin runs it at arm's length, and the recognised text re-derives deterministically into the attested layer. Audio-visual transcription stays in the overlay node by the <code>call/0030</code> split. The ready frontier is now the overlay.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="software-where"><a class="header" href="#software-where">Software: where</a></h1> <p>The action this project produces. Each component is a bare object store with worktrees, not committed into this repo; the recipe below is the reproducibility anchor. Materialize the worktrees locally with:</p> <pre><code>host-lifecycle software --materialize . </code></pre> <h2 id="host-lint"><a class="header" href="#host-lint">host-lint</a></h2> <ul> <li>url: https://github.com/connollydavid/host-lint.git</li> <li>pin: <code>ce683be96ba8d519e4e1b0c50ad5ee18459aaaa8</code></li> <li>worktrees: none (single canonical line)</li> </ul> <h2 id="host-lifecycle-1"><a class="header" href="#host-lifecycle-1">host-lifecycle</a></h2> <ul> <li>url: https://github.com/connollydavid/host-lifecycle.git</li> <li>pin: <code>3958b6248eaf3e7ad6b017fc597cd7e30f17a59b</code></li> <li>worktrees: none (single canonical line)</li> </ul> <h2 id="host-prove"><a class="header" href="#host-prove">host-prove</a></h2> <ul> <li>url: https://github.com/connollydavid/host-prove.git</li> <li>pin: <code>3d1bba79e817f43cdc64ed0e7438e7fdeeae7378</code></li> <li>worktrees: none (single canonical line)</li> </ul> <h2 id="host-grammar"><a class="header" href="#host-grammar">host-grammar</a></h2> <ul> <li>url: https://github.com/connollydavid/host-grammar</li> <li>pin: <code>9d51468ab1f0a7e253cd2ee7d149d7325e65f47b</code></li> <li>worktrees: none (single canonical line)</li> </ul> <h2 id="host-reference"><a class="header" href="#host-reference">host-reference</a></h2> <ul> <li>url: https://github.com/connollydavid/host-reference.git</li> <li>pin: <code>5ef7698b284fc19b93f73819ea7884a91cac0343</code></li> <li>worktrees: none (single canonical line)</li> </ul> <h2 id="host"><a class="header" href="#host">host</a></h2> <ul> <li>url: https://github.com/connollydavid/host.git</li> <li>pin: <code>699d423033bb3f04234f7e6c7edb562a9f8f75f6</code></li> <li>worktrees: none (single canonical line)</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="reserve-agentic-host-for-this-repository-scrub-the-collision-by-a-one-time-history-rewrite"><a class="header" href="#reserve-agentic-host-for-this-repository-scrub-the-collision-by-a-one-time-history-rewrite">Reserve <code>agentic-host</code> for this repository; scrub the collision by a one-time history rewrite</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Scope: instance</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-16</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p><code>agentic-host</code> was used two ways: as the name of <em>this repository</em> and as the generic <em>kind</em> a consumer builds. The collision leaked this repository's identity into the methodology a consumer adopts, and the <code>.agentic-host</code> stamp and the <code>template-agentic-host</code> name carried the same ambiguity into shipped software. An ambiguous name in the artifacts a consumer receives is a consumer-facing defect, not a cosmetic one. This repository is meta (adopter-zero); consumers do not see this repository's <code>call/</code> decisions, so the fix had to land in the software itself.</p> <h2 id="decision-outcome"><a class="header" href="#decision-outcome">Decision Outcome</a></h2> <ul> <li><strong><code>agentic-host</code> names <em>this repository</em> exclusively</strong>, the project that develops <code>host</code> and the <code>host-*</code> tooling.</li> <li><strong>A repo that adopts the methodology is "an agentic project"</strong> (e.g. <code>agentic-acme</code>). Two clean namespaces: <code>agentic-*</code> for <strong>projects</strong> (this repo, plus adopters), <code>host-*</code> for the methodology's <strong>reusable artifacts</strong>.</li> <li><strong>Structural renames:</strong> <code>template-agentic-host</code> to <strong><code>host-template</code></strong> (the scaffold is a methodology artifact, not a project, so it leaves the <code>agentic-*</code> namespace), and the <code>.agentic-host</code> stamp to <strong><code>.host</code></strong> (joining the <code>.host-*</code> config family).</li> <li><strong>Applied as a one-time, archive-first, map-only history rewrite</strong> across all four repos (<code>host</code>, <code>host-lifecycle</code>, <code>host-template</code>, <code>agentic-host</code>); the sanctioned exception to history-immutability. The literal map: <code>template-agentic-host ==> host-template</code>, <code>.agentic-host ==> .host</code>. Nothing outside the map changed byte-for-byte in history.</li> <li><strong>Prose was forward-only</strong> ("an agentic host" to "an agentic project", with a bracketed example on first mention; this repository named <code>agentic-host</code>; the word "forge" banned). Prose is context-dependent (the same phrase meant this repository in some files and the kind in others), so it could not be a history substitution. Legitimate "agentic" usages were preserved: <em>agentic tells</em> (host-lint's domain), <em>agentic LLM</em> (Wren), <em>agentic development</em>, and <code>Agentic-MCP-Win32s</code> (a correctly-named adopter).</li> <li><strong>Archive:</strong> an <code>archive/pre-host-rename</code> tag on every repo preserves the pre-rewrite tip verbatim. <code>host</code>'s <code>v0.1.0</code> and <code>host-lifecycle</code>'s <code>v0.4.1</code> were retagged onto the scrubbed commits.</li> </ul> <h2 id="consequences"><a class="header" href="#consequences">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: the methodology a consumer receives no longer carries this repository's name; <code>agentic-*</code> (projects) vs <code>host-*</code> (artifacts) is a clean dichotomy.</li> <li>Cost: every pre-rewrite SHA is invalid; any existing clone must re-fetch. The archive tags make the old state recoverable.</li> <li>This is a forge-only record. The consumer-facing half (the <code>agentic-*</code> / <code>host-*</code> vocabulary) lives in <code>host-template</code>'s own docs, where consumers read it.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="parallelizing-the-prose-scan-escalates-run-accumulation-to-the-timing-lane"><a class="header" href="#parallelizing-the-prose-scan-escalates-run-accumulation-to-the-timing-lane">Parallelizing the prose scan escalates run-accumulation to the timing lane</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-18</li> <li>Scope: host-grammar</li> <li>Relates: <code>call/0002</code> (verification lanes by property type, invokes its escalation bridge); <code>plan/0007</code> (the prose-tell engine being parallelized); <code>plan/0008</code> (the milestone that implements this).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-1"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-1">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p><code>plan/0007</code> shipped the prose-tell engine (<code>host-grammar::scan_prose</code>) as a single sequential pass. For large documents we want to parallelize it. The lexical and per-sentence tells are embarrassingly parallel, but three equations accumulate state <strong>across</strong> sentences (anaphora, countdown, listicle) so a run can straddle a chunk boundary. A chunked-parallel scan must therefore reconcile boundary runs and assemble output in document order while K workers complete in <strong>arbitrary order</strong>.</p> <p><code>call/0002</code> reserves the TLA+/Specula lane strictly for timing and concurrency ("for all interleavings") and routes functional invariants to allium. The question: does the parallel scan stay in allium's lane, or does it cross the escalation bridge into Specula's?</p> <h2 id="decision-1"><a class="header" href="#decision-1">Decision</a></h2> <p><strong>The parallel scan splits across both lanes; the worker-completion ordering crosses the bridge into the Specula/TLA+ lane.</strong></p> <ul> <li> <p><strong>allium lane (functional).</strong> The merge of per-chunk run summaries is an associative monoid; <code>parallel(split) == sequential</code> for all sentence sequences and all split points. This is a "for all inputs" property, verified by property-based testing in host-grammar.</p> </li> <li> <p><strong>Specula lane (temporal/interleaving).</strong> K workers complete in any order and their partial results are assembled into one ordered output. The invariant (<em>for every completion interleaving, the assembled output is identical and in document order</em>), plus liveness (every worker joins; no chunk is dropped), is a "for all interleavings" property that property-based testing cannot exhaustively explore. This is exactly the bridge condition in <code>call/0002</code>, so it escalates.
