A TLA+ spec model-checks it with TLC.</p> </li> </ul> <p>This is a software decision about <code>host-grammar</code>, not a methodology change (anti-ouroboros): it applies <code>call/0002</code>'s existing rule, it does not amend it.</p> <h2 id="consequences-1"><a class="header" href="#consequences-1">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: the bridge is exercised for the first time with a concrete, honest trigger; TLA+ is used on its home ground, not as a general requirements tool.</li> <li>Good: the two lanes stay complementary; PBT proves the merge math, TLC proves the interleaving safety/liveness; neither subsumes the other.</li> <li>Cost: a TLA+ spec and a TLC run join the milestone's obligations, and (per the milestone decision) TLC is wired into CI, which adds a Java/tla2tools dependency to the pipeline.</li> <li>Constraint: the implementation must keep workers free of shared mutable state (each returns an owned summary; the merge is sequential), so the model the spec checks matches the code.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="discharge-is-re-derivation-in-a-pinned-toolchain-not-a-signed-attestation-enforcement-is-project-pluggable"><a class="header" href="#discharge-is-re-derivation-in-a-pinned-toolchain-not-a-signed-attestation-enforcement-is-project-pluggable">Discharge is re-derivation in a pinned toolchain, not a signed attestation; enforcement is project-pluggable</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-20</li> <li>Scope: host-prove, host-lifecycle (the verification toolchain)</li> <li>Supersedes: <code>call/0016</code> (the CI-signed ed25519 attestation token).</li> <li>Relates: <code>call/0002</code> (verification lanes by property type); <code>plan/0023</code> (the verification ladder + host-prove's version+SHA256-pinned proof toolchains); <code>plan/0024</code> (the milestone, now shrunk to this); connollydavid/host #8 (discharge is name-presence), #9 (bound dropped). Generalizes the reproducible-build mechanism already shipping (<code>.host-software</code>'s <code>artifact = … <sha256></code> + <code>toolchain = …@sha256</code>, re-derived by <code>software --verify-build</code> <strong>in the recorded container</strong>, host#14) so it also covers proofs, not only builds.</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-2"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-2">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p><code>call/0016</code> fixed #8 (discharge was <code>src.contains(name)</code>, name-presence, not a real PASS) with a CI-signed ed25519 attestation token, verified offline. Two fatal problems surfaced:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Key management is a deal-breaker for adopters.</strong> host-lifecycle is one shared binary, so a baked-in public key can't serve every adopter, and a shared keypair would let any adopter forge any other's tokens. Each adopter would need its own keypair, an operator-managed CI secret, and an integrity anchor (branch protection) for the public key: a fragile, human-rooted burden the methodology cannot make mechanical.</li> <li><strong>It complicates parallel checkouts.</strong> Per-project secrets and committed <code>.att</code> files in every worktree do not compose with the bare-store/parallel-worktree model.</li> </ul> <p>And the fallback "CI-green is the discharge" <strong>overfits GitHub</strong> (required status checks). The methodology must not depend on GitHub, or any specific CI.</p> <p>The principle <code>AVAILABLE ≠ DISCHARGED</code> (a rung discharges only on a real PASS) is right. Only the mechanism for carrying "a real PASS happened" into a later check needs replacing.</p> <h2 id="decision-2"><a class="header" href="#decision-2">Decision</a></h2> <p><strong>An obligation discharges only by RE-DERIVING in its recorded pinned toolchain (checkable by anyone, anywhere), not by a signed token and not by any specific CI being green.</strong></p> <ul> <li><strong>Builds</strong> re-derive in the recorded <code>toolchain</code> container and must reproduce the recorded <code>artifact</code> sha256 (host#14, <code>software --verify-build</code>). A stub source yields a different hash.</li> <li><strong>Proofs</strong> re-run in host-prove's <strong>version+SHA256-pinned proof toolchain</strong> (apalache / kani / tlaps, plan/0023) via <code>obligations --prove</code>, and must PASS at the declared bound. A stub / failing / <code>THEOREM X == TRUE</code> proof fails the re-run.</li> <li><strong>Re-derivation runs anywhere</strong>: a developer's machine, any CI, a pre-push hook, a cron. The authority is the <em>pinned re-derivation</em>, never the platform that runs it.</li> <li><strong>The offline hook does only cheap structural checks</strong>: a name-presence <strong>lint</strong> (the named target exists, honest about being a lint, not the discharge) + <strong>input-digest staleness</strong> (<code>git hash-object</code> of the consumed inputs; a change since the recorded discharge ⇒ HAZARD).</li> <li><strong>Enforcement is project-pluggable.</strong> That re-derivation <em>ran and passed</em> before "done" / release is enforced by whatever a project has: a required status check (GitHub), a pipeline (any CI), a pre-push hook, or operator discipline via the <code>verify</code> lifecycle phase. The methodology prescribes the <strong>act</strong> (run <code>verify</code> before declaring done / releasing) and ships the <strong>re-deriver</strong> (<code>host-lifecycle</code>); the enforcement <strong>mechanism</strong> is the project's choice.</li> </ul> <h2 id="what-hash-artifacts-a-local-build-gives-us-and-their-limits"><a class="header" href="#what-hash-artifacts-a-local-build-gives-us-and-their-limits">What hash artifacts a local build gives us (and their limits)</a></h2> <ul> <li><strong>Artifact sha256</strong> (<code>.host-software</code>) discharges <em>build reproducibility</em>, unforgeable in the useful sense (any written hash is checked by rebuilding in the pinned toolchain).</li> <li><strong>Input digests</strong> (<code>git hash-object</code>) give offline <em>staleness</em>, a cheap honest-drift catcher (a determined agent can re-record the hash, but still faces the re-run). Not a forgery gate.</li> <li><strong>A proof's PASS is a boolean from running the verifier, not a blob to hash</strong>, so proofs re-run (cheaply, in the pinned proof toolchain); they are not hashed. (Proof certificates exist but are out of scope, over-engineering.)</li> </ul> <h2 id="consequences-2"><a class="header" href="#consequences-2">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li><strong>No keys, no secrets, no <code>.att</code>, no signer, no per-project key management, no parallel- checkout friction, no CI overfit.</strong> plan/0024 collapses to: reframe #8 (discharge = re-derivation), <code>obligations --prove</code> re-runs in the pinned toolchain checking PASS + bound (#9), input-digest staleness, and the small #10/#11/#12/#13 fixes. host-prove keeps its pinned toolchains + verdict/bound parser (an agent-UX aid); <strong>no crypto on any path</strong>.</li> <li><strong><code>AVAILABLE ≠ DISCHARGED</code> / <code>BUILT ≠ RELEASED</code> survive</strong>, discharge is still a real PASS, now proven by re-derivation rather than a signature.</li> <li><strong>Honest irreducible limit:</strong> a project with <em>no</em> enforcement (no CI, no hook, no discipline) can still self-assert locally; the methodology can ship the re-deriver but cannot force a project to run it (the same limit as "you can skip your own tests"). The <code>verify</code> phase + a receipt (<code>plan/0025</code>) make <em>not</em> running it visible, not impossible.</li> <li><strong>Property-triviality stays out of scope</strong> (a vacuous proof re-derives as PASS), a review concern, not mechanizable, exactly as <code>call/0016</code> already noted.</li> </ul> <h2 id="alternatives-considered"><a class="header" href="#alternatives-considered">Alternatives considered</a></h2> <ul> <li><strong>CI-signed ed25519 token</strong> (<code>call/0016</code>, superseded): per-adopter key management is a deal-breaker; complicates parallel checkouts.</li> <li><strong>CI-green as the authority</strong>: overfits GitHub / a specific CI; the methodology must stay platform-agnostic.</li> <li><strong>A committed plaintext result marker</strong>: forgeable by the agent (it edits the file); relocates #8, does not fix it.</li> <li><strong>Re-run the heavy verifier in the commit hook</strong>: breaks the fast/offline hook; re-derivation belongs in the heavy <code>verify</code> lane, not the hook.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="literal-tell-citations-live-in-a-host-lintignore-fenced-block-never---no-verify"><a class="header" href="#literal-tell-citations-live-in-a-host-lintignore-fenced-block-never---no-verify">Literal tell-citations live in a <code>host-lint:ignore</code> fenced block, never <code>--no-verify</code></a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-20</li> <li>Scope: host-lint (the <code>host-lint:ignore</code> fenced-block skip) and agentic-host's authored docs (the literal-citation disposition).</li> <li>Relates to: <code>call/0006</code> (the sanctioned-vocabulary allowlist), <code>call/0009</code> (exclude the generated record from the audit), <code>CLAUDE.md</code> §0 (host-lint is the hygiene lane).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-3"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-3">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>The host <em>develops</em> host-lint, so its own authored content legitimately cites tell-shaped tokens: decision records explain detection by naming the token classes they handle, and <code>PLAN.md</code> carries a mandated retired-ordinal dictionary (old name to content name, kept for reading history). <code>call/</code> is intentionally linted, and that caught a <em>real</em> ordinal tell that had hidden in a decision record. But the recurring legitimate citations were being committed with <code>git commit --no-verify</code>, a blanket bypass that defeats the lane: it is exactly how the real tell slipped in unnoticed. Excluding whole files via <code>.host-lintignore</code> is no safer; it mutes any <em>future</em> real tell in those files. We need an exclusion that is line-precise, explicit, visible in the source, and that cannot silently hide a real tell.</p> <h2 id="decision-outcome-1"><a class="header" href="#decision-outcome-1">Decision Outcome</a></h2> <p>A tell-shaped token in linted host content is legitimate only if it is a real tracker reference (the <code>closes</code>/<code>fixes</code> traceability allowlist) or a declared LEXICON entry; anything else is a violation and is reworded out. For the residual case, literal <em>reference</em> content that must reproduce old tokens for history (the retired-ordinal dictionary, an archived citation), that content goes in a fenced code block whose info string is <strong><code>host-lint:ignore</code></strong>:</p> <pre><code>```host-lint:ignore <literal reference content, reproduced verbatim> ``` </code></pre> <p>host-lint's naming scan skips such a block; its prose scan already skips every fenced block. Why this form:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Idiomatic markdown.</strong> It is the mermaid / PlantUML info-string pattern, a plain fenced block to a renderer, and a meaningful "this is excluded literal reference data" to a reader. No bolted-on HTML-comment pragma, no out-of-band config.</li> <li><strong>Explicit, never blanket.</strong> Only a block <em>tagged</em> <code>host-lint:ignore</code> is skipped; a regular code block and all prose stay linted. The exemption is always a deliberate, reviewable act, the "excluded, not bypassed, validated" rule, now self-documenting at the point of use.</li> <li><strong>Inline stays linted.</strong> Only fenced <em>blocks</em> are skipped, never inline backticks, so a tell cannot be laundered by inline-quoting it; it must be reworded.</li> <li><strong>Region-precise, drift-proof.</strong> It bounds exactly the fenced lines, with no line numbers to rot.</li> </ul> <p><code>git commit --no-verify</code> is retired for record-layer commits: the legitimate cases are now handled (a tracker reference, a LEXICON entry, or a <code>host-lint:ignore</code> block), and everything else is a real tell to fix. The lane is never bypassed.</p> <h2 id="consequences-3"><a class="header" href="#consequences-3">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: the host's own docs self-audit clean with no bypass; the gate is honest.</li> <li>Good: a future real tell in prose still flags; a tell moved into a <code>host-lint:ignore</code> block is a visible, reviewable change in the diff, not a silent mute.</li> <li>Cost: a small host-lint feature (the naming scan learns the info string) and a one-time cleanup (wrap the dictionary, reword the violations, retire the <code>--no-verify</code> rule).</li> <li>Builds on <code>call/0006</code> (an allowlist for legitimate vocabulary) and <code>call/0009</code> (exclude the generated record): this is the third escape, literal reference <em>citations</em>, and the strictest, because it is per-block and visible.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="the-host--family-is-software-developed-here-embedded-as-where-room-components"><a class="header" href="#the-host--family-is-software-developed-here-embedded-as-where-room-components">The host-* family is software developed here, embedded as Where-room components</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-21</li> <li>Scope: host-lint, host-lifecycle, host-prove, host-grammar (the host-* software family) and agentic-host's <code>.host-software</code> composition</li> <li>Relates to: <code>call/0010</code> (the <code>.host-software</code> Where-room recipe), <code>call/0013</code> (reserve agentic-host for this repository), <code>call/0017</code> (every phase emits a receipt, the <code>recurring-per-component</code> machinery), <code>STRUCTURE.md</code> ("the Where room is the software under test, one or more components").</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-4"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-4">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>agentic-host is the development host for the <strong>whole</strong> host-* family (host-lint, host-lifecycle, host-prove, host-grammar), not just host-lint. The planning (<code>plan/</code>, <code>call/</code>, <code>MEMORY.md</code>) and the worktrees for all of them live here, and their code is authored here.</p> <p>Each host-* artifact wears two hats at once:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Developed software</strong>: its own repo (<code>connollydavid/host-*</code>), its own test suite and <strong>CI</strong> (<code>ci.yml</code>), its own co-located specs, its own version/tag/release lifecycle. The <em>producer</em> identity.</li> <li><strong>Methodology tooling</strong>: agentic-host (and any adopter) consumes a pinned version to operate or verify the methodology: host-lint as the hygiene hook, host-lifecycle as the lifecycle driver, host-prove as the deep-rung verifier. The <em>consumer</em> identity.</li> </ol> <p>The residency was inconsistent: <strong>host-lint</strong> is a <code>.host-software</code> Where component (developed here, its built binary and skill served from the worktree), while <strong>host-lifecycle</strong> and <strong>host-prove</strong> sit under <code>tools/</code> as referenced submodules, and <strong>host-grammar</strong> is a git-dep library. That asymmetry, an accident of host-lint being "first," not a principled line, led to a false "tooling vs Where-room software" binary: a conclusion that <code>host-lifecycle release host-lifecycle</code> "cannot resolve" and that embedding a tool as a Where component would be a category error. It is not. The methodology already supports <strong>more than one software-under-development per host</strong>: <code>.host-software</code> is explicitly "one or more components", agentic-host already carries two (<code>host-lint</code> + <code>host</code>), and the receipt machinery's <code>embed</code>/<code>release</code> are <code>recurring-per-component</code>.</p> <h2 id="decision-outcome-2"><a class="header" href="#decision-outcome-2">Decision Outcome</a></h2> <p><strong>agentic-host is a multi-software development host.</strong> Everything <code>host-*</code> is software <em>developed here</em> and is embedded as a <code>.host-software</code> <strong>Where-room component</strong>: host-lint (already), host-lifecycle, host-prove, host-grammar. Each is released through the lifecycle orchestration (<code>host-lifecycle release <component></code>) and emits its receipt in the project ledger, while keeping its own producer CI and release in its own repo. Its tooling function is served from its Where worktree (built binary + worktree-sourced skills), the host-lint model.</p> <p>The clean line:</p> <ul> <li><strong><code>tools/</code></strong> holds only genuinely <strong>external</strong> referenced verification tools: <strong>allium</strong> (JUXT), <strong>specula</strong>. "Reference, don't vendor" governs an <em>adopter</em> consuming tools; it does not govern the <em>development host</em> that builds them.</li> <li><strong><code>.host-software</code></strong> holds the software developed here, the whole host-* family.</li> </ul> <p>The split identity is honored by a <strong>lightweight</strong> boundary (each tool's own repo, CI, and release/version); giving each tool its own agentic project is <strong>not</strong> required. agentic-host stays the <em>single</em> development host for a coupled family (host-grammar is a shared build dependency of host-lint and host-lifecycle; the roadmap is one). Fragmenting into a project per tool would leave agentic-host "responsible only for host-template", over-separation that dissolves a real strength.</p> <h2 id="considered-options"><a class="header" href="#considered-options">Considered options</a></h2> <ul> <li><em><em>Each host-</em> tool as its own agentic project</em>* (own <code>cast/</code>/<code>call/</code>/<code>plan/</code>/<code>.host</code>). Rejected: the producer identity needs only own-repo + CI + release, not full methodology ceremony per tool; N projects fragment one coupled roadmap and multiply operational cost for one developer.</li> <li><strong>Status quo</strong>: host-* tools stay <code>tools/</code> submodules, released ad-hoc. Rejected: it rests on the false tools-vs-software binary, blocks dogfooding <code>release</code> on the tools, and is asymmetric with host-lint for no principled reason.</li> <li><strong>Embed the family as Where components of one multi-software development host</strong> (chosen): uniform with host-lint, makes <code>release <component></code> resolve, and reuses the <code>recurring-per-component</code> machinery already built.</li> </ul> <h2 id="consequences-4"><a class="header" href="#consequences-4">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>host-lifecycle, host-prove, host-grammar stop being <code>tools/</code> submodule / git-dep references and become <code>.host-software</code> Where components (see <code>plan/0028</code>); <code>tools/</code> then holds only allium + specula.</li> <li>The skill-wiring mechanism (<code>link-skills.sh</code>, which symlinks <code>tools/*/skills/*</code>) must also wire a Where component's skills from its worktree, the host-lint pattern, generalized to multi-skill components.</li> <li>host-grammar is a library (no deployable artifact): it releases tag-only, and its dependents (host-lint, host-lifecycle) keep their git-rev dependency pinned to its released tag (closing the current unpinned-host-grammar gap in host-lifecycle's manifest).</li> <li>The general pattern, <em>a development host embeds the tools it develops as Where software, an adopter references them</em>, may warrant a spine clarification in <code>host-template</code>; that is a separate follow-up, not this project-scoped decision.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="the-host--static-musl-releases-build-hermetically-against-a-pinned-dependency-bundle"><a class="header" href="#the-host--static-musl-releases-build-hermetically-against-a-pinned-dependency-bundle">The host-* static-musl releases build hermetically against a pinned dependency bundle</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-22</li> <li>Scope: host-lint, host-lifecycle, host-prove (the artifact-bearing Where-room software)</li> <li>Relates: the spine MUST authored in <code>host-template</code> (<code>ecce498</code>, the methodology home for "a component shipping static release binaries reproduces them offline from pinned inputs"); <code>plan/0032</code> (the milestone); <code>plan/0028</code> (the <code>GLIBC_2.39</code> divergence this retires); <code>call/0010</code> (the <code>.host-software</code> recipe); <code>call/0018</code> (discharge is re-derivation in a pinned toolchain, to which this adds offline hermeticity).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-5"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-5">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>The recorded canonical artifact for each host-* binary was a glibc-dynamic <code>target/release/<bin></code> built in <code>rust:1.95.0</code>, with the <code>host-grammar</code> git dependency and the crates.io deps fetched over the network during the build. Two gaps followed:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Not portable.</strong> The certified binary linked <code>GLIBC_2.39</code>, so it did not run on an older-glibc host. <code>plan/0028</code>'s <code>--install-hooks</code> had to fall back to a local build that differed from the canonical hash (sanctioned by <code>plan/0009</code>, but a standing divergence).</li> <li><strong>Not hermetic.</strong> A network fetch at build time means the build inputs are not pinned, so the re-derivation <code>call/0018</code> relies on is not reproducible from a fixed input set.</li> </ul> <p>The producer release CI separately cross-compiled a static-musl asset by a <em>different</em> recipe, so the distributed binary was not the certified one (distribute did not equal certify).</p> <h2 id="decision-3"><a class="header" href="#decision-3">Decision</a></h2> <p><em><em>The host-</em> artifact tools record a static <code>x86_64-unknown-linux-musl</code> canonical artifact, built in a digest-pinned <code>clux/muslrust</code> image, offline against a pinned hash-verified vendored-dependency bundle.</em>*</p> <ul> <li>The <code>.host-software</code> recipe for each tool records the musl <code>toolchain</code> digest, a <code>build</code> that passes <code>--offline --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl</code> (with <code>RUSTUP_TOOLCHAIN=stable</code>, the image std's toolchain name), and the musl <code>artifact</code> path and sha256.</li> <li><strong>host-lint owns the bundle.</strong> Its dependency set is the superset, so it publishes the versioned hash-pinned <code>vendor-vN</code> release; host-lint and host-lifecycle record <code>deps-bundle = <url> <sha256></code> and build under <code>--network none</code> after a verified download. Hosting the bundle on agentic-host would invert the host-to-software direction.</li> <li><strong>host-prove has no third-party dependencies</strong>, so it builds <code>--offline</code> against an empty source set with no bundle (hermetic by emptiness, not by <code>--network none</code>).</li> <li>The methodology MUST is in the template, not here. This decision records only the host-* family adopting the production anchor as its first adopter, an instance-scoped software change.</li> </ul> <h2 id="consequences-5"><a class="header" href="#consequences-5">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li><strong>Distribute equals certify.</strong> Each producer release job builds the musl asset by the recorded recipe in the pinned image, so the shipped asset is byte-identical to the <code>--verify-build</code> artifact.</li> <li><strong>The <code>plan/0028</code> local-build workaround retires.</strong> The canonical musl binary is static-pie and runs on any glibc, so <code>software --install-hooks</code> installs the canonical binary itself, verified against its recorded hash.</li> <li><strong>The bundle is a maintained, versioned input.</strong> A dependency change requires a new <code>vendor-vN</code>, a re-pin in <code>.host-software</code> and the producer <code>deps-bundle.lock</code>, and a re-release. <code>software --check</code> enforces the drift between the two pins; <code>software --verify-build</code> enforces the two-part hermeticity (the staged-bundle sha matches <em>and</em> the build runs with no egress).</li> <li><strong>Reproducibility certification stays OS-granular</strong> (<code>attest-host</code>): only linux-x86_64-musl is certified; other-platform assets ship with a checksums manifest, stated uncertified.</li> </ul> <h2 id="alternatives-considered-1"><a class="header" href="#alternatives-considered-1">Alternatives considered</a></h2> <ul> <li><strong>A simpler older-glibc canonical image</strong> (portability without musl): a single static binary on any libc was preferred over chasing a glibc floor, and musl is what the producer CI already distributed.</li> <li><strong>Fetch-at-build with a committed <code>Cargo.lock</code> only</strong>: the lock pins versions but the build still reaches the network, so it is reproducible-ish but not hermetic; <code>--verify-build</code> could pass while consuming an upstream that later changes.</li> <li><strong>Vendoring the sources into each repo (no bundle)</strong>: bloats every producer with the same tree and has no shared, hash-verifiable anchor; one published <code>vendor-vN</code> is checked once and consumed by both bundle-bearing tools.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="emergent-tell-discovery-is-a-mechanical-gather-that-the-operator-triages"><a class="header" href="#emergent-tell-discovery-is-a-mechanical-gather-that-the-operator-triages">Emergent-tell discovery is a mechanical gather that the operator triages</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-24</li> <li>Scope: host-lint (the <code>gather</code> subcommand) and host-lifecycle (the <code>verify</code> and <code>adopt</code> skill reflect steps). The software implementation of the spine's reflective grammar-growth doctrine.</li> <li>Relates: the spine doctrine in <code>host-template</code> (<code>27d815b</code>, "the hygiene grammar grows by reflective practice"); <code>plan/0034</code> (which authored the doctrine and deferred this software decision); <code>plan/0035</code> (the milestone that built it); host-lint's <code>LEXICON</code> allowlist (the per-project legitimacy declaration the gather complements).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-6"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-6">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p><code>plan/0034</code> authored the doctrine: the shared tell corpus is incomplete and grows by reflective practice, with discovery mechanical-first (sweep history and recent work for a recurring shape the lane misses) and operator-validated. It deferred the software decision for the discovery tooling until that tooling existed. <code>plan/0035</code> built it. This records how the discovery is implemented and where its limits are.</p> <h2 id="decision-4"><a class="header" href="#decision-4">Decision</a></h2> <p>Discovery is a <code>gather</code> subcommand on host-lint. It runs <code>git log</code> over the repository and scans the tracked markdown headers itself (one command, parse-free for a weak agent), extracts the word-then-numeral shape, drops what the grammar already catches (<code>FLAG_TERMS</code>) and what the legitimacy allowlists name (<code>PREV_SKIP</code>, <code>UNITS</code>, a small stop set, a <code>#</code> reference, a year or hash), and reports the recurring residue ranked by count with example lines.</p> <p>The gather is <strong>advisory</strong> and exits zero; it never gates. It <strong>never auto-graduates</strong> a candidate into the shared grammar, and it <strong>never auto-bans</strong> one. The operator triages each candidate: propose it upstream to the shared grammar, declare it in the <code>LEXICON</code>, or leave it. The <code>verify</code> and <code>adopt</code> skills prompt the gather, at the verify gate and at the migration moment.</p> <h2 id="considered-options-1"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-1">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>Auto-graduate a frequently-recurring shape.</strong> Rejected: the universality call (a tell or this project's own domain vocabulary?) needs operator judgment, and an auto-graduation would flag an adopter's legitimate vocabulary.</li> <li><strong>A gating gather that blocks the commit or the gate.</strong> Rejected: discovery is recall-biased and surfaces some noise, so blocking on it would be a false gate. The gates stay the green sweep; the gather informs.</li> <li><strong>An advisory surface that the operator triages (chosen).</strong> Keeps the human in the loop for the call the tool cannot make.</li> </ol> <h2 id="consequences-6"><a class="header" href="#consequences-6">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: mechanizes the doctrine's discovery; one weak-agent command, git and parse in one process; reuses host-lint's own grammar and allowlists to separate a candidate from noise; keeps the operator's judgment where the universality call lives; surfaced a real candidate (<code>lens</code>) on the first dogfood.</li> <li>Costs: recall-biased, so it surfaces some residue the operator dismisses; the first corpus is commit subjects and markdown headers, with prose and code comments a later extension; no project-local ban surface (YAGNI, every tell seen so far is universal).</li> </ul> <h2 id="confirmation"><a class="header" href="#confirmation">Confirmation</a></h2> <p>gather unit and integration tests pass; a dogfood run on this repository surfaces a sensible candidate set; the <code>verify</code> and <code>adopt</code> skills name the gather step; the weak-agent (Qwen-3.5-4B) triages a candidate correctly.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="reconcile-is-concept-as-uri-project-local-facts-and-three-checks"><a class="header" href="#reconcile-is-concept-as-uri-project-local-facts-and-three-checks">Reconcile is concept-as-URI: project-local facts and three checks</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-25</li> <li>Scope: host-lifecycle (<code>reconcile</code>, the <code>.host-software</code> schema additions, and the <code>manifest --check</code> hardening). The software implementation of the spine's concept-as-URI reconcile doctrine.</li> <li>Relates: the spine doctrine in <code>host-template</code> (<code>7be692f</code>, the reconcile arm; <code>e4f6207</code>, the <code>UPGRADING</code> entry); <code>plan/0039</code> (which authored the doctrine and this decision); <code>call/0020</code> (the host family as Where-room software) and the earlier inline-annotation reconcile (<code>plan/0036</code>).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-7"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-7">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p><code>plan/0036</code> shipped the reconcile arm as an inline <code><!-- host-reconcile: KIND --></code> annotation: a restatement that had to stay carried a comment the tool checked against the spine manifest's <code>[components]</code>/<code>[verification]</code> data. The operator rejected every inline annotation form on aesthetic grounds (any inline annotation is checker machinery in the prose), and the manifest data was an agentic-host overfit living in the shared spine. <code>plan/0039</code> evolved the arm to concept-as-URI. This records the software implementation and its limits.</p> <h2 id="decision-5"><a class="header" href="#decision-5">Decision</a></h2> <p>A project's own facts source from its <code>.host-software</code>, never the shared spine. The <code>components</code> are the <code>[software]</code> members minus a single-file front door (a member marked <code>front-door = true</code>); the <code>verifiers</code> are a <code>[verification] drivers = ...</code> stanza. The lifecycle manifest is hardened to phases only: <code>manifest --check</code> rejects any stanza that is not a <code>[phase "..."]</code>, so no adopter inherits another project's facts and no overfit creeps back into the spine.</p> <p>Each methodology concept (<code>components</code>, <code>verifiers</code>, <code>software-root</code>, <code>spec-home</code>) is defined once at a <code>{#id}</code> anchor on a heading (its home) and pointed at with a <code>[text](FILE#id)</code> link. <code>reconcile</code> runs three checks over the tracked docs: link-integrity (every concept link resolves to its home), declared-anchor (the link names a real concept), and coverage (each project-local home names its full <code>.host-software</code> set, so a dropped tool fails by absence). A home is a heading ending in <code>{#id}</code> (the placement mdBook honors) and its section runs to the next heading of the same or higher level.</p> <p>The inline annotation is deprecated: it keeps checking during the transition and is warned, and the form retires a spine revision later. The cutover is fail-safe, since a surviving annotation is never silently inert.</p> <h2 id="considered-options-2"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-2">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>Keep the inline annotation, renamed.</strong> Rejected: every inline form is checker machinery sitting in the prose, which the operator rejected on aesthetic grounds.</li> <li><strong>Generate a <code>concepts.md</code> aggregating the definitions.</strong> Rejected: with every definition in one authored doc, a page of links back to them is redundant; the tool carries the concept vocabulary and the homes are found by their anchors.</li> <li><strong>Concept-as-URI with project-local facts (chosen).</strong> One canonical definition per concept, pointed at; the facts live with the project; the spine stays phases only.</li> </ol> <h2 id="consequences-7"><a class="header" href="#consequences-7">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: single-source definitions a weak agent reads through correctly; the spine carries no project overfit; project facts are project-local and tool-enforced; validated on stock mdBook and the real Qwen-3.5-4B, which authored a home, a stanza, and a pointer migration.</li> <li>Costs: coverage guards the home, so an enumeration left un-pointed elsewhere can drift unguarded (the author's choice); the weak agent needs the home and stanza forms shown, so the doctrine shows them; the inline form lingers one spine revision under deprecate-then-retire.</li> </ul> <h2 id="confirmation-1"><a class="header" href="#confirmation-1">Confirmation</a></h2> <p>The host-lifecycle suite passes; <code>reconcile</code>, <code>prose</code>, and <code>software --check</code> are green on this repository with the released v0.25.0; the real Qwen-3.5-4B authors a concept home (with the anchor at the heading end), the <code>[verification]</code> stanza, and an annotation-to-pointer migration; adversarial review closed the heading-placement and coverage-section holes before release.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="in-plan-tasks-are-a-receipted-graph-anchored-nodes-a-third-ledger-prerequisite-edges"><a class="header" href="#in-plan-tasks-are-a-receipted-graph-anchored-nodes-a-third-ledger-prerequisite-edges">In-plan tasks are a receipted graph: anchored nodes, a third ledger, prerequisite edges</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-25</li> <li>Scope: host-lifecycle (the task-graph feature: the task-block parser, the <code>.host-task-receipts</code> ledger, the global dependency graph, the per-task gate, the verify-field runner, and the task-anchor link checker). The software implementation of the <code>plan/0042</code> spine doctrine.</li> <li>Relates: <code>plan/0042</code> (which designed this and carries the five-lens adversarial review and the operator re-cut); the spine doctrine authored in <code>host-template</code> for <code>plan/0042</code>; <code>plan/0037</code> (the receipts ontology this extends to a third kind); <code>plan/0039</code> and <code>call/0023</code> (the anchored-reference pattern); <code>call/0018</code> (re-derivation plus input-digest staleness).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-8"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-8">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>A milestone's build sequence is numbered prose, so an in-plan task has no receipt (a step marked done is an unverifiable claim, the same defect the phase receipts close) and no stable identity (a position renumbers when a plan is re-cut, so neither a receipt nor a dependency edge can key on it). An arbitrary-complexity project also has independent and cross-milestone tasks a prose list cannot express. <code>plan/0042</code> makes the tasks first-class; this records the software decision and the grammar, settled after the adversarial review.</p> <h2 id="decision-6"><a class="header" href="#decision-6">Decision</a></h2> <p>A task is a <code>### </code> heading under the <code>## Build sequence</code> section that ends in <code>{#<anchor>}</code> (the placement stock mdBook honors). A <code>### </code> heading outside that section, or one inside it without an anchor, is rejected, so a task is never confused with an ordinary subsection. Structured fields follow the heading as bullets:</p> <pre><code>## Build sequence ### Gather data {#gather-data} - verify: cargo test gather ### Implement the parser {#implement-parser} - depends: #gather-data, plan/0041#fail-closed - verify: cargo test parser - inputs: src/parser.rs ### Record the decision {#record-decision} - depends: #implement-parser - verify: attested call/0024 </code></pre> <ul> <li><code>depends</code> lists the prerequisites: a local <code>#<anchor></code> or a cross-milestone <code>plan/NNNN#<anchor></code>. Omitting the bullet means the task depends on the previous task in document order (the linear default); the first task in the section is a root. The author declares prerequisites only; the tool derives the parallel frontier (the tasks whose prerequisites all carry a done receipt), never the author.</li> <li><code>verify</code> is either a shell command the gate re-runs (mechanical), or <code>attested <call/NNNN | operator></code> (attested by a decision the gate resolves, or an operator confirmation). A mechanical <code>done</code> with no command is a HAZARD.</li> <li><code>inputs</code> lists the files a mechanical verify covers, fingerprinted for digest staleness; without it the gate re-runs the command on every check (no cheap offline staleness).</li> </ul> <p>Each task emits a receipt into a new <code>.host-task-receipts</code> ledger, tool-written, keyed by the global anchor <code>plan/NNNN#anchor</code>, in the git-config stanza form the other receipt ledgers use:</p> <pre><code>[receipt "plan/0042#implement-parser"] disposition = done verify = cargo test parser inputs = src/parser.rs digest = <git-hash-object of inputs> evidence = <the re-derivation record> tool = host-lifecycle@X.Y.Z recorded = 2026-06-25 </code></pre> <p>The graph is project-wide: because an anchor is a global URI and the ledger is keyed globally, a <code>depends</code> may cross milestones, so cycle detection and the dangling-dependency check run over the whole resolved edge set. The gate fires per task (a declared anchored task with no receipt is a HAZARD, the way the receipt gate already HAZARDs a manifest phase with no receipt), so it needs no per-milestone completion signal. A substantive <code>skip</code> carries a resolvable <code>call/NNNN</code> citation; the gate is non-omission assurance for declared work, not a completeness proof. A reverse check holds every ledger key to a live anchor, so a renamed or removed task cannot leave a stale done behind. A coordinator dispatches a frontier to parallel sub-agents only when they are resource-isolated (separate worktrees), since <code>depends</code> is an ordering constraint, not a mutual-exclusion lock.</p> <h2 id="considered-options-3"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-3">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>An inline status the author hand-edits.</strong> Rejected: it is not tamper-evident, so the gate could be cleared by hand under pressure.</li> <li><strong>Task receipts in the existing operational <code>.host-lifecycle-receipts</code> keyspace.</strong> The review's recommendation (a task receipt is operational by the <code>plan/0037</code> discriminant). Rejected by the operator for physical separation.</li> <li><strong>A new <code>.host-task-receipts</code> ledger, a third receipt kind (chosen).</strong> Physically separate, tool-written, keyed by the global anchor. The operator accepted the ontology cost.</li> </ol> <h2 id="consequences-8"><a class="header" href="#consequences-8">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: a task gains auditable, re-derivable completion and a stable identity; the project becomes one schedulable graph for parallel sub-agents; the anchored reference reuses the <code>call/0023</code> pattern; the form holds at the weak-agent bar (the Qwen-3.5-4B authored the within-milestone and cross-milestone forms).</li> <li>Costs: a third receipt kind beside the <code>plan/0037</code> two-file ontology; a mandatory per-task gate raises the adopter bar, so the line between a task and a trivial step stays at the anchored heading; author-written verify commands run during the gate, so a guarded, non-recursive runner is load-bearing.</li> </ul> <h2 id="confirmation-2"><a class="header" href="#confirmation-2">Confirmation</a></h2> <p>The grammar is <code>host-lint --all</code> clean, and the real Qwen-3.5-4B authored the local and cross-milestone <code>depends</code> forms (<code>plan/0042</code>). The host-lifecycle implementation and its tests (a cross-milestone diamond and a broken-reference fixture) land with the <code>plan/0042</code> build, and the spine doctrine after it; <code>host-lifecycle validate call/</code> passes on this decision.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="next-fails-closed-on-a-directory-with-no-numbered-entries"><a class="header" href="#next-fails-closed-on-a-directory-with-no-numbered-entries">next fails closed on a directory with no numbered entries</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-25</li> <li>Scope: host-lifecycle (the <code>next</code> subcommand: the fail-closed exit and the did-you-mean room suggestion). The software change of <code>plan/0041</code>.</li> <li>Relates: <code>plan/0041</code> (which carries the operator ruling and the Qwen-3.5-4B ergonomics data); <code>connollydavid/host-lifecycle#1</code> (the footgun this closes); <code>plan/0039</code> (whose migration surfaced it).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-9"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-9">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p><code>host-lifecycle next <dir></code> printed the next free <code>NNNN</code> number as the maximum of a directory's numbered entries plus one, and fell back to <code>0000</code> when a directory had none. So <code>next .</code> (the host root), a typo'd path, or any non-room path returned a plausible <code>0000</code>, and an agent could go on to create a colliding <code>0000-slug</code> in the wrong place. At the weak-agent bar a garbled argument returned a wrong answer rather than an error.</p> <h2 id="decision-7"><a class="header" href="#decision-7">Decision</a></h2> <p><code>next</code> fails closed. A path that is not a directory, and a directory with no <code>NNNN-slug</code> entries (the host root, a typo, a non-room path, or a freshly created empty room), exit non-zero (code <code>2</code>, the code the tool already returns for its sibling path and argument errors) rather than printing <code>0000</code>. The error names the likely fix. When the parent holds a known room with entries, the message carries a did-you-mean line with the exact command (<code>host-lifecycle next plan</code>); otherwise it points at a room and states that a room's first entry is <code>0000</code>. The suggestion is drawn only from the known rooms, so a generated or build directory is never suggested.</p> <p>The fresh-empty-room case folds into fail-closed rather than a special case, because a room gains a first entry quickly and fail-safe beats fail-unsafe. The Qwen-3.5-4B ergonomics check confirmed the form: the weak agent recovered the intended room from the diagnostic, and recognized the empty room as a first-entry case without a dedicated affordance (<code>plan/0041</code>).</p> <h2 id="considered-options-4"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-4">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>Keep the <code>0000</code> fallback.</strong> Rejected: it is the footgun.</li> <li><strong>Add a <code>--first</code> flag for the empty-room case.</strong> Deferred: the ergonomics data showed the agent recovers unaided, so the affordance is unwarranted now.</li> <li><strong>Fail closed with a did-you-mean drawn from the known rooms (chosen).</strong> Fail-safe, and the suggestion is the form the weak agent recovered from most reliably.</li> </ol> <h2 id="consequences-9"><a class="header" href="#consequences-9">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: a garbled or misdirected <code>next</code> is a loud error rather than a silent wrong number, so a colliding <code>0000-slug</code> cannot be created by mistake; the suggestion names a real room only.</li> <li>Costs: a freshly scaffolded empty room now errors on its first <code>next</code>; the blast radius is small (the change is local to <code>next</code>), and the message states the first-entry number.</li> </ul> <h2 id="confirmation-3"><a class="header" href="#confirmation-3">Confirmation</a></h2> <p>A unit test covers a populated room (the maximum plus one, unchanged), an empty directory, a missing path, and the host root (each non-zero). <code>cargo test</code> is green (<code>101</code> tests) and <code>cargo clippy</code> is clean. The released binary gates green, and <code>software --check</code> and <code>software --verify-build</code> reproduce the artifact.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="the-front-door-check-cover-the-spines-structured-facts-generate-the-stamp"><a class="header" href="#the-front-door-check-cover-the-spines-structured-facts-generate-the-stamp">The front-door check: cover the spine's structured facts, generate the stamp</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-25</li> <li>Scope: host-lifecycle (the <code>front-door</code> and <code>front-door --check</code> subcommands: phase and tool coverage, and <code>.host</code> stamp generation). The software change of <code>plan/0040</code>.</li> <li>Relates: <code>plan/0040</code> (the milestone and its two-lens design review); <code>plan/0039</code> and <code>call/0023</code> (the reconcile coverage pattern this reuses for a link-free doc); <code>plan/0038</code> (the meta-repo precedent: no adopter obligation).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-10"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-10">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>The single-file front door (the <code>.host-software</code> member marked <code>front-door = true</code>) is a published README in its own repo, outside any host's verify gate. It must stay self-contained, so it cannot point at the spine with a link the way an in-host doc does (the reconcile arm); it restates. Every spine move stales the restatement silently, and the front-door phase list had already dropped the <code>release</code> phase.</p> <h2 id="decision-8"><a class="header" href="#decision-8">Decision</a></h2> <p><code>host-lifecycle front-door [--check]</code> holds the front door to the spine's structured facts:</p> <ul> <li>Coverage of the lifecycle phases against the manifest (each phase named, checked as a backtick token, since a bare word like "release" recurs in prose), and of the wired tools against the <code>.host-software</code> <code>[verification]</code> drivers plus the lifecycle engine.</li> <li>Generation of the <code>.host</code> stamp block from the tool's canonical format, checked byte-exact.</li> </ul> <p>The check is wired as a step in agentic-host's CI (the reproducible-build job), where the spine sources are materialized. It is agentic-host-local: a separate step, not the shared spine recheck, so no adopter without a front door runs it, and no <code>UPGRADING</code> entry is owed.</p> <p>A two-lens adversarial review rejected the original mechanism (the template carries a hand-authored fragment per section): a fragment is a second source of truth, so the check would prove the README matches the fragment and never that the fragment matches the spine. Generation draws only from the structured data the tool already reads. The version pins, the lanes rule, and the tool descriptions have no structured home and stay authored; a structured pin home is a named follow-up.</p> <h2 id="considered-options-5"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-5">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>Hand-authored template fragments, stitched (the original proposal).</strong> Rejected: a second source of truth that relocates the drift rather than removing it.</li> <li><strong>A drift gate only, with no generation.</strong> Declined by the operator earlier (it leaves the restatements in place, against the front-door principle).</li> <li><strong>Generate from structured data, coverage for the fact-sets, pins de-scoped (chosen).</strong> Drift-proof for every fact with a home, and honest about the rest.</li> </ol> <h2 id="consequences-10"><a class="header" href="#consequences-10">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: a phase or wired tool dropped from the spine fails the front door by absence, and the stamp block cannot be restated wrong; the teaching prose stays authored, so the front door still reads as written.</li> <li>Costs: the version pins are not drift-proof yet (no structured home); the check runs in agentic-host's CI rather than the front-door repo, an asymmetry the design review records.</li> </ul> <h2 id="confirmation-4"><a class="header" href="#confirmation-4">Confirmation</a></h2> <p><code>front-door --check</code> is clean on the front door once the <code>release</code> phase is named, and a deliberately staled front door fails it (a drifted stamp block, or a phase named nowhere). Unit tests cover the coverage and stamp logic. <code>cargo test</code> and <code>cargo clippy</code> are green, and the released binary gates green.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="rename-the-front-door-check-to-entrance-with-a-deprecate-then-retire-migration"><a class="header" href="#rename-the-front-door-check-to-entrance-with-a-deprecate-then-retire-migration">Rename the front-door check to <code>entrance</code>, with a deprecate-then-retire migration</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-25</li> <li>Scope: host-lifecycle (the <code>entrance</code> and <code>entrance --check</code> subcommands, formerly <code>front-door</code>; the <code>.host-software</code> <code>entrance = true</code> flag, formerly <code>front-door = true</code>; the deprecate-then-retire migration shim). agentic-host-local. The rename is pulled forward from <code>plan/0043</code>, ahead of that milestone's opt-in generalization.</li> <li>Relates: <code>plan/0043</code> (the entrance milestone; the name settled by Qwen-3.5-4B data); <code>call/0026</code> and <code>plan/0040</code> (the front-door check this renames; its agentic-host-local scope stands); <code>plan/0039</code> (the deprecate-then-retire path and the fail-safe cutover, reused here for a config-key rename).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-11"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-11">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>The front-door check (<code>call/0026</code>, host-lifecycle v0.27.0) named the methodology's single-file entry point <code>front-door</code>. <code>plan/0043</code> settled the name <code>entrance</code> by a Qwen-3.5-4B run over the host-and-guest metaphor (a guest comes in the entrance), chosen with no alias the way <code>plan/0039</code> fixes a name that overfits. The operator chose to land the rename now as a standalone change, keeping the capability hardwired and agentic-host-local, and to defer the opt-in generalization to <code>plan/0043</code>.</p> <p>A rename with no alias strands any surviving old spelling. A <code>.host-software</code> that still reads <code>front-door = true</code> is silently demoted to a component, the silent failure <code>plan/0039</code> forbids. So the rename owes a migration.</p> <h2 id="decision-9"><a class="header" href="#decision-9">Decision</a></h2> <ul> <li>Rename <code>front-door</code> to <code>entrance</code> with no alias: the subcommand, the <code>.host-software</code> flag, the code, and the agentic-host CI invocation. Released as host-lifecycle v0.28.0.</li> <li>A migration carries the transition, deprecate-then-retire (<code>plan/0039</code>). The parser accepts a legacy <code>front-door = true</code> as the entrance, so a pre-rename <code>.host-software</code> keeps working rather than dropping to a component; the <code>entrance</code> command warns and names the rename, so the old spelling never passes silently. The shim is slated for removal a release later. Released as host-lifecycle v0.28.1.</li> <li>Comments stay forward-looking and name <code>entrance</code>; the migration shim is the one place the old spelling lives.</li> <li>The scope stays agentic-host-local. No adopter declares an entrance, so no <code>UPGRADING</code> entry is owed; the generalization that would make entrances adopter-facing stays with <code>plan/0043</code>.</li> </ul> <h2 id="considered-options-6"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-6">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>Rename and generalize as one atomic change (<code>plan/0043</code> as designed).</strong> Declined by the operator for now: pull the rename forward, defer the generalization.</li> <li><strong>Rename with a permanent alias.</strong> Rejected: the <code>plan/0039</code> way fixes an overfit name, it never aliases it.</li> <li><strong>Rename with no migration (the v0.28.0 cutover alone).</strong> Rejected: a surviving <code>front-door = true</code> is silently demoted to a component.</li> <li><strong>Rename, no alias, plus a deprecate-then-retire migration shim (chosen).</strong></li> </ol> <h2 id="consequences-11"><a class="header" href="#consequences-11">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: the name matches the host-and-guest metaphor of the methodology; a pre-rename <code>.host-software</code> still resolves and is told to rename; the codebase reads forward-looking.</li> <li>Costs: a second release (v0.28.1) carries the migration that the v0.28.0 cutover omitted; the shim is debt, owed a retirement a release later (a named follow-up). The full plan/0039 retire step (a hard fail plus an adopter ledger entry) waits until the generalization makes entrances adopter-facing.</li> </ul> <h2 id="confirmation-5"><a class="header" href="#confirmation-5">Confirmation</a></h2> <p><code>entrance --check .</code> is clean on agentic-host's front door. A legacy <code>front-door = true</code> warns and still resolves the entrance, covered by a unit test. The full host-lifecycle suite is green, the released binary gates green, and the whole-suite CI is green.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="the-entrance-check-generalizes-to-a-declared-entrance-stanza"><a class="header" href="#the-entrance-check-generalizes-to-a-declared-entrance-stanza">The entrance check generalizes to a declared <code>[entrance]</code> stanza</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-25</li> <li>Scope: host-lifecycle (the <code>entrance</code> check: the <code>[entrance]</code> stanza parse, the routed concept set, the document resolution, the legacy shim) and host-template (the entrance doctrine and the adopter <code>UPGRADING</code> entry). Adopter-facing. The software change of <code>plan/0043</code>.</li> <li>Relates: <code>plan/0043</code> (the milestone, its design review and code review); <code>call/0026</code> and <code>call/0027</code> (the front-door check and the rename this generalizes and completes); <code>plan/0039</code> (the reconcile arm the entrance is the standalone sibling of); <code>plan/0040</code> (the front-door check, now generalized).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-12"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-12">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>The front-door check (<code>call/0026</code>, <code>plan/0040</code>) was agentic-host-local and hardwired to one member's <code>README.md</code> marked <code>entrance = true</code>. The same self-blindness recurs for any self-contained document that restates the spine and cannot link to it: a published front door, a standalone <code>SKILL.md</code>, an operator-and-agent landing page. Each restates, and the restatement stales when the spine moves, with no in-context link to give. The capability had no way for another document, or another project, to opt in.</p> <h2 id="decision-10"><a class="header" href="#decision-10">Decision</a></h2> <p>A project declares one entrance, a global singleton, in an <code>[entrance]</code> stanza in <code>.host-software</code>: <code>member</code> (the <code>[software]</code> member it belongs to, set apart from <code>components</code>), <code>document</code> (the file within that member's worktree, default <code>README.md</code>, so a <code>SKILL.md</code> or a landing page is reached by path), and <code>restates</code> (<code>true</code> for every concept, or a named subset of the closed vocabulary <code>phases</code>, <code>tools</code>, and the <code>.host</code> stamp). <code>host-lifecycle entrance --check</code> then holds the document complete against the declared concepts: it generates the stamp and covers the rest. The entrance is the standalone sibling of reconcile, which holds a linkable document with pointers; the entrance holds a self-contained one with coverage and generation, because it cannot link.</p> <p>The declaration is a stanza rather than a per-member value, so the marker that sets the entrance member apart from <code>components</code> stays unambiguous and no richer value can demote the front door into the component set. The spine carries the doctrine and an adopter <code>UPGRADING</code> entry (host-template <code>ba86125</code>). The legacy per-member marker (<code>front-door = true</code> or <code>entrance = true</code>) is accepted by a deprecation shim, warned, until a later revision retires it (the named follow-up, the <code>plan/0039</code> deprecate-then-retire path).</p> <h2 id="considered-options-7"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-7">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>Keep the front-door check agentic-host-local (<code>plan/0040</code>).</strong> Superseded: the same self-blindness recurs for any project with a self-contained document.</li> <li><strong>A per-member value form (<code>entrance = phases tools</code>).</strong> Rejected by the code review: it breaks the marker that defines <code>components</code> and silently demotes the front door, the <code>call/0027</code> failure reintroduced.</li> <li><strong>A dedicated singleton <code>[entrance]</code> stanza reaching any document by path (chosen).</strong> The marker is unambiguous, the document is not hardwired to <code>README.md</code>, and the entrance stays the one set-apart entry point.</li> </ol> <h2 id="consequences-12"><a class="header" href="#consequences-12">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: any project may declare an entrance and have it held to the spine; the front door is no longer hardwired to one <code>README.md</code>; the marker-break is designed out; the scope is honest, since a document that restates only home-less doctrine declares no checkable concept.</li> <li>Costs: the legacy per-member marker is debt, owed a retirement a release later (the named follow-up); a partial entrance declared on a real component member is a misuse outside the model (the entrance is the single set-apart entry point), a recorded boundary.</li> </ul> <h2 id="confirmation-6"><a class="header" href="#confirmation-6">Confirmation</a></h2> <p><code>entrance --check .</code> is clean on agentic-host's stanza-declared entrance with no deprecation warning; <code>reconcile</code> and <code>software --check</code> are green; a malformed stanza is loud in every consumer (<code>reconcile</code> exits <code>2</code> on a typo'd member, the entrance exits <code>2</code> on an empty <code>restates</code>). The form was settled by a three-reviewer adversarial design review (re-scope verdict, operator re-cut to expand), a Qwen-3.5-4B run on the declaration form and the stanza ergonomics, and a three-reviewer adversarial code review that fixed two blocking defects before release. <code>cargo test</code> is green and <code>cargo clippy</code> clean. Released as host-lifecycle v0.29.0; host-template <code>ba86125</code> carries the doctrine and the <code>UPGRADING</code> entry; agentic-host adopted it.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="the-prose-lane-consults-the-per-project-lexicon"><a class="header" href="#the-prose-lane-consults-the-per-project-lexicon">The prose lane consults the per-project LEXICON</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-25</li> <li>Scope: host-lint (the <code>scan_prose_text</code> mask, the <code>--prose</code> and <code>--docs</code> lanes, the README masked-modes wording). The software change of <code>plan/0044</code>. No spine change, since the existing doctrine is met rather than changed, so it is adopter-facing only as fixed behaviour on the next host-lint bump.</li> <li>Relates: <code>plan/0044</code> (the milestone and its <code>gather-data.md</code>); host-lint issue #13 (the LEXICON) and host-lint issue #16 (the divergence this closes); <code>plan/0039</code> (the living-grammar doctrine the LEXICON is the home for).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-13"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-13">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>The LEXICON allowlist masks a sanctioned phrase before the naming lane classifies a line, so a legitimate tell-shaped token a project declares stops flagging. The prose lane, the host-grammar density engine behind <code>--prose</code> and <code>--docs</code>, never consulted that allowlist. A legitimate domain word the prose grammar reads as an ai-diction trope had no sanctioned escape: the only inline-source escape is the block-level <code>host-lint:ignore</code> fence, and there is no per-project prose allowlist. The living-grammar doctrine names the LEXICON as the home for such a token, so the doctrine was unmet for the prose lane. host-lint issue #16 reports this.</p> <h2 id="decision-11"><a class="header" href="#decision-11">Decision</a></h2> <p>The prose lane consults the same LEXICON. <code>scan_prose_text</code> masks the whole document with <code>mask_allowed</code>, the naming lane's pre-detection blank-out, before both the per-tell scan and the document density score, so a declared phrase contributes to neither. The mask is surgical at the word boundary: a declared <code>rehost harness</code> clears the trope on <code>harness</code> within that phrase and leaves a standalone occurrence reported. One declaration serves both lanes. The README masked-modes wording adds <code>--prose</code> and <code>--docs</code>, so its "in any mode" claim holds. The spine is unchanged, since the doctrine is met rather than changed, so host-template carries no UPGRADING entry.</p> <h2 id="considered-options-8"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-8">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>Extend the existing mask to the prose lane (chosen).</strong> One declaration, the same provenance-gated entries, the same word-boundary specificity. Reuses <code>mask_allowed</code>, so the prose lane grants no muting power the naming lane lacked.</li> <li><strong>A separate prose-vocabulary allowlist.</strong> Rejected: a second list to maintain and a second provenance model, for a need the existing LEXICON already meets.</li> <li><strong>Document the gap as intentional.</strong> Rejected: it would leave the living-grammar doctrine's promised home absent for the prose lane, and force reword-or-tolerate on a legitimate domain word.</li> </ol> <h2 id="consequences-13"><a class="header" href="#consequences-13">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: the doctrine's home now exists for the prose lane; one declaration serves both lanes; the README claim becomes true; no new abuse surface, since the entries are the naming lane's own and the density denominator is the sentence count.</li> <li>Costs: the mask now feeds the density engine, so a mis-scoped entry could hide a real trope; the provenance guards and the per-occurrence word boundary keep it specific. The change removes prose findings when a phrase is declared, so it ships under the removes-findings change class.</li> </ul> <h2 id="confirmation-7"><a class="header" href="#confirmation-7">Confirmation</a></h2> <p>A Qwen-3.5-4B run, direct against rope with a real system prompt and non-contaminated options, confirmed the lane-consistency reading and the surgical word boundary, and judged the reuse adequately bounded with the dissent option available and not chosen (<code>gather-data.md</code>). Regression tests pin the behaviour: a declared phrase clears the trope within the phrase, a standalone occurrence still flags, an empty allowlist leaves the scan unchanged, and the density score drops by the masked phrase alone. The feature shipped as host-lint v0.10.0; the <code>src/lib.rs</code> edit staled two Kani-backed <code>DetectInternalCodeAsName</code> obligations, so v0.10.1 (<code>0c2bfc3</code>, artifact <code>941126c9</code>) re-derived the obligation digests through host-prove and is the pinned release. <code>.host-software</code> re-pinned, the release receipt recorded, host-lint issue #16 closed, and the whole suite green.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="host-reference-is-a-reference-compiler-a-deterministic-normalised-layer-and-a-collaborative-overlay"><a class="header" href="#host-reference-is-a-reference-compiler-a-deterministic-normalised-layer-and-a-collaborative-overlay">host-reference is a reference compiler: a deterministic normalised layer and a collaborative overlay</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-27</li> <li>Scope: a new instance-only <code>host-*</code> component, <code>host-reference</code>, embedded as a Where-room component (a bare store pinned in <code>.host-software</code>). The milestone is <code>plan/0049</code>. This is instance software and binds no adopter; there is no spine change. Two follow-on decisions are carved out: <code>call/0031</code> (the untrusted-input threat model) and <code>call/0032</code> (the engineering-geometry token target).</li> <li>Relates: <code>plan/0049</code> (the milestone, the open questions, and the weak-agent run in its <code>gather-data.md</code>); <code>plan/0039</code> (the concept-as-URI vocabulary the semantic layer binds to); <code>call/0018</code> (discharge as re-derivation in a pinned toolchain, which the immutable layer reuses); <code>call/0010</code> (software as a bare store with worktrees); <code>plan/0042</code> (the receipted task graph the build runs as).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-14"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-14">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>An agentic project applies rigour to what it produces. The build is reproducible, the authored prose is linted, the specifications are checked. The material it consumes has none of that treatment. External reference knowledge, the vendor documents and datasheets and schematics an agent reasons over, enters the context window raw and unpinned. It is heavy in tokens, and nothing records where it came from. The <code>reference</code> memory kind records only a pointer to such material, not its content. So there is no inbound counterpart to the outbound discipline.</p> <p>The primary goal is in-context interpretation: let an agent understand external material inside its context window at low token cost, and act on it and cite it with a provenance trail. The material arrives in many shapes. The available tools (MarkItDown, Docling, Marker) convert most shapes to Markdown, but they are best-effort, they are not reproducible, and they carry no provenance, so their output cannot be attested or traced span by span. They also normalise every shape to Markdown, which ignores that the token-optimal target depends on the content kind. The gap that is ours to fill is a normalisation that is reproducible, attested, and provenance-bearing, the discipline the project already applies to a binary.</p> <h2 id="decision-12"><a class="header" href="#decision-12">Decision</a></h2> <p>Adopt <code>host-reference</code>, a reference compiler, built on a two-layer model.</p> <ul> <li>The immutable normalised layer holds the normalised corpus, a semantic skeleton, and a content-addressed source map that resolves in both directions. It is re-derived byte for byte from the source bytes in a pinned toolchain, so <code>software --check</code> and host-prove attest it the same way they attest a binary (<code>call/0018</code>).</li> <li>The mutable overlay layer holds annotations, edits, and agent notes. It is collaborative and anchored into the immutable layer by standard selectors, so it survives a re-derivation. It is not attested.</li> </ul> <p>Four properties carry the design.</p> <ul> <li>Semantic normalisation to tokens. Each content kind maps to its token-optimal target, and the semantic structure that aids interpretation (headings, reading order, tables, entities, cross references) is preserved rather than discarded. The content spine is Markdown, the token-lean lingua franca; the semantics ride alongside as a sidecar in JSON-LD, whose <code>@context</code> binds to the project concept vocabulary of <code>plan/0039</code>.</li> <li>Deterministic re-derivation and attestation. The immutable layer is a reproducible artifact. Any model-based transform is non-reproducible by nature, so it lives in the overlay, behind a single provider-agnostic adapter, recorded with its provenance (the source hash, the model identity, the prompt). It never enters the attested layer.</li> <li>Bidirectional provenance and editing. The source map resolves a span to its origin and an origin to its span. Round-trip fidelity is a declared per-kind property, verified as a lens law in the property-based lane (full for text and data and markup, approximate for Office and PDF, none for the lossy kinds). An editable view writes back where a well-behaved lens exists. A hard-to-edit kind such as PDF carries its edits in a standard sidecar (the W3C Web Annotation model, and XFDF for PDF) rather than a mutated original.</li> <li>Tiered, range-addressable views. A cheap skeleton is always resident; a full slice is fetched on demand by page range, section, offset, or token budget, so a large document costs only the slice a task needs. Each ingest records the raw token count against the normalised token count, so the saving is a measured number.</li> </ul> <p>The format superset is specified once and built one kind at a time, each kind with a conformance fixture that re-derives byte for byte. The breadth collapses to a small set of shared mechanisms (a zip reader, a compound-file reader, an XML reader, fixed-layout extraction, optical character recognition, and the engineering parsers).</p> <p>Two settled mechanism choices, taken from 2026 practice and the weak-agent run.</p> <ul> <li>The overlay is a Loro document. It is Rust-native, so a pure-Rust reproducible workspace can use it without a second toolchain. Its model is version-controlled by design, and its encoding is compact. Automerge is the recorded fallback if a Git-like change-attribution feature is later required.</li> <li>Optical character recognition is hybrid. A pinned deterministic engine reads clean scans into the immutable layer. The provider-agnostic vision adapter reads diagrams, noisy scans, and handwriting into the overlay, recorded with its provenance.</li> </ul> <p>A capability that a normaliser does not declare defaults to the most restrictive setting (no round-trip, no write-back), so a normaliser that omits a declaration cannot over-claim editability (the Bly fail-safe rule from the cast review).</p> <h2 id="considered-options-9"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-9">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>A new instance-only <code>host-*</code> component (chosen).</strong> It isolates a genuinely new concern as its own bare store, reproducible build, and spec, the same shape as the other components.</li> <li><strong>A subcommand of <code>host-lifecycle</code>.</strong> Rejected: it bolts a large, independent content-transform concern onto the generator and gate tool, whose job is generation and gating.</li> <li><strong>Adopt a state-of-the-art converter as the engine.</strong> Rejected: MarkItDown and Docling are best-effort, not reproducible, and carry no provenance, so their output cannot be attested or traced, and a single-target-to-Markdown conversion ignores the per-kind token-optimal target. They stay useful prior art for the per-kind extractors.</li> <li><strong>Build model-based semantic compression into the core now.</strong> Rejected: it is not byte reproducible, so it would break the one property that lets the corpus be attested. It is kept as a later, evolvable overlay layer behind the same provider adapter, recorded with provenance.</li> </ol> <h2 id="consequences-14"><a class="header" href="#consequences-14">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: inbound reference material gains the reproducibility and provenance discipline the project already applies to its own artifacts; the token saving is measured rather than claimed; in-context interpretation is carried by preserved semantic structure; the source map makes a fact citable and an edit possible; broad format coverage rides a small set of mechanisms; the deterministic line keeps the corpus attestable while the overlay stays free to hold model output and collaborative edits.</li> <li>Costs: the component is a large Rust workspace, since each format kind is its own normaliser with its own fixture; the overlay and the annotation standards add surface; the boundary between the deterministic engine and the model adapter must be policed so no model output leaks into the attested layer; the parser dependencies must stay licence-compatible with the component licence; and two questions are deferred, the untrusted-input threat model (<code>call/0031</code>) and the engineering-geometry token target (<code>call/0032</code>).</li> </ul> <h2 id="confirmation-8"><a class="header" href="#confirmation-8">Confirmation</a></h2> <p>The four agent-facing surfaces (the windowed retrieval selector, the capability declaration, the content-is-data posture, and the deterministic boundary between the two layers) passed at the weak-agent bar: the real <code>qwen3.5-4b</code> on rope chose the intended option in three of three runs each, with the option order rotated so the pass reflects content reasoning rather than position (<code>plan/0049/gather-data.md</code>). The cast reviewed the instrument before the run and the results after it. The component lands as a bare store pinned in <code>.host-software</code>, built reproducibly, with its <code>.allium</code> spec, its check, analyse, and plan lanes, and its obligations discharged, its build run as a receipted task graph (<code>plan/0042</code>), and its release carried by <code>host-lifecycle</code>. The deferred decisions <code>call/0031</code> and <code>call/0032</code> follow.</p> <h2 id="lineage-and-sources"><a class="header" href="#lineage-and-sources">Lineage and sources</a></h2> <p>The per-kind token-target choice rests on the format token-efficiency research: the <a href="https://www.improvingagents.com/blog/best-input-data-format-for-llms/">eleven-format table benchmark</a> (the accuracy-against-tokens trade-off), <a href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/2603.03306">TOON</a>, and <a href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.05447">structured context for file-native agents</a>. Markdown as the content spine and JSON-LD as the separated semantic layer follow 2026 practice (<a href="https://www.searchcans.com/blog/markdown-llm-output-benefits/">markdown for language models</a>, <a href="https://w3c.github.io/json-ld-bp/">JSON-LD best practices</a>). The overlay choice rests on the <a href="https://www.pkgpulse.com/guides/yjs-vs-automerge-vs-loro-crdt-libraries-2026">CRDT comparison</a> and <a href="https://github.com/loro-dev/loro">Loro</a>. The hybrid recognition posture follows the <a href="https://joshua8.ai/ocr-models-vs-vision-llms-vs-tesseract/">2026 recognition guidance</a>. The prior art is <a href="https://github.com/microsoft/markitdown">MarkItDown</a> and Docling. The bidirectional editing model is the lens, or bidirectional transformation, tradition, and the sidecar standards are the W3C Web Annotation model and XFDF.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="ingested-material-is-untrusted-defensive-parsing-a-legible-boundary-fail-safe-refusal"><a class="header" href="#ingested-material-is-untrusted-defensive-parsing-a-legible-boundary-fail-safe-refusal">Ingested material is untrusted: defensive parsing, a legible boundary, fail-safe refusal</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-27</li> <li>Scope: the threat model for <code>host-reference</code> (<code>plan/0049</code>, <code>call/0030</code>). Instance software, binds no adopter, no spine change. Carved out of <code>call/0030</code> as the security follow-on.</li> <li>Relates: <code>call/0030</code> (the component this hardens); <code>plan/0049/gather-data.md</code> (probe four, the content-is-data posture, passed at the weak-agent bar); <code>call/0018</code> (re-derivation and attestation, which the immutable layer's integrity rests on).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-15"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-15">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>The whole job of <code>host-reference</code> is to bring external, untrusted material into an agent's context. That makes it a security surface in two distinct ways, and the two have different victims.</p> <ul> <li>Content-level injection. A document carries text written to be read by the downstream agent as an instruction, the indirect prompt injection of the OWASP list, its first entry. The victim is the consuming agent rather than the parser. A retrieval pipeline is the textbook case, since its job is to pull external content into the context window.</li> <li>Parser-level exploitation. A document attacks the parser itself rather than the model.
