The known classes are external-entity resolution (XXE) and entity-expansion denial of service (the billion-laughs and quadratic-blowup attacks, CWE-776) in XML, vector, and Office formats; decompression bombs and path traversal (zip-slip) in the zip and compound-file containers; active content such as Office macros and vector-format scripts; and malformed input that crashes a native parser. The victim is the host process.</li> </ul> <p>A tool that addressed only one would either poison the agent it serves or expose the host that runs it.</p> <h2 id="decision-13"><a class="header" href="#decision-13">Decision</a></h2> <p>Ingested material is untrusted. <code>host-reference</code> treats it so, in three parts.</p> <h3 id="content-is-data-with-a-legible-boundary"><a class="header" href="#content-is-data-with-a-legible-boundary">Content is data, with a legible boundary</a></h3> <p>The normaliser never acts on an instruction found in content, and never executes or renders a document. It preserves the content faithfully, since sanitising by deletion would lose fidelity and give false assurance, and it marks the content as data from an untrusted source in the provenance record, so the source map carries every span's origin and its untrusted status. This is the content-is-data posture probe four confirmed at the weak-agent bar.</p> <p><code>host-reference</code> cannot prevent the downstream agent from being injected; that rests with the consumer. What it provides is the primitive the OWASP guidance asks for: external content is segregated and clearly identified as untrusted, so the consuming agent can hold it as data and the operator can apply least privilege, where an agent does not hold a high-risk tool in the same turn it reads untrusted reference content. The skeleton may flag a span that matches an instruction pattern, as an advisory signal that never removes the span. The boundary is stated plainly so a consumer does not over-trust: <code>host-reference</code> makes the untrusted boundary legible and enforceable, and it does not promise the consumer's enforcement.</p> <h3 id="defensive-parsing-with-hard-bounds-and-no-reach-out"><a class="header" href="#defensive-parsing-with-hard-bounds-and-no-reach-out">Defensive parsing with hard bounds and no reach-out</a></h3> <p>Every parser runs inside hard resource bounds and a closed environment.</p> <ul> <li>No external entity resolution and no document-triggered network fetch, so no XXE and no server-side request forgery. External entities and remote document type definitions are off.</li> <li>A cap on entity expansion, on nesting depth, and on decompressed size with a compression-ratio limit, so the billion-laughs, quadratic-blowup, and decompression-bomb attacks meet a bound rather than exhaust memory.</li> <li>Validated archive entry names, so a zip-slip path cannot escape the contained extraction.</li> <li>No active content. Office macros are read and never run; vector-format scripts and event handlers are inert data and never executed.</li> <li>Memory-safe parsers preferred. Where a native library is unavoidable (some fixed-layout, image, and engineering parsers), it runs within the bounds and in a constrained subprocess, and every parser carries a fuzz corpus alongside its conformance fixture.</li> </ul> <h3 id="refusal-is-fail-safe-and-recorded"><a class="header" href="#refusal-is-fail-safe-and-recorded">Refusal is fail-safe and recorded</a></h3> <p>A parse that hits a bound or meets a hostile structure refuses cleanly and records the refusal in the manifest. It never emits a silent partial that a later read would mistake for the whole. This is the Bly rule applied to the parse: an omission makes a later read over-report the missing work rather than hide it.</p> <p>The immutable layer's attestation (<code>call/0018</code>) closes the loop. The corpus a consumer reads is byte for byte what was re-derived from the pinned source, so nothing is tampered with between ingest and use, and a changed source invalidates exactly its spans.</p> <h2 id="considered-options-10"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-10">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>Defensive parsing behind a legible untrusted boundary, with a fail-safe refusal (chosen).</strong> It hardens the host against the file-level classes and gives the consumer the segregation primitive the injection guidance requires, without pretending to solve the consumer's enforcement.</li> <li><strong>Sanitise content by stripping instruction-like text.</strong> Rejected: it loses fidelity and provenance (probe four rejected the same move at the agent surface), it cannot catch every payload, and a normaliser that silently edits content gives a false sense of safety.</li> <li><strong>Convert only, and leave all security to the consumer.</strong> Rejected: it abdicates the legible-boundary duty and leaves the host exposed to the parser-level classes. The tool that brings the material in must mark it untrusted and harden its own parsers.</li> <li><strong>Isolate every parse in a heavy virtual machine.</strong> Rejected as the default: it is disproportionate for the common case. The bounds, the closed environment, and the memory-safe parsers carry the bulk; a constrained subprocess is reserved for the unavoidable native parsers, which is defence in depth without the weight.</li> </ol> <h2 id="consequences-15"><a class="header" href="#consequences-15">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: the known file-level attack classes meet a bound with a fail-safe refusal; the untrusted boundary is legible and the consumer can enforce least privilege against it; provenance makes a poisoned input attributable and a changed source self-invalidating; no active content ever runs; the agent-facing posture is already weak-agent validated.</li> <li>Costs: the hardening steers the parser library choice toward memory-safe and sandboxable code and adds a bounds-and-fuzz surface to every normaliser; some unusual but legitimate documents will hit a bound and be refused, the deliberate price of refusing over a silent partial; and the boundary must be stated clearly and often, so a consumer does not read faithful normalisation as injection safety.</li> </ul> <h2 id="confirmation-9"><a class="header" href="#confirmation-9">Confirmation</a></h2> <p>The agent-facing half, that ingested content is data rather than instruction, passed at the weak-agent bar as probe four (<code>plan/0049/gather-data.md</code>, three of three, the model reaching it through the byte-for-byte integrity rule). The parser-hardening rules become per-normaliser conformance and fuzz tests in the build, with each bound exercised by a hostile fixture (an entity bomb, a zip-slip name, a decompression bomb, a macro-bearing document) that must refuse cleanly. The cast's fail-safe rules are folded in: Bly's recorded refusal over a silent partial, and Orin's plainly stated boundary of what the tool does and does not promise, so a cold reader does not over-trust.</p> <h2 id="lineage-and-sources-1"><a class="header" href="#lineage-and-sources-1">Lineage and sources</a></h2> <p>Indirect prompt injection and its mitigations (segregate and identify untrusted content, defence in depth, and least privilege so an agent does not hold a high-risk tool in the turn it reads untrusted content) are the first entry of the <a href="https://owasp.org/www-project-top-10-for-large-language-model-applications/assets/PDF/OWASP-Top-10-for-LLMs-v2025.pdf">OWASP Top Ten for language-model applications, 2025</a>. The parser-level classes and their defences are the <a href="https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/XML_External_Entity_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html">OWASP XML external entity prevention cheat sheet</a>, MITRE <a href="https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/776.html">CWE-776, the XML entity expansion weakness</a> behind the billion-laughs and quadratic-blowup attacks, and the <a href="https://security.snyk.io/research/zip-slip-vulnerability">Zip Slip archive-extraction path traversal</a>. A 2026 entity-expansion denial of service in a widely used XML parser (<a href="https://nvd.nist.gov/vuln/detail/CVE-2026-26278">CVE-2026-26278</a>, CWE-776) is the standing reminder that the bound is not optional.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="the-engineering-geometry-token-target-is-a-deterministic-structure-and-metadata-summary"><a class="header" href="#the-engineering-geometry-token-target-is-a-deterministic-structure-and-metadata-summary">The engineering-geometry token target is a deterministic structure-and-metadata summary</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-27</li> <li>Scope: the token target for the engineering-geometry modality of <code>host-reference</code> (<code>plan/0049</code>, <code>call/0030</code>). Instance software, binds no adopter, no spine change. Carved out of <code>call/0030</code>, which named this the one open design judgement.</li> <li>Relates: <code>call/0030</code> (the component and the immutable-against-overlay split); <code>call/0018</code> (re-derivation and attestation); <code>plan/0049/gather-data.md</code> (probe five part one, the deterministic boundary, passed; probe five part two, the weak agent's field elicitation, recorded as input here); <code>cast/</code> (Mara, Wren, Bly, Orin, the lenses this judgement was taken to).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-16"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-16">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>Geometry is the one modality with no document-like text to normalise. A STEP assembly, a mesh, a toolpath, or a parametric script is structure and numbers, so the question is what a deterministic, token-lean representation of it should hold, such that an agent can interpret the part or the assembly in context without the whole model.</p> <p><code>call/0030</code> settled two constraints that bound the answer. The target must be deterministic, since the immutable layer is re-derived and attested (<code>call/0018</code>); probe five part one confirmed at the weak-agent bar that a model-inferred description of a render is not deterministic and belongs in the overlay. And the target must be token-lean, since inlining a full boundary representation or a mesh is the token blow-up normalisation exists to avoid.</p> <p>There is adjacent 2026 work, but it solves the opposite problem: it tokenises a boundary representation to generate or round-trip CAD, which is large and lossless by design. Our target reads, it does not generate, so it summarises rather than tokenises the geometry. The technique worth borrowing from that work is the depth-first linearisation of a cross-referenced graph, which gives a deterministic, locality-preserving serialisation. The summary target itself has no settled answer, so <code>call/0030</code> routed it to the cast.</p> <h2 id="the-cast-judgement"><a class="header" href="#the-cast-judgement">The cast judgement</a></h2> <p>The weak agent's elicitation (probe five part two) proposed an assembly hierarchy, a critical-component list, interface topology, an assembly sequence, and constraint boundaries. The cast took that as input and tempered it.</p> <ul> <li>Mara asked what an agent actually needs from a twelve-hundred-part assembly: the structure to navigate by, the metadata that identifies a part, and the scale, so it can decide where to look closer, rather than the geometry itself. That argues for a lean summary keyed on the tree, with detail fetched on demand.</li> <li>Wren reads the tree as the index and each node's lean facts as the entry: the skeleton stays resident and the source map pulls a node's full detail when a task needs it. Inlining the geometry would break the context budget it must hold the whole problem in.</li> <li>Bly drew the reproducibility line. A fact parsed straight from the source (a name, the hierarchy, a unit, an instance count, a declared material, a tolerance annotation) is exactly reproducible. A fact a kernel computes (a bounding box, a volume, a surface area, a watertight flag) is reproducible only with a pinned kernel and a canonicalised fixed-precision value. So the parsed facts are safe in the attested layer; a computed fact enters it only when the pinned kernel yields a canonical value, and otherwise it is omitted, never left silently non-deterministic.</li> <li>Orin refused a frozen universal field list. Geometry has no settled answer and the useful fields vary by domain, so writing one list overfits the author's domain. The decision states the extraction principle and a sensible per-format default that a project extends. Orin also split the weak agent's list: the assembly hierarchy and any declared constraints are facts in the source, while a critical-component judgement, an inferred interface, and an assembly sequence are interpretations the source rarely declares, so they belong in the overlay rather than the attested layer.</li> </ul> <h2 id="decision-14"><a class="header" href="#decision-14">Decision</a></h2> <p>The engineering-geometry token target is a deterministic structure-and-metadata summary, keyed on the model tree.</p> <ul> <li>The tree is the spine. Parse the product or model hierarchy (the STEP product structure, the 3MF or AMF model tree, the OBJ groups, the OpenSCAD module tree, the DXF block and layer structure) into the navigable skeleton, serialised by depth-first linearisation so a cross-referenced graph becomes a deterministic, locality-preserving sequence. Each node carries its facts.</li> <li>Per-node facts split by reproducibility. Parsed facts (names, units, instance and topology counts, declared materials, the product-manufacturing and tolerance annotations, the DXF text and dimensions, the G-code header summary) are exactly reproducible and enter the attested layer. Kernel-computed facts (bounding box, volume, surface area, watertightness) enter it only as a canonical fixed-precision value from the pinned kernel, and are omitted when no such value can be produced.</li> <li>Interpretation goes to the overlay. A model-inferred label for an unnamed body, a criticality judgement, an inferred mating interface, or a reconstructed assembly sequence is recorded in the overlay with its provenance, never in the attested layer.</li> <li>The full geometry is tier one. The boundary representation, the mesh, or a rendered view is fetched on demand through the source map, so the summary never carries the whole model.</li> <li>The per-format field set is a default that a project declares and extends, rather than a frozen list.</li> </ul> <p>The defaults, each becoming a normaliser's conformance fixture:</p> <ul> <li>STEP: the assembly tree, part names, units, instance and topology counts, declared materials, the product-manufacturing annotations, and a canonical per-part bounding box.</li> <li>DXF: the layers and block definitions, the per-kind entity counts, every text and dimension value, and the units.</li> <li>STL and OBJ: object and group names, triangle and vertex counts, declared units, and the canonical bounding box, volume, surface area, and watertight flag.</li> <li>3MF and AMF: the model tree, object names, materials, build items, metadata, and units.</li> <li>G-code: the layer count, canonical print dimensions, the estimated time and filament and temperatures from the header, the slicer identity and settings, and the object count.</li> <li>OpenSCAD: the module and function tree, parameters with their defaults, and the include and use dependencies.</li> </ul> <h2 id="considered-options-11"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-11">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>A deterministic structure-and-metadata summary keyed on the tree, with a parsed-against-computed reproducibility split and extensible defaults (chosen).</strong> It is deterministic, token-lean, navigable, and honest about the domain variance.</li> <li><strong>A frozen universal field list.</strong> Rejected (Orin): geometry has no settled answer and the useful fields vary by domain, so a frozen list overfits one domain and underserves the rest.</li> <li><strong>A model render-and-describe as the primary target.</strong> Rejected (probe five part one, <code>call/0030</code>): a model reading is not deterministic, so it cannot be attested and belongs in the overlay; it also loses the structure an agent navigates by.</li> <li><strong>Tokenise the full boundary representation, after the 2026 generative work.</strong> Rejected: that target is built for generation and round-trip, so it is large and lossless, the token blow-up the summary avoids. The full model stays tier one, fetched on demand.</li> </ol> <h2 id="consequences-16"><a class="header" href="#consequences-16">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: the summary is deterministic and attestable; it is token-lean, since it carries the tree and facts rather than the geometry; it is navigable, since the tree is the skeleton and detail is fetched on demand; and it is honest about the unsettled domain through the extensible default. The parsed-against-computed split keeps the attested layer reproducible.</li> <li>Costs: the computed facts need a pinned geometry kernel and canonicalisation, a real engineering burden and a dependency-licence concern (<code>call/0030</code>); the extensible default means a domain may need to declare its own fields; and the geometry semantics an agent often wants, what a part is for, are available only through the overlay's model-inferred layer, which is not attested.</li> </ul> <h2 id="confirmation-10"><a class="header" href="#confirmation-10">Confirmation</a></h2> <p>This is a cast judgement, so the cast is its confirming body: the four lenses above shaped the decision, with the weak agent's elicitation (probe five part two) as input that was tempered rather than adopted whole. The deterministic boundary the decision rests on passed the weak-agent bar as probe five part one (<code>plan/0049/gather-data.md</code>). The per-format defaults become the geometry normalisers' conformance fixtures, each re-deriving byte for byte. The decision is revisited if a real adopter domain shows the default underserves it, the path the extensible field set leaves open.</p> <h2 id="lineage-and-sources-2"><a class="header" href="#lineage-and-sources-2">Lineage and sources</a></h2> <p>The adjacent 2026 work tokenises a boundary representation for generation and round-trip, a different goal from this reading summary: <a href="https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.12641">STEP-LLM</a>, whose depth-first reserialisation of the cross-referenced STEP graph this decision borrows for deterministic serialisation, and the <a href="https://arxiv.org/pdf/2601.16771">holistic boundary-representation tokenisation</a> whose split into geometry, position, and topology mirrors the structure, bounding-box, and counts decomposition here. The formats parsed are the published standards: STEP is <a href="https://www.loc.gov/preservation/digital/formats/fdd/fdd000448.shtml">ISO 10303-21:2016</a>, 3MF is the <a href="https://3mf.io/spec/">3MF Consortium specification</a> (now ISO/IEC 25422:2025), and the toolpath is the <a href="https://reprap.org/wiki/G-code">RepRap G-code reference</a> over the NIST RS274NGC base; DXF, STL, OBJ, AMF, and OpenSCAD follow their published format references.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="host-reference-is-pluggable-and-pure-rust-each-format-stands-up-by-feature-with-the-best-pure-rust-reader"><a class="header" href="#host-reference-is-pluggable-and-pure-rust-each-format-stands-up-by-feature-with-the-best-pure-rust-reader">host-reference is pluggable and pure-Rust: each format stands up by feature, with the best pure-Rust reader</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-27</li> <li>Scope: the reader architecture and the dependency-licence policy of <code>host-reference</code> (<code>plan/0049</code>, <code>call/0030</code>). Instance software, binds no adopter, no spine change. Refines the format-coverage and the recognition notes of <code>call/0030</code>.</li> <li>Relates: <code>call/0030</code> (the component and the immutable-against-overlay split); <code>call/0031</code> (untrusted input and the no-reach-out rule); the reproducible-build production anchor.</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-17"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-17">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>The format superset spans many readers, and some are heavy (the Office documents, PDF, recognition). Three constraints shape how they are brought in. The reproducible build is offline and pure-Rust, so a reader that links a C library (libtesseract through <code>leptess</code>, or PDFium) does not fit. A project rarely needs every format, so the weight of a reader it does not use should not be a cost it pays. And the dependency licences must stay clear of the strongest copyleft.</p> <p>A single comprehensive Office reader was evaluated, to avoid the per-format work. <code>litchi</code> reads the whole Office and OpenDocument family in pure Rust, which is attractive. It was deferred for two reasons. Its embedded objects and images are still in progress, so it cannot extract the images a slide deck carries, the capability this work needs. And it sits at version 0.0.1, with an API that by its own note may change without notice. It is worth revisiting once it stabilises and gains embedded-media extraction.</p> <h2 id="decision-15"><a class="header" href="#decision-15">Decision</a></h2> <ul> <li>Pluggable by feature. Each format or family is a normaliser crate, gated by a Cargo feature in the consumer. Only the enabled formats compile and register, so a project stands up the readers its corpus needs and pays for no others. A heavy reader is an opt-in cost.</li> <li>Pure-Rust, with no C dependency. Every reader builds in pure Rust, so the offline reproducible build and the no-reach-out rule of <code>call/0031</code> hold without a foreign toolchain. The C-linking readers are out.</li> <li>The best maintained pure-Rust reader per format, chosen on merit rather than weight, since the weight is opt-in. The current picks: <ul> <li><code>calamine</code> for spreadsheets, both xlsx and the legacy xls.</li> <li><code>htmd</code> for HTML.</li> <li><code>mail-parser</code> for internet mail, with <code>msg_parser</code> over <code>cfb</code> for Outlook <code>.msg</code>.</li> <li>the <code>zip</code> and <code>quick-xml</code> and <code>image</code> crates for the Office documents and their embedded media.</li> <li><code>lopdf</code> with <code>pdf-extract</code> for born-digital PDF.</li> <li><code>ocrs</code> over <code>rten</code> for optical character recognition.</li> </ul> </li> <li>Recognition stays in the overlay. A pure-Rust optical-character reader does exist, <code>ocrs</code> over the <code>rten</code> runtime, so recognition need not link C. It is a machine-learning reader, though: it carries model weights, vendored for the offline build, and its inference can vary across hosts at the float level. So the recognised content (the text inside an image, a description of it) lives in the overlay through the provider-agnostic adapter, recorded with its provenance, while the attested layer holds only what re-derives deterministically: the image metadata and its alt-text and the structure. A pinned model that proves cross-host deterministic could later promote the text into the attested layer. This refines the deterministic-recognition note of <code>call/0030</code>.</li> <li>The in-scope format set is the adopter's judgement, not ours. The methodology carries the superset of readers and the open plugin interface; the adopting project enables the subset its corpus needs and may author further readers on the interface. We build the menu and the interface and leave the scope to the project.</li> <li>Licences are per plugin, and copyleft is bounded. Each plugin's licence is the one its libraries carry, so a project reads a plugin's licence before it enables it. AGPL is never allowed, a hard rule. GPL is avoided where a permissive reader exists, and any GPL reader is expressly noted, so enabling its plugin is a knowing choice. A <code>cargo-deny</code> lane enforces the rules: it denies AGPL and surfaces GPL. The current readers are all permissive (MIT, Apache-2.0, or Unlicense), so the build carries no GPL and no AGPL today; <code>mail-parser</code> is Apache-2.0 or MIT, the parser crate rather than the AGPL Stalwart server.</li> <li>A GPL reader, were one ever the only option for a format, runs out-of-process behind an arms-length boundary (a pipe or a socket or a command line, the FSF aggregation position), so our plugin and the build stay permissive rather than taking on the copyleft. The boundary stays arms-length, not a shared-memory or tightly-coupled call that the FSF reads as equivalent to linking. Such a plugin is flagged GPL and is opt-in, and it carries a dependency on the external binary, which leaves the in-process pure-Rust model. LGPL may link directly; AGPL has no such escape and stays banned.</li> </ul> <h2 id="considered-options-12"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-12">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>Pluggable, pure-Rust, the best reader per format, permissive licences (chosen).</strong> A project pays only for what it stands up, the build stays offline and reproducible, a reader is chosen on merit, and the licence stays clear of copyleft.</li> <li><strong>One comprehensive Office reader, litchi.</strong> Rejected for now: it extracts no embedded media and carries a 0.0.1 unstable API. A revisit candidate once it matures.</li> <li><strong>A fixed, always-compiled reader set.</strong> Rejected: every project would pay the weight of every reader, the heavy ones included.</li> <li><strong>Allow a C-linking reader where it is best (libtesseract, PDFium).</strong> Rejected: it breaks the pure-Rust offline reproducible build and the no-reach-out rule.</li> <li><strong>Allow AGPL or GPL where convenient.</strong> Rejected: AGPL is banned outright, and GPL is avoided and flagged, so a plugin's copyleft is never a surprise to the project that enables it.</li> </ol> <h2 id="consequences-17"><a class="header" href="#consequences-17">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: a project pays only for the formats it enables; the build stays pure-Rust and reproducible; a reader is chosen on merit; recognition stays out of the attested layer; and the licence of each plugin is legible and copyleft-bounded.</li> <li>Costs: the feature matrix adds build-configuration surface; pptx image extraction is a scoped in-house walk (<code>zip</code> with the <code>image</code> crate), since no pure-Rust library extracts slide media today; optical character recognition is machine-learning, so its text is overlay-only until a pinned model proves deterministic; and a <code>cargo-deny</code> lane has to be carried and kept current.</li> </ul> <h2 id="confirmation-11"><a class="header" href="#confirmation-11">Confirmation</a></h2> <p>The normalisers already built (prose, structured data, HTML, vector, netlist) are retrofitted behind feature gates, the Office and mail and fixed-layout readers each land behind a feature, and a <code>cargo-deny</code> lane denies AGPL and surfaces GPL. The conformance fixtures and <code>software --check</code> stay green. The operator set the direction this session: pluggable, pure-Rust, the best reader per format, the adopter's judgement on the in-scope set, and copyleft bounded with AGPL banned.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="ocr-ships-now-as-the-first-out-of-process-plugin-attested-behind-an-arms-length-boundary"><a class="header" href="#ocr-ships-now-as-the-first-out-of-process-plugin-attested-behind-an-arms-length-boundary">OCR ships now as the first out-of-process plugin, attested behind an arms-length boundary</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: accepted</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-28</li> <li>Scope: the OCR reader of <code>host-reference</code> (<code>plan/0049</code>, <code>call/0030</code>, <code>call/0033</code>). Instance software, binds no adopter, no spine change. Refines the recognition-stays-in-overlay clause of <code>call/0033</code> and the recognition split of <code>call/0030</code>.</li> <li>Relates: <code>call/0030</code> (the immutable-against-overlay split and "a deterministic engine for the attested layer"); <code>call/0033</code> (pluggable pure-Rust readers, the out-of-process rule, the recognition-in-overlay clause, and the pinned-model promotion path); <code>call/0031</code> (untrusted input and the no-reach-out rule).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-18"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-18">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>The recognition-and-engineering wave was directed to deliver image and OCR together. <code>call/0033</code> had parked the recognised text of an image in the overlay until a pinned model proved cross-host deterministic, and it named the GPL OpenSCAD helper as the first use of the out-of-process rule. Two facts reshaped the choice for OCR.</p> <p>The only pure-Rust OCR engine, <code>ocrs</code> over the <code>rten</code> runtime, carries its model weights as a pair of <code>.rten</code> files licensed CC-BY-SA-4.0. That is a copyleft content licence. The <code>host-reference</code> component is otherwise permissive (Unlicense code, permissive dependencies), and it must not absorb a ShareAlike obligation into its own distribution as a derivative of the weights.</p> <p>The engine is also run-to-run deterministic on a host: the helper run twice over the same image with the same pinned models returns byte-identical text. So the recognised text of a fixed engine over fixed bytes behaves as a parse, not as the open-ended inference the overlay was built for.</p> <p>The out-of-process rule of <code>call/0033</code>, designed to keep the GPL OpenSCAD code out of the permissive build, fits the CC-BY-SA models exactly. A separate helper program carries the licence-encumbered engine and weights, and the permissive plugin runs it at arm's length.</p> <h2 id="decision-16"><a class="header" href="#decision-16">Decision</a></h2> <ul> <li>OCR ships now as an attested reader, rather than deferring to the overlay. The recognised text of a fixed engine over fixed bytes is the deterministic parse that <code>call/0030</code> placed in the attested layer, and the vendored pinned models are the pinned model that <code>call/0033</code> named as the promotion path.</li> <li>It is the first out-of-process plugin, ahead of OpenSCAD. The <code>host-reference-ocr-helper</code> binary embeds <code>ocrs</code>, <code>rten</code>, and the CC-BY-SA-4.0 ocrs models. The permissive <code>host-reference-ocr</code> plugin writes the image to a temporary file, runs the helper as a separate process, and reads the recognised text from its stdout. The two are an aggregation, not a linkage, so the plugin and its dependents stay permissive and the copyleft weights never enter their distribution as a derivative. This is the same arms-length boundary <code>call/0033</code> specified for GPL, applied to a content licence.</li> <li>The plugin implements the same <code>Normalizer</code> interface as every in-process reader, which proves the interface drives an out-of-process helper with no special case. That is the interface test <code>call/0033</code> wanted from the OpenSCAD route.</li> <li>The helper must be installed. The plugin finds it through <code>HOST_REFERENCE_OCR_HELPER</code> or the binary name on <code>PATH</code>, and returns a recorded refusal when it is absent, which is the cost the <code>call/0033</code> out-of-process consequence already named.</li> <li>Conformance is a byte-for-byte golden over a synthetic text image, the same contract the in-process readers carry. Cross-host bit-determinism is not yet proven, so the reproducible-build attestation of the recognised text is the open edge, carried by the verification ladder when the spec-and-release task lands.</li> </ul> <h2 id="consequences-18"><a class="header" href="#consequences-18">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>The CC-BY-SA-4.0 models are confined to the helper and attributed in its <code>NOTICE.md</code>. The licence watch-list in <code>readers.md</code> records the first content-copyleft dependency and its confinement behind the boundary.</li> <li>A consumer that enables the <code>ocr</code> feature must install the helper binary. The default text build pays nothing for OCR.</li> <li>OpenSCAD remains the planned GPL out-of-process plugin. OCR is the first instance of the pattern and validates it before OpenSCAD arrives.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="claudemd"><a class="header" href="#claudemd">CLAUDE.md</a></h1> <p>Behavioral guidelines to reduce common LLM coding mistakes. Merge with project-specific instructions as needed.</p> <p>Tradeoff: These guidelines bias toward caution over speed. For trivial tasks, use judgment.</p> <h2 id="0-project-overview"><a class="header" href="#0-project-overview">0. Project Overview</a></h2> <p>This repository is <code>agentic-host</code>; it develops the <code>host-*</code> <a href="STRUCTURE.html#components">components</a>. It holds planning documents (PLAN.md, milestone docs, MEMORY.md), mdBook site config, and Claude skills. The working codebases live under <code>software/<name>/main/</code>, materialized locally from <code>.host-software</code>.</p> <ul> <li>Software (the <em>Where</em> room): the <code>host-*</code> family, each a bare store with worktrees materialized under <code>software/<name>/main/</code> and pinned in <code>.host-software</code> (gitignored, materialized locally; the pin is the reproducibility anchor, plan/0028). The artifact-bearing components reproduce from a recorded toolchain: <code>host-lint</code> (a Rust CLI that detects phase-synonym agentic tells in commit messages, markdown headers, and code comments; VOCABULARY.md is its rule source), <code>host-lifecycle</code> (the generator, migrator, and lifecycle gate), and <code>host-prove</code> (the verification-ladder lane driver). <code>host-grammar</code> (the shared detection grammar and <code>.tla</code> spec home) is a repo-only source, consumed by the others as a git dependency.</li> <li>Submodule: <code>host-template/</code>, the scaffold template for <em>new</em> agentic projects; the verification-lane tool submodules <code>tools/{allium,specula}</code> stay external by source.</li> <li>Build/test a component inside its worktree: <code>cargo build</code>, <code>cargo test</code>, <code>./test-integration.sh</code>, <code>./lint-skill.sh</code> (for host-lint, inside <code>software/host-lint/main/</code>).</li> <li>Fresh-clone setup: the worktrees are absent until materialized, and <code>host-lifecycle</code> is itself one of the components it would materialize, so a fresh clone seeds it first: <code>cargo install --git https://github.com/connollydavid/host-lifecycle --rev <the host-lifecycle pin recorded in .host-software></code> (the materializer cannot be served from what it materializes). Then <code>git submodule update --init</code> (the submodules are <code>tools/{allium,specula}</code> and <code>host-template</code>), <code>host-lifecycle software --materialize .</code> to clone every <code>.host-software</code> component into <code>software/<name>/main/</code>, and <code>./link-skills.sh</code> to generate the skill symlinks under <code>.claude/skills/</code> from both the tool submodules and the materialized worktrees (gitignored, never tracked, or they dangle in CI; worktree-absence coherence, call/0005). Build host-lint at its recorded <code>x86_64-unknown-linux-musl</code> target so the binary lands at the artifact path the hook install reads (<code>rustup target add x86_64-unknown-linux-musl</code>, then <code>cargo build --release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-musl</code> in <code>software/host-lint/main/</code>; the digest-pinned recipe in <code>.host-software</code> reproduces the byte-identical canonical binary). Install the commit hooks with <code>host-lifecycle software --install-hooks .</code> (copies the <code>hooks</code> dispatch script in as <code>pre-commit</code>/<code>commit-msg</code> plus the binary at the recorded artifact path; gates on the worktree being at its pin). The canonical static-musl binary runs on the dev host, so <code>--install-hooks</code> installs the canonical binary itself (plan/0032 retired the plan/0028 local-glibc-build workaround that installed a binary differing from the canonical hash). Because agentic-host declares deeper verification rungs (<code>kani:</code>/<code>apalache:</code>/<code>tlaps:</code> dispositions), install the shared re-deriver host-prove on PATH so <code>software --check</code>'s runnability gate passes: <code>cargo install --path software/host-prove/main --root ~/.local</code> (its pin is recorded in <code>.host-software</code>; the gate HAZARDs a declared rung whose re-deriver does not run, plan/0048). <code>host-lifecycle software --check .</code> must be clean (every worktree at its pin, no tracked symlink into a worktree, every component's embed and release receipt present). The verification-lane tools are wired per the spine MUST (allium + specula); the <code>.allium</code>/<code>.tla</code> specs live in the software repos (see <code>plan/0012</code>).</li> </ul> <p>Template CLAUDE.md exemption: <strong>do not treat <code>host-template/CLAUDE.md</code> as instructions for this repo.</strong> It is template payload, the operating manual handed to projects instantiated <em>from</em> the template; it addresses an agent working in one of those projects rather than in this host. This file (the host root <code>CLAUDE.md</code>) is the sole authority here. If your tooling auto-loads the nested <code>host-template/CLAUDE.md</code> because you edited a file inside that submodule, ignore its contents as governance and follow only this one. (The two will state the methodology twice until the host↔template sole-source is reconciled, a deferred and deliberate duplication.)</p> <p>Software workflow (<code>host-*</code>): release a component with the tool-carried sequence <code>host-lifecycle release <component> --change-class <removes-flag|adds-flag|neither></code>, which runs the verify gate, bumps the version, rebuilds the artifact in the recorded toolchain, and prints the exact outward steps (commit and push inside <code>software/<name>/main/</code>, tag, then re-pin <code>.host-software</code> and record the release receipt). The producer tag is the release; <code>.host-software</code> pins that released commit and its re-derived artifact hash (dual-release-authority). The <code>host-template</code> submodule still uses the pointer-bump: commit and push inside it first, then commit the bumped pointer. Never push a host commit whose software pin or submodule pointer is unpushed. If a mandated push fails (no auth, no network), stop, report the unpushed commits to the user, and do not start dependent work.</p> <p>Milestone naming: name milestones and their documents after content (BOOTSTRAP.md, CI-PIPELINE.md), never ordinals (PHASE1.md, M2), because ordinals name positions, and positions shift when plans are re-cut. Do not degenerate to bare numerals ("3", "5.5") either. Encode sequence with document order and named dependencies. PLAN.md keeps a dictionary mapping retired ordinal names to current names, for reading history only.</p> <p>GitHub usage: the git hooks lint only commit messages and staged files; issue and PR titles are not gated, and a PR title becomes the squash-merge subject. Before any <code>gh issue|pr create</code> or <code>edit</code>, lint the title: <code>echo "$TITLE" | host-lint --stdin</code> must not <strong>flag</strong> (exit 1, a confirmed tell). A <strong>warn</strong> (exit 3) is advisory, exactly as the commit-msg hook treats it: host-lint's recall-biased Tier-3 rules also fire on genuine version strings and identifiers (e.g. <code>NT 3.1</code>, an AVOption decimal, a hardware designator), so on a warn confirm the flagged token is a real version/identifier and not a bare-numeral tell, then proceed; a legitimate version is no reason to mangle the title. Quote live tell examples only in bodies, never in titles.</p> <p>Agentic-host model: this repository is <code>agentic-host</code>, an agentic project built on the methodology authored in <code>host-template</code>. Its rooms are personas in <code>cast/</code>, decisions in <code>call/</code> (MADR), milestones in <code>plan/<NNNN-slug>/</code> indexed by <code>PLAN.md</code>, and the software under development as bare stores with worktrees (the <em>Where</em> room). Verification runs across the ladder's lanes (the <a href="STRUCTURE.html#verifiers">verifiers</a>); our own tooling is the <code>host-*</code> <a href="STRUCTURE.html#components">components</a>.</p> <p>Copy-at-version: the methodology spine (the four principles below, plus audited plans and append-only memory) is a copy held at the template revision recorded in <code>.host</code>; the template is the canonical, versioned source. To change the spine, change the template and apply the revision-keyed upgrade ledger (<code>host-template/UPGRADING.md</code>); do not fork the spine here in isolation. The nested <code>host-template/CLAUDE.md</code> is that source, not live governance for this repo (the exemption above).</p> <h2 id="1-think-before-coding"><a class="header" href="#1-think-before-coding">1. Think Before Coding</a></h2> <p>Do not assume. Do not hide confusion. Surface tradeoffs explicitly.</p> <p>Before writing any code, do the following:</p> <ul> <li>State your assumptions out loud in plain text. If you are not sure about something, stop and ask the user. Do not guess.</li> <li>If the user's request can be interpreted in more than one way, list all reasonable interpretations and ask which one they mean. Do not silently pick one.</li> <li>If a simpler approach exists than your first instinct, describe it. Push back on the request if a simpler solution is clearly better. Explain why.</li> <li>If any part of the request is unclear or ambiguous, stop immediately. Name the specific thing that is confusing. Ask a clarifying question before writing any code.</li> </ul> <p>The goal is: no surprises. The user should never see your output and say "that's not what I meant."</p> <h2 id="2-simplicity-first"><a class="header" href="#2-simplicity-first">2. Simplicity First</a></h2> <p>Write the minimum code that solves the stated problem. Nothing speculative. Nothing extra.</p> <p>Rules:</p> <ul> <li>Do not add features the user did not ask for. If the user says "add a login endpoint," do not also add a registration endpoint.</li> <li>Do not create abstractions (base classes, interfaces, factories, wrapper functions) for code that is used in exactly one place. Write the concrete thing directly.</li> <li>Do not add "flexibility" or "configurability" unless the user specifically requested it. Hardcode values if only one value is needed right now.</li> <li>Do not add error handling for scenarios that cannot occur given the current code and inputs.</li> <li>If your implementation is 200 lines and the same result can be achieved in 50 lines, rewrite it in 50 lines.</li> </ul> <p>Self-check: Read your finished code and ask "would a senior engineer say this is overcomplicated?" If the answer is yes, simplify before presenting it.</p> <h2 id="3-surgical-changes"><a class="header" href="#3-surgical-changes">3. Surgical Changes</a></h2> <p>When editing existing code, touch only what is necessary to fulfil the request. Clean up only your own mess.</p> <p>What NOT to do when editing existing code:</p> <ul> <li>Do not "improve" nearby code that is unrelated to the request. This applies to comments and variable names as much as to formatting and whitespace.</li> <li>Do not refactor working code that is not broken and not part of the request.</li> <li>Match the existing code style exactly, even if you would write it differently in a new project. If the file uses tabs, use tabs. If it uses snake_case, use snake_case.</li> <li>If you notice unrelated dead code or bugs, mention them in your response as a note to the user. Do not fix or delete them silently.</li> </ul> <p>What TO do when your changes create orphaned code:</p> <ul> <li>If YOUR changes made an import, variable, or function unused, remove that unused item in the same commit.</li> <li>Do not remove pre-existing dead code unless the user explicitly asks you to.</li> </ul> <p>Self-check: Look at every line you changed. Each changed line must trace directly back to something in the user's request. If a changed line does not connect to the request, revert it.</p> <h2 id="4-goal-driven-execution"><a class="header" href="#4-goal-driven-execution">4. Goal-Driven Execution</a></h2> <p>Transform every task into a concrete, verifiable goal. Then loop until the goal is verified.</p> <p>Examples of transforming vague tasks into verifiable goals:</p> <ul> <li>When the user says "add validation", your goal becomes: write tests for invalid inputs, then write code until those tests pass.</li> <li>When the user says "fix the bug", your goal becomes: write a test that reproduces the bug, then modify code until that test passes.</li> <li>When the user says "refactor X", your goal becomes: confirm all existing tests pass before refactoring, then confirm all existing tests still pass after refactoring.</li> </ul> <p>For any task with more than one step, state a brief numbered plan before starting. Each step must have a verification check:</p> <pre><code>[What you will do] → verify by: [how you will confirm it worked] [What you will do] → verify by: [how you will confirm it worked] [What you will do] → verify by: [how you will confirm it worked] </code></pre> <p>Strong success criteria (example: "test X passes") let you loop and self-correct without asking the user again. Weak success criteria (example: "make it work") force you to guess what "work" means. When success criteria are weak, ask the user to clarify before starting.</p> <h2 id="5-audited-planmd-and-milestone-docs"><a class="header" href="#5-audited-planmd-and-milestone-docs">5. Audited PLAN.md and milestone docs</a></h2> <p>All changes to PLAN.md and milestone docs MUST be committed and pushed immediately.</p> <p>Rules:</p> <ul> <li>Every edit to PLAN.md or any milestone doc (e.g. BOOTSTRAP.md, CI-PIPELINE.md) triggers a git commit and git push. Do not batch these with other changes.</li> <li>After completing a plan step in code, update the relevant plan file to reflect what was actually implemented, then commit and push that update as a separate commit.</li> <li>PLAN.md and milestone docs live in the host repo (top level or topic folders), never inside git submodules. Submodules contain the working codebase; planning documents are kept outside of them.</li> </ul> <h2 id="6-maintain-memorymd"><a class="header" href="#6-maintain-memorymd">6. Maintain MEMORY.md</a></h2> <p>MEMORY.md is a persistent scratchpad that records key decisions, discovered constraints, and lessons learned during the project. It exists so that context is not lost between sessions.</p> <p>Rules:</p> <ul> <li>After completing a significant task, resolving a non-obvious bug, or discovering an unexpected constraint, add a short entry to MEMORY.md. Each entry should be one to three sentences describing what happened and why it matters.</li> <li>Update MEMORY.md in a separate commit. Do not bundle MEMORY.md changes with code changes. Commit and push immediately, under the same rule as PLAN.md and milestone docs (see the audited-plans rule above).</li> <li>Do not wait until the end of a session to update MEMORY.md. Write entries as you go. If you are unsure whether something is worth recording, record it. Too many entries is better than a missing entry that causes repeated mistakes.</li> <li>MEMORY.md lives in the top-level repository alongside PLAN.md. Do not place it inside submodules.</li> <li>Do not delete or rewrite old entries. MEMORY.md is append-only. If an earlier entry turns out to be wrong, add a new entry that corrects it and references the old one.</li> <li>Append-only has exactly one sanctioned exception: a <strong>one-time, archive-first, map-only, recorded</strong> transformation, the document analog of a Deep history rewrite. It is permitted only when adopting a new naming convention during a methodology migration, and only when <strong>all</strong> of these hold: (1) the original is preserved verbatim (an archive file or a tagged commit) before any edit; (2) the change substitutes <em>only</em> the tokens named in a documented rename map, so every unmapped identifier (review/finding codes, version strings, software details) stays byte-for-byte, and the diff shows nothing but mapped substitutions; (3) a <code>call/</code> decision records the authorization, the map, and the archive pointer. It is never free-form (no rewording of substance, no "improving" historical entries; that destroys the epistemic trail the log exists to preserve) and never self-authorized by the agent. Absent all three conditions, append-only stands and corrections go in a new entry.</li> </ul> <p>The purpose of MEMORY.md is this: when a new session starts with no prior conversation context, a read of MEMORY.md should be enough to avoid past mistakes and to understand decisions that are not obvious from the code alone.</p> <h2 id="7-automatic-static-site-builds-for-self-documenting-work"><a class="header" href="#7-automatic-static-site-builds-for-self-documenting-work">7. Automatic Static Site Builds for Self-Documenting Work</a></h2> <p>All markdown documentation in the repository is automatically built into a static website using mdBook and published to GitHub Pages. This creates a living, browsable record of the project.</p> <p>Rules:</p> <ul> <li>A GitHub Actions workflow triggers on every push to the main branch. It builds all .md files (including PLAN.md, PHASEx.md, and any other documentation) into a static HTML site using mdBook.</li> <li>The mdBook configuration file (book.toml) and the SUMMARY.md file MUST be committed to the repo. SUMMARY.md defines the sidebar navigation and must be updated whenever a new document is added. book.toml lives in the repository root.</li> <li>The GitHub Actions workflow installs mdBook, runs <code>mdbook build</code>, and publishes the output directory to the gh-pages branch. GitHub Pages serves this branch automatically. Do not commit built HTML artifacts to the main branch.</li> <li>The published site is the single source of truth for project status. Anyone with access to the repository can read current plans, completed phases, and design decisions by visiting the GitHub Pages URL, with no local checkout required.</li> <li>When a new PHASEx.md file is created or a new document is added, add an entry to SUMMARY.md in the same commit. If SUMMARY.md is not updated, the new document will not appear in the site navigation.</li> </ul> <p>Style:</p> <ul> <li>The site must be clean and beautiful, in a minimalist way. Use generous whitespace and avoid clutter, decorative elements, and unnecessary UI chrome. The content is the interface.</li> <li>In book.toml, set <code>default-theme = "light"</code> and <code>preferred-dark-theme = "navy"</code>. Add a custom CSS file (committed to the repo) that includes a <code>@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)</code> block to automatically switch to the dark theme on page load. This way the site respects the reader's OS-level light/dark setting without manual toggling.</li> <li>Keep all CSS customisations under 50 lines. Limit changes to subtle refinements: tighter max-width, improved typography, muted colours. Do not override mdBook's built-in themes beyond this.</li> </ul> <hr /> <p>These guidelines are working correctly when you observe: fewer unnecessary changes appearing in git diffs, fewer rewrites caused by overcomplication, and clarifying questions happening before implementation rather than after mistakes are discovered.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="bootstrapmd"><a class="header" href="#bootstrapmd">BOOTSTRAP.md</a></h1> <h2 id="bootstrap-initial-setup"><a class="header" href="#bootstrap-initial-setup">Bootstrap: Initial Setup</a></h2> <h3 id="goals"><a class="header" href="#goals">Goals</a></h3> <ul> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> Establish agentic project directory structure</li> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> Add host-lint as git submodule</li> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> Define initial project scope and objectives</li> </ul> <h3 id="scope-1"><a class="header" href="#scope-1">Scope</a></h3> <ul> <li>Rust CLI binary (<code>host-lint</code>) that detects phase-synonym agentic tells in commit messages, markdown headers, and code comments</li> <li>Agent skill (<code>SKILL.md</code>) for callable linting</li> <li>Pre-commit hook wrapper for integration into git workflows</li> <li>Vocabulary reference (<code>VOCABULARY.md</code>) as source of truth for flag/allowlist/gray-zone terms</li> </ul> <h3 id="success-criteria"><a class="header" href="#success-criteria">Success Criteria</a></h3> <ul> <li>Host structure is committed and pushed</li> <li>Submodule is configured and accessible</li> <li>PLAN.md and this document are tracked and auditable</li> </ul> <h3 id="notes"><a class="header" href="#notes">Notes</a></h3> <p>Bootstrap establishes the scaffolding for agentic development. The software-under-development lives in the <code>host-lint</code> submodule.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="ci-pipelinemd"><a class="header" href="#ci-pipelinemd">CI-PIPELINE.md</a></h1> <h2 id="ci-pipeline"><a class="header" href="#ci-pipeline">CI Pipeline</a></h2> <h3 id="goals-1"><a class="header" href="#goals-1">Goals</a></h3> <ul> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> GitHub Actions workflow: static binary build, conformance gates, integration tests</li> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> lint-skill.sh: mechanical conformance gates G1-G8</li> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> Integration tests from VOCABULARY.md should/must-not-match cases</li> </ul> <h3 id="success-criteria-1"><a class="header" href="#success-criteria-1">Success Criteria</a></h3> <ul> <li>Workflow passes on push to main</li> <li>Static binary produced (musl, linux-amd64)</li> <li>All G1-G8 mechanical gates pass</li> <li>All VOCABULARY.md test cases pass (should match / must not match)</li> </ul> <h3 id="notes-1"><a class="header" href="#notes-1">Notes</a></h3> <p>Build runs in the host-lint submodule. Three jobs: build, conformance, test.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="formal-specmd"><a class="header" href="#formal-specmd">FORMAL-SPEC.md</a></h1> <h2 id="formal-specification--property-testing"><a class="header" href="#formal-specification--property-testing">Formal Specification & Property Testing</a></h2> <h3 id="goals-2"><a class="header" href="#goals-2">Goals</a></h3> <ul> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> Remove checked-in binary; add <code>.gitignore</code></li> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> Convert to Cargo project (required for proptest)</li> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> Write Allium spec (<code>.allium</code>) for host-lint detection behavior</li> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> Add proptest property-based tests</li> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> Update CI: cargo build, proptest test, GitHub release with binary artifact</li> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> Write README.md for host-lint</li> </ul> <h3 id="success-criteria-2"><a class="header" href="#success-criteria-2">Success Criteria</a></h3> <ul> <li>Binary is no longer committed; produced only by CI</li> <li>Allium spec validates with <code>allium</code> CLI</li> <li>Proptest suite passes with 100% coverage of VOCABULARY.md rules</li> <li>CI produces a GitHub release with static binary artifact on tag</li> <li>README.md documents project, usage, building, and testing</li> </ul> <h3 id="notes-2"><a class="header" href="#notes-2">Notes</a></h3> <p>Cargo conversion is required for proptest. Allium spec defines formal behavior. CI builds and tests the project, then publishes releases.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="skill-hardeningmd"><a class="header" href="#skill-hardeningmd">SKILL-HARDENING.md</a></h1> <h2 id="skill-hardening"><a class="header" href="#skill-hardening">Skill Hardening</a></h2> <h3 id="goals-3"><a class="header" href="#goals-3">Goals</a></h3> <ul> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> Fix pre-commit hook: dispatch on <code>$(basename "$0")</code> because git passes no hook name in <code>$1</code>; commit-msg receives the message file path (verified in throwaway repo)</li> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> Implement bare-numeral header rule (<code>## 3</code>, <code>## 5.5</code>) in src, proptest, Allium spec, integration tests (CI run 27243442903 green)</li> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> Verify CI-YAML/Dockerfile scoping exclusions are implemented (<code>is_ci_file</code> in src/lib.rs, applied in scan_file)</li> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> SKILL.md: install via GitHub release download; note skill-discovery location (<code>.claude/skills/</code>)</li> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> Bump CI actions off Node 20 via <code>FORCE_JAVASCRIPT_ACTIONS_TO_NODE24</code> (forced default on 2026-06-16)</li> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> Release architecture matrix: prebuilt binaries for linux/macos/windows, amd64 + arm64 (run 27243561995)</li> <li><input disabled="" type="checkbox" checked=""/> Cut v0.1.0 tag; release publishes all six matrix binaries (needed <code>permissions: contents: write</code> on the release job)</li> <li>Deferred: crates.io publish, postponed until the skill is 100% complete (note: crates.io hosts source only; prebuilds stay on GitHub Releases)</li> </ul> <h3 id="success-criteria-3"><a class="header" href="#success-criteria-3">Success Criteria</a></h3> <ul> <li>Hook installed as <code>pre-commit</code> or <code>commit-msg</code> blocks flagged commits and passes clean ones</li> <li>All VOCABULARY.md rules (including bare-numeral headers) covered by proptest and integration tests; CI green</li> <li>v0.1.0 release exists with a static linux-amd64 binary attached</li> <li>SKILL.md install instructions work without a local Rust toolchain</li> </ul> <h3 id="notes-3"><a class="header" href="#notes-3">Notes</a></h3> <p>Local cargo cannot build the project (2015 pre-nightly); all verification goes through CI. Depends on CI Pipeline being green (it is, since run 27242782567).</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="skill_authoringmd"><a class="header" href="#skill_authoringmd">SKILL_AUTHORING.md</a></h1> <p>A conformance standard for writing agent skills that run unmodified across Claude Code, Codex CLI, Gemini CLI, GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Cline, Windsurf, and OpenCode. The <code>SKILL.md</code> format is the shared substrate; this document defines the portable subset and the gates a skill must pass before it ships.</p> <p>Scope: authoring rules plus pass/fail gates. The gates are split into <strong>mechanical</strong> (a script can verify) and <strong>judgement</strong> (a human or agent reads and decides). A skill conforms when every mechanical gate passes and no judgement gate is failing.</p> <hr /> <h2 id="1-anatomy"><a class="header" href="#1-anatomy">1. Anatomy</a></h2> <pre><code>skill-name/ ├── SKILL.md # required; YAML frontmatter + markdown body ├── reference.md # optional; loaded on demand ├── examples.md # optional; loaded on demand └── scripts/ # optional; executed via bash, never read into context └── validate.py </code></pre> <p>Only <code>SKILL.md</code> is required. Everything else exists to keep <code>SKILL.md</code> short.</p> <h2 id="2-frontmatter"><a class="header" href="#2-frontmatter">2. Frontmatter</a></h2> <pre><code class="language-yaml">--- name: pdf-processing description: Extracts text and tables from PDFs, fills forms, merges documents. Use when working with PDF files or when the user mentions PDFs, forms, or extraction. --- </code></pre> <p><code>name</code> and <code>description</code> are the only fields guaranteed to be read by every agent. Anything else (Claude Code's <code>context</code>, Codex's <code>openai.yaml</code> keys, <code>allowed-tools</code>) is tool-specific and breaks the portability guarantee. Keep it out of a skill meant to be universal, or fence it so a missing parser degrades cleanly.</p> <p>The <code>description</code> is the entire discovery mechanism. It is the only text pre-loaded into the agent's context, so it is the sole basis for deciding whether the skill fires. Write it third person, as <em>what it does</em> plus <em>when to use it</em>, and pack it with the nouns a user would actually type. A vague description is the most common reason a correct skill never triggers.</p> <h2 id="3-body"><a class="header" href="#3-body">3. Body</a></h2> <p>Procedural knowledge: workflows, decision points, gotchas. Three loading tiers operate here: frontmatter is always resident, the <code>SKILL.md</code> body is read once the skill triggers, and supporting files are read only when the body points to them. Push depth outward:</p> <pre><code>## Editing documents For tracked changes see REDLINING.md For OOXML internals see OOXML.md </code></pre> <p>The linked file costs nothing until the task needs it. Scripts cost only their output, not their source, because they run under bash rather than being read, so deterministic logic belongs in a script, not in prose the agent has to re-derive each time.</p> <p>State rules with their reason. A naked imperative tells the agent the letter of a rule but not how to handle the case the rule never anticipated.</p> <pre><code>Use constructor injection. Field injection can't be mocked without a Spring context, so it breaks unit tests. </code></pre> <p>reads better and generalises further than <code>NEVER use field injection</code>. The reason becomes the rubric for the edge cases.</p> <p>For multi-step work, give an ordered checklist the agent copies into its response and ticks off. Where output quality matters, add a validation loop: run a check, fix what it flags, repeat until clean. The check can be a script or a reference doc the agent compares against.</p> <h2 id="4-portability-constraints"><a class="header" href="#4-portability-constraints">4. Portability constraints</a></h2> <ul> <li> <p>The hosted execution environment has no network and no runtime package installation. List required packages in the body and confirm they are present in the target environment. Stdlib-only scripts avoid the problem entirely and are the safest default for a skill distributed across agents.</p> </li> <li> <p>Reference every supporting file by relative path. Absolute paths and repo-specific layouts do not survive being copied into another agent's skills directory.</p> </li> <li> <p>When documenting file creation in the body, use a heredoc rather than a "create this file:" heading followed by a code block:</p> <pre><code>cat > config/settings.yaml << 'EOF' key: value EOF </code></pre> <p>The heredoc is unambiguous about target path and exact content; the heading form leaves the agent to infer both.</p> </li> </ul> <hr /> <h2 id="5-conformance-gates"><a class="header" href="#5-conformance-gates">5. Conformance gates</a></h2> <p>Each gate carries an ID and an assertion alongside its check and pass condition. Mechanical gates are phrased so a linter can evaluate them. Run mechanical gates first; they are cheap and catch the structural failures.</p> <h3 id="mechanical"><a class="header" href="#mechanical">Mechanical</a></h3> <p><strong>G1: Frontmatter present and parseable.</strong> Check: file begins with a <code>---</code> fenced block that parses as YAML. Pass: parse succeeds and yields a mapping.</p> <p><strong>G2: Required keys.</strong> Check: the mapping contains <code>name</code> and <code>description</code>, both non-empty strings. Pass: both present and non-empty.</p> <p><strong>G3: Name format.</strong> Check: <code>name</code> is lowercase, hyphen-separated, ≤ 64 chars, and equals the containing directory name. Pass: matches <code>^[a-z0-9]+(-[a-z0-9]+)*$</code>, length ≤ 64, equals dirname.</p> <p><strong>G4: Description length.</strong> Check: <code>description</code> length ≤ 1024 chars. Pass: within bound. (Empty or one-word descriptions also fail G9.)</p> <p><strong>G5: Portable frontmatter only.</strong> Check: frontmatter keys are a subset of <code>{name, description}</code> for a skill declaring itself universal. Extra keys are reported, not silently accepted. Pass: no keys outside the allowed set, OR each extra key is documented in a <code># Portability notes</code> section as a known tool-specific extension.</p> <p><strong>G6: Body length.</strong> Check: line count of the markdown body (everything after the closing <code>---</code>). Limit: ≤ 500 lines. Over it, content belongs in a reference file.</p> <p><strong>G7: References resolve.</strong> Check: every relative path and intra-repo link in the body points to a file that exists in the skill directory. Pass: zero dangling references.</p> <p><strong>G8: Imperative density.</strong> Check: count of standalone all-caps directives (<code>MUST</code>, <code>ALWAYS</code>, <code>NEVER</code>) in the body. Pass: each occurrence is accompanied within the same paragraph by a reason. A linter approximates this by flagging any such token whose sentence contains no "because", "so", "since", or equivalent causal marker, for human review.</p> <h3 id="judgement"><a class="header" href="#judgement">Judgement</a></h3> <p><strong>G9: Description triggers.</strong> Assertion: the description names the artifacts, file types, and verbs a user would actually say, not an abstract category. Fail signal: you cannot list three realistic user phrasings the description would match.</p> <p><strong>G10: Reason over rule.</strong> Assertion: rules in the body explain why, so the agent can generalise. Fail signal: imperatives that would leave the agent guessing on an unlisted edge case.</p> <p><strong>G11: Disclosure discipline.</strong> Assertion: detail used in a minority of runs lives in a reference file, not the body. Fail signal: the body is near the G6 limit because of content most tasks skip.</p> <p><strong>G12: Logic in scripts.</strong> Assertion: deterministic, repeatable procedures are scripts the agent executes, not prose it re-derives. Fail signal: the body walks through a fixed algorithm step by step in English.</p> <p><strong>G13: Real invocation.</strong> Assertion: the skill has been triggered on at least one genuine task end to end, not only read back. Fail signal: no record of the skill firing and producing correct output.</p> <p><strong>G14: Cross-agent dry run.</strong> Assertion: the skill has been dropped into at least one second agent's skills directory and triggered there. Fail signal: only ever exercised in the agent it was written for.</p> <hr /> <h2 id="6-ship-checklist"><a class="header" href="#6-ship-checklist">6. Ship checklist</a></h2> <p>A skill ships when G1 through G8 pass mechanically and G9 through G14 have no open fail signal. A future <code>lint-skill.sh</code> reads this file's mechanical section and asserts G1 through G8 directly; the judgement gates stay a human or agent review step.</p> <h2 id="7-sources"><a class="header" href="#7-sources">7. Sources</a></h2> <ul> <li>Anthropic, Skill authoring best practices: https://docs.claude.com/en/docs/agents-and-tools/agent-skills/best-practices</li> <li>Agent Skills format reference (portable subset): https://www.agensi.io/learn/skill-md-format-reference</li> <li>Open standard overview: agentskills.io</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="structure"><a class="header" href="#structure">Structure</a></h1> <p><code>agentic-host</code> is the methodology's meta repo <strong>and</strong> an agentic project instance. Its rooms:</p> <div class="table-wrapper"><table><thead><tr><th>W</th><th>room</th><th>holds</th></tr></thead><tbody> <tr><td>Who</td><td><code>cast/</code></td><td>personas</td></tr> <tr><td>What</td><td>with the software</td><td>specs (<code>.allium</code> / <code>.tla</code>) at the <a href="STRUCTURE.html#spec-home">spec-home</a>, co-located in each component's repo</td></tr> <tr><td>When</td><td><code>plan/</code></td><td>milestone index and folders (see <code>PLAN.md</code>)</td></tr> <tr><td>Where</td><td><code>software/<name>/main/</code></td><td>the <code>host-*</code> <a href="STRUCTURE.html#components">components</a> under development in the <a href="STRUCTURE.html#software-root">software-root</a>: bare stores with worktrees (<code>.host-software</code>)</td></tr> <tr><td>Why</td><td><code>call/</code></td><td>decisions about the software (MADR); <strong>instance-only; binds no adopter</strong>. Settled methodology decisions stay as immutable history, marked <code>Status: superseded by the spine</code></td></tr> <tr><td>How</td><td><code>CLAUDE.md</code> + tooling</td><td>this repo's manual and the <code>host-*</code> tools</td></tr> </tbody></table> </div> <p><strong>Adopter boundary.</strong> The normative methodology lives in <code>host-template/</code> (the versioned source you copy-at-version), not in this repo's top-level rooms. This repo's <code>call/</code> is its own Why room; do not read it as normative. See <code>README.md</code>.</p> <h2 id="concepts"><a class="header" href="#concepts">Concepts</a></h2> <p>The methodology concepts this project defines once and points at from everywhere else. Each has a stable <code>{#id}</code> anchor; other docs link to it rather than restate it.</p> <h3 id="components"><a class="header" href="#components">Components</a></h3> <p>The <code>host-*</code> components under development: host-lint, host-lifecycle, host-prove, host-grammar, host-reference. Each is a bare store with worktrees under <code>software/<name>/main/</code>, pinned in <code>.host-software</code>. The single-file <code>host</code> entrance is set apart (it is the <code>member</code> of the <code>[entrance]</code> stanza), not a component; agentic-host, the development environment, is not one either.</p> <h3 id="verifiers"><a class="header" href="#verifiers">Verifiers</a></h3> <p>The verification ladder's drivers, base to deep: host-lint (naming hygiene), allium (requirements and property-based testing), specula (bounded timing via TLA+), host-prove (the deeper symbolic, proof, and conformance rungs).</p> <h3 id="software-root"><a class="header" href="#software-root">Software-root</a></h3> <p>Where the software under development lives: <code>software/</code>.</p> <h3 id="spec-home"><a class="header" href="#spec-home">Spec-home</a></h3> <p>Where specifications live: with their software, co-located in each component's repo, never under <code>plan/</code>.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><h1 id="template-rewrite-rewrite-host-template-into-the-canonical-agentic-project"><a class="header" href="#template-rewrite-rewrite-host-template-into-the-canonical-agentic-project">TEMPLATE-REWRITE: rewrite host-template into the canonical agentic project</a></h1> <blockquote> <p><strong>Status: done (2026-06-14).</strong> Executed as template <code>0e5dd7f</code> to <code>59315bb</code> (rewrite <code>668b521</code> + host-lint pointer bump <code>59315bb</code>), host pointer bump <code>cddf673</code>. All steps below landed; all follow-up tidy-ups completed (see the closing note at the bottom).</p> </blockquote> <p>Plan for the parked "template content rewrite" work. The template scaffold exists (<code>tools/</code> submodules, <code>.claude/skills</code>, <code>cast</code>/<code>plan</code>/<code>call</code> seed, <code>STRUCTURE.md</code>); what remains is replacing the <strong>inherited Karpathy-guidelines skin</strong> with our own canonical methodology, retiring the inherited artifacts, and flipping the license to Unlicense. This is the coupled CLAUDE.md-rewrite leading to the Unlicense-flip.</p> <h2 id="anchors-heads-at-time-of-writing-2026-06-14"><a class="header" href="#anchors-heads-at-time-of-writing-2026-06-14">Anchors (heads at time of writing, 2026-06-14)</a></h2> <ul> <li>template <code>host-template</code> @ <code>0e5dd7f</code></li> <li>host-lint <code>c0b19f4</code> · host-grammar <code>342f507</code> (v0.1.1) · host-lifecycle <code>6dac2fc</code></li> <li>host <code>agentic-host</code> @ <code>7ad7e8e</code></li> </ul> <h2 id="inherited-artifacts-still-on-the-template-to-replaceretire"><a class="header" href="#inherited-artifacts-still-on-the-template-to-replaceretire">Inherited artifacts still on the template (to replace/retire)</a></h2> <ul> <li><code>README.md</code>, titled "Karpathy-Inspired Claude Code Guidelines"; has a plugin install section.</li> <li><code>CLAUDE.md</code>, the four Karpathy principles only (swept to say <code>host-lint</code>); no host methodology.</li> <li><code>skills/karpathy-guidelines/</code>, the inherited single skill.</li> <li><code>.claude-plugin/{plugin.json,marketplace.json}</code>, plugin/marketplace metadata.</li> <li><code>LICENSE</code>, MIT, © "Jiayuan Zhang and the andrej-karpathy-skills contributors".</li> </ul> <h2 id="steps"><a class="header" href="#steps">Steps</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>Retire inherited artifacts (mechanical).</strong> <code>git rm -r skills/karpathy-guidelines .claude-plugin</code>; remove the plugin-install section from <code>README.md</code>.</li> <li><strong>Rewrite <code>CLAUDE.md</code> as the canonical methodology</strong> in our own words (this is what makes the content ours and enables the Unlicense flip). <strong>Tight framing (D1): write for weaker LLMs and lesser harnesses; crisp, explicit, directive; clarity over cleverness; never rely on a strong model to infer intent.</strong> Outline: <ul> <li>What an agentic project is: host = externalized <em>thought</em>, software = <em>action</em>; the five-W rooms (<code>cast</code>/<code>spec</code>/<code>plan</code>/Where-slot/<code>call</code>/tools).</li> <li>The four working principles (think-before-coding, simplicity-first, surgical-changes, goal-driven-execution), Karpathy-inspired, rewritten, credited in Provenance.</li> <li>The three verification lanes: host-lint (hygiene), allium (requirements + property-based testing), Specula (timing/concurrency via TLA+), plus reference-not-vendor and instruct-not-patch.</li> <li>The <code>host-*</code> tools: host-grammar (rules), host-lint (checker), host-lifecycle (token-free generator/validator); generator and checker share host-grammar.</li> <li>Names/numbers/milestones (<code>plan/</code>), decisions (<code>call/</code>, MADR from the <code>0000</code> bootstrap), specs (<code>spec/</code> <code>.allium</code>/<code>.tla</code>), personas (<code>cast/</code>, XP-Persona).</li> <li>Audited plans + append-only MEMORY discipline.</li> <li>Provenance: Karpathy's observations via Jiayuan Zhang (@forrestchang); personas via Powell, Keenan & McDaid (2007).</li> </ul> </li> <li><strong>Reframe <code>README.md</code></strong> as "the host template": what it is, how to instantiate (clone, then <code>git submodule update --init</code>, then replace <code>cast/</code> examples, then add the software submodule), Provenance, Unlicense.</li> <li><strong>Flip <code>LICENSE</code> from MIT to Unlicense</strong> (the content is then ours; Provenance carries the credit, not an obligation).</li> <li><strong>Commit + push</strong> the template.</li> </ol> <h2 id="decisions-resolved"><a class="header" href="#decisions-resolved">Decisions (resolved)</a></h2> <ul> <li><strong>D1: CLAUDE.md scope, full cut, tightly framed.</strong> Write the full methodology (the rewrite below), but for <strong>weaker LLMs and lesser harnesses</strong>: crisp, explicit, directive framing; clarity over cleverness; never rely on a strong model to infer intent. The doc must be followable by a weak agent; that is the success criterion.</li> <li><strong>D2: Host and template sole-source, defer liberally.</strong> The host builds the template whose methodology the host itself follows, a self-hosting bootstrap (a C compiler compiling a C compiler). Defer the host-sync; reconcile later. This pass touches only the template's identity.</li> </ul> <h2 id="follow-up-tidy-ups-separate-not-blocking-the-rewrite-all-done"><a class="header" href="#follow-up-tidy-ups-separate-not-blocking-the-rewrite-all-done">Follow-up tidy-ups (separate, not blocking the rewrite); all done</a></h2> <ul> <li><strong>Done.</strong> Bumped <code>tools/host-lint</code> pointers (template + host) <code>a9a7cd3</code> to <code>c0b19f4</code>. Verified <code>c0b19f4</code> is remote <code>main</code> head and the child of <code>a9a7cd3</code>, so the bump is forward (the <code>git describe</code> <code>v0.1.2-2-ga9a7cd3</code> had made the direction look ambiguous).</li> <li><strong>Done.</strong> Refreshed the host's installed git hooks: renamed the binary <code>.git/hooks/no-phase</code> to <code>host-lint</code> and reinstalled the canonical <code>pre-commit</code> script (which references <code>$SCRIPT_DIR/host-lint</code>) as both <code>pre-commit</code> and <code>commit-msg</code>. Verified: blocks a tell, passes a clean message, passes a Co-Authored-By trailer. (Local, per-clone, not committed.)</li> <li><strong>Done.</strong> Closed host issue #1 with a resolution comment: superseded by the cast/spec/plan/call + three-lane design; the "don't pad" departure it proposed was settled the other way (we zero-pad via host-grammar).</li> </ul> <h2 id="d2-deferred-host-and-template-sole-source"><a class="header" href="#d2-deferred-host-and-template-sole-source">D2 (deferred): host and template sole-source</a></h2> <p>Still deferred per the decision above: the host's own <code>CLAUDE.md</code> and the template's <code>CLAUDE.md</code> now state the methodology twice. Reconcile later (the self-hosting bootstrap).
