The real defect is narrower: <code>host-lifecycle book</code> <strong>reserved</strong> <code>docs/</code> as its generated output and wiped it each run (<code>fs::remove_dir_all</code>), which is correct-by-contract for a greenfield host (the methodology gitignores <code>docs/</code>), but a <strong>migration gap</strong> — <code>classify</code>/<code>adopt</code> never inspect a pre-existing tracked <code>docs/</code>, so a project migrating in with hand-written docs there loses them. <strong>Operator reframed it as a location decision</strong> (not a <code>book</code> bug, not a classify-guard): move the generated trees off <code>docs/</code> and the footgun dissolves.</p> <p><strong>Fix:</strong> <code>host-lifecycle book</code> now writes the generated mdBook source to <code>mdBook/src/</code> and the built HTML to <code>mdBook/out/</code>, with <code>book.toml</code> kept at the repo root, so <code>mdbook build</code> still runs from the root (<code>book_toml</code> sets <code>src = "mdBook/src"</code> + <code>[build] build-dir = "mdBook/out"</code>). <code>docs/</code> is freed. One gitignored <code>mdBook/</code> entry replaces today's three (<code>book.toml</code> stays, <code>/docs/</code> + <code>/book/</code> → <code>/mdBook/</code>). Released <strong>host-lifecycle v0.30.2</strong> (<code>f2b4607</code>, artifact <code>3a9e59ce</code>, on vendor-v2 — no host-lint dep change, bundle still valid). A regression test (<code>write_book_targets_mdbook_dir_and_ leaves_docs_intact</code>) pins it.</p> <p><strong>This was decided with data + the cast (the user's process, run in full):</strong></p> <ul> <li><strong>Fen (real qwen3.5-4b via rope, true system prompt) is reachable from env now.</strong> The token lives in <code>$ROPE_TOKEN</code> (exported from <code>~/.profile</code>, added by the user); the harness snapshots env at session start, so <code>set -a; . ~/.profile; set +a</code> reloads it. Read it from env, never ask. Use <code>enable_thinking:false</code> via <code>chat_template_kwargs</code> to suppress the always-on <code><think></code> (the <code>/no_think</code> directive is NOT honored by this server, and the non-thinking <em>sampler</em> alone does not disable it). See [[qwen-pal-model-infra]].</li> <li><strong>First probe (mdBook idiom) picked <code>src/</code> 4/5; a fair tie-break that also stated the ensemble conventions flipped to a consolidated <code>book/</code> 3-2 (4-1 by reasoning).</strong> The reconciler was the fact that mdBook's <code>src</code>/<code>build-dir</code> are configurable (we already set <code>src = "docs"</code>), so a custom path is NOT "fighting mdBook." Acceptance test: the 4B judged <code>docs/</code> safe under the new layout <strong>5/5</strong> and reproduced the footgun on the old layout <strong>3/3</strong> — the weak agent perceives the fix.</li> <li><strong>The cast (cast/*.md — Mara/Wren/Bly/Orin/Fen) confirmed the layout and set 4 shipping requirements</strong> the naive fix would miss: the adopter <code>UPGRADING</code> entry must be <strong>independent</strong> (Bly), <strong>fail-safe</strong> cleanup that re-lists if skipped (Bly), <strong>tool-carried</strong> no hand-edit (Fen), and <strong>version-gated</strong> <code>requires</code> the new binary (Orin).</li> <li>Operator chose the folder name <strong><code>mdBook/</code></strong> (brand casing) over <code>book/</code>/<code>mdbook/</code>.</li> </ul> <p><strong>Spine + adoption (host-template <code>e068828</code>):</strong> STRUCTURE.md doctrine + <code>.gitignore</code> (<code>/mdBook/</code>) + an <code>UPGRADING</code> entry keyed <code>e068828</code>, <code>independent = true</code>, <code>requires host-lifecycle v0.30.2</code>, <code>verify = grep -rqs "mdBook/src" host-template/STRUCTURE.md</code>. <code>call/0014</code> is already superseded-by-spine, so no new agentic-host <code>call/</code>. agentic-host adopted it with <code>upgrade --record e068828</code> (recorded out-of-order against baseline <code>de8a517</code>, the fail-safe partial model). <strong>Gotcha: the UPGRADING rev is a self-referential label</strong> — you cannot key an entry to the SHA of the commit that contains it. <code>git commit --amend</code> changes the SHA and orphans the key; the clean move is to commit the entry, then re-key it to the pushed revision in a tiny follow-up commit (the rev is a label, ordered by file position, ancestry not checked).</p> <p><strong>CI:</strong> mdbook.yml <code>publish_dir</code> → <code>./mdBook/out</code> + the generate comment, paired with the host-lifecycle rev bump to v0.30.2 (the publish path and the rev are coupled — the new binary writes mdBook/out; change them together or the Site publishes an empty dir). reproducible-build.yml rev bumped too. plan/0046 dogfooded as 5 anchored receipted tasks. <code>software --check</code> clean; the local <code>host-lifecycle book .</code> + <code>mdbook build</code> produced <code>mdBook/out</code>. host-lifecycle#3 closed.</p> <h2 id="2026-06-26--plan0047-complete-the-prose-lane-audits-the-authored-working-tree-host-lint17"><a class="header" href="#2026-06-26--plan0047-complete-the-prose-lane-audits-the-authored-working-tree-host-lint17">2026-06-26 — plan/0047 COMPLETE: the prose lane audits the authored working tree (host-lint#17)</a></h2> <p><code>connollydavid/host-lint#17</code> closed. The shared <code>run_docs</code> (host-lint lib, <code>--docs</code> + the <code>host-lifecycle prose</code> recheck) walked <code>git ls-files</code> (tracked and staged only), so a <strong>brand-new authored doc that was not yet staged was silently skipped</strong> — a pre-commit <code>prose</code> read clean, then the verify recheck HAZARDed the same doc once committed, with no content change. <strong>Unlike #3 the description was accurate</strong>: I'd hit this skip myself (had to <code>git add</code> plan READMEs before <code>host-lifecycle prose</code> saw them), and the auto-memory already documented it.</p> <p><strong>Reframing (the user was unsure).</strong> The skip is <em>partly intentional</em> — <code>git ls-files</code> is exactly what excludes generated/vendored/gitignored/worktree content — and <strong>staging closes the gap</strong> (<code>git add</code> puts the file in the index, which <code>ls-files</code> lists). So the window is only create-but-unstaged, with a documented workaround. What made it a real defect was the <strong>cast</strong>: a <code>prose: clean</code> that silently skipped a file is Bly's "overstates completeness," Fen's silent trap (clean pre-commit → HAZARD post-commit), and Orin's "fails unsafe when followed literally." Operator chose fix (a).</p> <p><strong>Fix:</strong> <code>run_docs</code> now walks <code>git ls-files</code> <strong>plus</strong> <code>git ls-files --others --exclude-standard</code> (untracked files git would offer to add). The two sets are disjoint (in-index vs not), so no dedup. <code>--exclude-standard</code> keeps gitignored output, vendored deps, and un-materialized worktrees out, so a <strong>fresh CI checkout is unchanged</strong> (no untracked authored files there) — only a local pre-commit / working-tree run gains the coverage. Released <strong>host-lint v0.10.4</strong> (<code>ce683be</code>, <code>a9ef0865</code>) + <strong>host-lifecycle v0.30.3</strong> (<code>0de2843</code>, <code>cf59cc16</code>, on <strong>vendor-v3</strong> <code>817a71d9</code>).</p> <p><strong>Validation (the user asked for careful validation):</strong> unit test pins tracked-scanned / untracked-scanned / gitignored-excluded; CLI repro confirmed (untracked authored caught, generated excluded); on agentic-host <code>git ls-files --others --exclude-standard</code> finds no <code>.md</code>, so the verify recheck is unaffected. <strong>Fen acceptance (real qwen3.5-4b via rope): new walk catches a new unstaged doc 5/5, old walk misses it 3/3</strong> (the 2 "unparsed" were <code>**Q: YES**</code> — markdown-bold broke the grep, so a true 5/5). <strong>No spine change</strong> — the walk scope is a host-lint implementation detail, NOT spine doctrine (the spine never states the mechanism), so zero adopter <code>UPGRADING</code> churn.</p> <p><strong>Process notes:</strong></p> <ul> <li><strong>Cascade is the plan/0045 shape</strong> (shared <code>run_docs</code> → host-lint release → host-lifecycle dep bump → vendor-v3 → host-lifecycle release → re-pin). <strong>The kani digests were re-derived BEFORE tagging this time</strong> (the plan/0045 lesson applied first try: <code>git hash-object src/lib.rs</code> → <code>cc31f540</code> into <code>host-lint.obligations.digests</code>, committed with the release), so the <strong>v0.10.4 tag is green</strong> — no born-red detour. And <code>rm -rf vendor vendor-config.toml</code> after producing the bundle (no vendor-leftover false-HAZARD).</li> <li><strong>Only agentic-host's own CI pins</strong> (mdbook.yml + reproducible-build.yml) bumped to v0.30.3; the <strong>host-template + host prose CIs stay at v0.30.1</strong> on purpose — #17 changes only local-uncommitted behavior, and their CI runs on a fresh checkout (no untracked authored files), so their verdict is identical. Bumping them would cascade two more repos for zero behavioral change.</li> <li><strong>New behavior to remember:</strong> <code>host-lifecycle prose</code> / <code>--docs</code> now scans untracked-non-ignored <code>.md</code>, so a working-tree draft is audited before it is staged. Keep new docs prose-clean from the moment they exist (gitignore a genuine scratch file). The <code>verify</code> recheck in <code>software --check</code> picks this up too.</li> </ul> <h2 id="plan0048--a-declared-rungs-re-deriver-must-be-runnable-corrects-the-wsl-enoent-misdiagnosis"><a class="header" href="#plan0048--a-declared-rungs-re-deriver-must-be-runnable-corrects-the-wsl-enoent-misdiagnosis">plan/0048 — a declared rung's re-deriver must be runnable; CORRECTS the WSL-ENOENT misdiagnosis</a></h2> <p><strong>The misdiagnosis (lines 667, 669, 1264 are WRONG and superseded by this entry).</strong> Those entries say <code>host-prove</code>'s local spawn of <code>cargo kani</code> fails with <code>ENOENT</code> on the <code>/mnt/c</code> WSL mount, a host-prove-invocation gap blamed on the filesystem, repeated three times across two weeks. <strong>That is false.</strong> The re-derivation runs fine on <code>/mnt/c</code>. The real causes were two: (1) <strong>host-prove was never installed on PATH</strong> by the local setup — host-lint's CI installs it, but the fresh-clone discipline never did, so <code>host-prove</code> was <code>command not found</code> and the spawn error read as a filesystem <code>ENOENT</code>; and (2) the <code>obligations --rederive</code> invocation handed host-prove <code>src/</code> rather than the <strong>crate root</strong> (host-prove's <code>run_kani</code> does <code>current_dir(dir)</code>, so <code>--dir</code> must be the dir holding <code>Cargo.toml</code>). Once host-prove is installed (<code>cargo install --path software/host-prove/main --root ~/.local --force</code>) and pointed at the crate root, <code>host-prove kani --harness ... --dir software/host-lint/main</code> → <code>SUCCESSFUL</code>, and <code>host-lifecycle obligations host-lint.allium --tests tests --rederive .</code> re-derives <strong>both rungs, 50 dispositioned, clean, on <code>/mnt/c</code></strong>.</p> <p><strong>Why it hid for two weeks (call/0018, turned on the re-deriver itself).</strong> The kani proof is verified by a <em>second</em> path — CI runs <code>cargo kani</code> directly and re-derives there — so the local re-derivation's brokenness was invisible, and a <code>command not found</code> reads as a filesystem fault, so it was filed as a WSL problem and never re-opened. The re-deriver was referenced, materialized, and its CI lane present, yet <strong>not runnable on the box that needed it</strong>: available but never discharged, the very <code>call/0018</code> distinction (AVAILABLE ≠ DISCHARGED).</p> <p><strong>The migration (not a tactical fix to this one host).</strong> Generalized the spine invariant "declare a deeper rung, oblige its lane" into "oblige a lane that is <strong>runnable</strong>, not merely present in CI config." <code>software --check</code> (host-lifecycle <strong>v0.31.0</strong>, pin <code>3958b62</code>, artifact <code>fe428240</code>) now probes that a declaring component's re-deriver (the shared driver host-prove) <strong>executes</strong> — a cheap <code>--help</code> probe, never the proof — and HAZARDs when it cannot, beside the existing no-CI-lane HAZARD. Narrowed to host-prove only (NOT the specific verifier): TLAPS is CI-only by design (plan/0023) and the apalache JVM is optional locally. Decoupled into its own <code>tier_rederiver_problems</code> (the probe inside <code>spec_lane_problems</code> broke <code>tier_lanes_are_opt_in_and_inert</code>'s exact-count asserts; that fn stays pure/portable). 105 tests green with host-prove hidden; the gate HAZARDs correctly when it is.</p> <p><strong>Earned digest, not hand-edited (the born-red root).</strong> The recurring born-red was the <em>hand-recorded</em> digest standing in for a proof that no longer ran. The digest a rung records is now <strong>earned</strong> only by <code>obligations --rederive --record-digests</code> (records on a pass), never a hand edit, so the cheap offline staleness check soundly means a passing re-derivation on the current code. Validated with Fen (real qwen3.5-4b, neutral framing after discarding a leading round): a PATH probe alone is insufficient (six of eight see on-PATH ≠ works), a recorded pass on the current code is best (eight of eight); the 4B reconstructed call/0018 from first principles.</p> <p><strong>Spine + ledger.</strong> <code>host-template/CLAUDE.md</code> doctrine widened (verify phrase <strong>"a re-deriver that runs"</strong>); one independent version-gated <code>UPGRADING</code> entry keyed <code>6174996</code> (<code>requires = host-lifecycle v0.31.0</code>), pushed at host-template <code>4182df9</code>. agentic-host advanced its host-template pointer so the entry reads <strong>PENDING</strong> here; <strong>adoption is deferred to the operator trigger</strong> "Read and follow https://github.com/connollydavid/host to keep this repository an agentic project" — which records the entry, installs host-prove in the fresh-clone setup and the CI that runs <code>software --check</code>, and bumps the CI host-lifecycle pins. Until then host-prove is installed locally only, so local <code>software --check</code> is green while the ledger entry stays unrecorded.</p> <p><strong>Adopted (the operator triggered it).</strong> Followed the <code>host</code> front-door as a case-(c) upgrade. The host-template pointer was already at the target <code>4182df9</code>, so the work was: <code>upgrade --record</code>, install host-prove where the gate runs, and bump the CI pins. agentic-host now installs host-prove in the fresh-clone setup (<code>cargo install --path software/host-prove/main --root ~/.local</code>, CLAUDE.md) and in the reproducible-build CI before <code>software --check</code> (pin <code>3d1bba79</code>), and both CI host-lifecycle pins (mdbook.yml + reproducible-build.yml) are at v0.31.0 (<code>3958b62</code>). <code>software --check</code> reports the re-deriver runnable for both declaring components; whole-suite CI green on <code>bcf3d75</code>.</p> <p><strong>GOTCHA for a future adoption: an all-digit <code>UPGRADING</code> key collides with the ordinal parser.</strong> <code>host-lifecycle upgrade --record <id></code> takes "an unambiguous prefix or a ledger ordinal," and it tries the ordinal parse first. The entry key <code>6174996</code> is a git-SHA prefix that happens to be all decimal digits, so <code>--record 6174996</code> was read as ordinal 6174996 and failed "out of range (1..=37)." Record it by its <strong>ledger ordinal</strong> instead (it was the last entry, position 37: <code>upgrade --record 37 .</code>). A key with any hex letter (a-f) would not collide. The applied claim lands in <code>.host-receipts</code> as <code>applied = 6174996 recorded=... via=verify</code> (an out-of-order applied entry, not a baseline advance), and the <code>.host</code> baseline stays <code>de8a517</code>.</p> <p><strong>GOTCHA: marking a milestone "complete" must not land in a commit before its task receipts.</strong> The task gate (<code>software --check</code>, run by the reproducible-build CI) HAZARDs <code>task <key> — no receipt (its milestone is marked complete)</code> and exits 1 when a plan README Status reads complete but a task in that milestone has no receipt yet. plan/0048 hit it: commit <code>8d1dad7</code> flipped the README to complete, but the <code>#adopt</code> receipt was committed in the next commit (<code>9ec9087</code>), so <code>8d1dad7</code>'s CI went red on the verify-build job (the commits on either side passed). The branch self-heals at the next commit (HEAD stayed green), but a red run sits in history. Fix the ordering: commit the backing receipts in the same commit as the status flip or earlier, never after. This refines [[complete-means-whole-suite-green]] to intra-sequence commit ordering, not just cross-repo sweeps. Re-running the red intermediate commit is pointless; its tree is genuinely inconsistent.</p> <h2 id="plan0049--external-reference-corpus-cut-host-reference-agent-surfaces-pass-at-the-fen-bar"><a class="header" href="#plan0049--external-reference-corpus-cut-host-reference-agent-surfaces-pass-at-the-fen-bar">plan/0049 — external reference corpus cut; host-reference agent surfaces pass at the Fen bar</a></h2> <p>plan/0049 cut (in design): a new component <code>host-reference</code>, a reference compiler that normalises external documentation (mixed shapes: Markdown, HTML, PDF, Office, images, structured data, electronic design, mechanical and 3D-printing CAD) into a token-lean form an agent reads in context, with a deterministic immutable layer (re-derived, attested) and a collaborative overlay for annotations and edits. Design direction and the open questions are in plan/0049/README.md; the adversarially reviewed weak-agent probe set, the cast review (pre and post), and the run are in plan/0049/gather-data.md. The four agent-facing surfaces (windowed retrieval selector, capability flags, content-is-data posture, the deterministic immutable-against-overlay boundary) passed three of three each against <code>qwen3.5-4b</code> on rope, with the option order rotated so the pass reflects content reasoning and not position. Decisions follow in call/0030, with a security sibling and the engineering-geometry target left to the cast.</p> <p><strong>GOTCHA for a future Fen probe run: do not put a multiple-choice instruction in a shared system prompt when an open elicitation probe is in the same batch.</strong> The system prompt said to answer the multiple-choice question; the open geometry-elicitation probe spent its whole token budget reconciling that mismatch and truncated before answering. Run an open elicitation with its own open-ended system prompt, or split the batch by response shape.</p> <p><strong>GOTCHA: give the thinking model room.</strong> <code>qwen3.5-4b</code> thinks before it answers, so <code>max_tokens</code> 1500 truncated mid-think and the answer never emitted; 8000 finished cleanly. Force the answer onto a parseable final line (<code>ANSWER: X</code>) and rotate the option order across runs, so a pass is content reasoning rather than a position or string-match artefact. See [[qwen-4b-weak-agent-eval]] and [[weak-agent-probe-no-false-premise]].</p> <h2 id="plan0049--host-reference-embedded-source-only-prose-density-check-needs-host-lifecycle-prose"><a class="header" href="#plan0049--host-reference-embedded-source-only-prose-density-check-needs-host-lifecycle-prose">plan/0049 — host-reference embedded source-only; prose density check needs <code>host-lifecycle prose</code></a></h2> <p>The host-reference component was scaffolded (a Cargo workspace, <code>host-reference-core</code> carrying the <code>Normalizer</code> trait and the two-layer types, plus a CLI skeleton), pushed to the new public repo connollydavid/host-reference at <code>88c6bcf</code>, and embedded source-only in <code>.host-software</code> (url, pin, worktrees, no build provenance). The reproducible-build artifact, the deps-bundle, and <code>--verify-build</code> land in the plan/0049 build wave once the CLI is functional; the release receipt is a deliberate <code>skip</code> until then. <code>software --check</code> is green. A greenfield component still needs its embed and release receipts (<code>host-lifecycle receipt --record</code>), and adding a <code>.host-software</code> component forces its mention in the <code>STRUCTURE.md</code> components home or reconcile HAZARDs by coverage.</p> <p><strong>GOTCHA: check authored-doc prose with <code>host-lifecycle prose</code>, not <code>host-lint --docs <file></code>.</strong> I pre-linted call/0030 through call/0032 and the plan/0049 docs with <code>host-lint --docs <file></code> (exit 0) and the commit hook passed, yet <code>software --check</code> then re-opened the verify phase: <code>host-lifecycle prose</code> flagged ten ai-diction warns (negative-parallelism, tricolon, ing-tail) that the <code>--docs <file></code> mode does not surface. The density tropes are caught by <code>host-lifecycle prose</code> (the gate engine) and by the commit-msg <code>host-lint --stdin</code> (it flagged the antithesis in my own fix commit's message), so a doc can pass the per-file <code>--docs</code> check and the hook while still failing the gate. Before committing authored markdown, run <code>host-lifecycle prose</code>, and watch antithesis ("not X but Y"), three-beat lists, and trailing -ing clauses in prose and in commit messages. See [[prose-clean-on-front-door-trigger]] and [[ai-diction-traps-in-my-writing]].</p> <p><strong>GOTCHA: do not name a group of tasks by ordinal; host-lint will not catch it.</strong> When I gave plan/0049 a build-sequence task graph, I named the task groups by ordinal (the rejected position-naming the milestone rule forbids). It passed <code>host-lint</code>, <code>host-lifecycle prose</code>, and <code>software --check</code>, and the operator caught it by eye. Two gaps, filed as https://github.com/connollydavid/host-lifecycle/issues/4 with plan/0049 as the case study: the task model has no sanctioned construct for a group of tasks (so an author reaches for an ordinal name), and host-lint's VOCABULARY does not carry the ordinal-group form (a <code>gather</code> candidate, plan/0035). For now: name every task and task group by content, and let the order live in the <code>depends</code> edges. The fix renamed the groups to <code>text-cheap-kinds</code>, <code>office-mail-fixed-layout</code>, <code>recognition-and-engineering</code>, <code>overlay</code>, and <code>spec-and-release</code>. See [[no-forge-word]] for the related ban-by-name discipline.</p> <h3 id="2026-06-27--host-reference-expand-wave-skeletons-are-structure-not-content"><a class="header" href="#2026-06-27--host-reference-expand-wave-skeletons-are-structure-not-content">2026-06-27 — host-reference expand wave: skeletons are structure, not content</a></h3> <p>The expand-wave readers (data extensions ndjson/tsv/ipynb/toml; the config crate ini/properties/env; the calendar crate ics/vcf; the columnar crate parquet/arrow) confirmed the core principle: a Tier-0 skeleton is the SHAPE of a document (keys and types, columns and row counts, sections, component tallies, a vCard's property union), never the values. That shape is where the token saving lives (a Jupyter notebook 182→41, a Parquet file 721→28); the full content stays in the Tier-1 view. Three supporting lessons held across the wave. Determinism is met by reading only what re-derives identically everywhere: a dotenv emits keys, not interpolated values; the columnar reader reads file metadata, not data pages, so no compression codec is linked; and a binary fixture is generated in-test by the pinned writer, not committed. <code>cargo-deny</code> reviews each reader's licence as it lands (the config crate added the permissive BSD-3-Clause and CC0-1.0). And a no-fix informational advisory (RUSTSEC-2024-0436, <code>paste</code> unmaintained via parquet) takes a documented per-id ignore, so the lane still catches real vulnerabilities. Each reader is a feature-gated crate (call/0033): the light ones join the text-cheap default, the heavier ones (calendar, columnar) stay opt-in.</p> <h3 id="2026-06-28--in-test-zip-fixtures-must-use-stored-not-deflated"><a class="header" href="#2026-06-28--in-test-zip-fixtures-must-use-stored-not-deflated">2026-06-28 — in-test zip fixtures must use Stored, not Deflated</a></h3> <p>A binary fixture generated in the conformance test (the office OOXML package, the EPUB zip) whose content id, the sha of the bytes, feeds the committed golden must build its zip entries with <code>CompressionMethod::Stored</code>, not <code>Deflated</code>, plus a fixed mtime. Deflate output varies with zip feature unification across the workspace, so the office docx golden passed under <code>cargo test -p host-reference-office</code> in isolation yet failed under a full <code>cargo test</code> as the bytes drifted by a few bytes (different content id, different raw token count). Stored entries are deflate-independent, so the content id is stable in any build configuration. This applies to every future zip-backed fixture (3MF, more office formats). Two process notes: verify in the full workspace, not just the single crate; and the broken commit landed because the shell command did not gate the git step on the test result, so gate commits on a green test.</p> <h3 id="2026-06-28--stage-the-denytoml-entry-with-its-reader-the-dev-disk-filled"><a class="header" href="#2026-06-28--stage-the-denytoml-entry-with-its-reader-the-dev-disk-filled">2026-06-28 — stage the deny.toml entry with its reader; the dev disk filled</a></h3> <p>Two operational gotchas from the office wave. When a reader adds a licence to deny.toml's allow-list, stage that deny.toml change in the SAME commit as the reader. The epub crate's Zlib entry was added but not staged with it, so the pinned epub state's cargo-deny lane was actually missing Zlib and only the local working copy (with the uncommitted entry) passed; a fresh-clone CI check would have failed. It surfaced when a later <code>git status</code> still showed deny.toml modified. Separately, the dev disk (/mnt/c, a WSL-mounted Windows drive) reached 100% full and git could not write the commit object ("unable to write loose object file", exit 128, not a test failure); <code>cargo clean</code> in the worktree reclaimed 11.4 GiB and unblocked it, and a full host-reference build leaves target/ around 12 GiB.</p> <h3 id="2026-06-28--the-recognition-split-puts-ocr-and-transcription-in-overlay-not-the-reader-wave"><a class="header" href="#2026-06-28--the-recognition-split-puts-ocr-and-transcription-in-overlay-not-the-reader-wave">2026-06-28 — the recognition split puts OCR and transcription in #overlay, not the reader wave</a></h3> <p>plan/0049's #recognition-and-engineering wave landed the ATTESTED, deterministic readers of the recognition split (call/0030): image format, dimensions, and EXIF; audio-visual container metadata under a new AudioVisual modality; the EDA tallies (KiCad, Gerber, Eagle); and the engineering-geometry parsers (STL, glTF, DXF, OBJ, PLY, G-code). The machine-learning half (OCR text over an image, the transcript of audio-visual media) is non-deterministic inference and cannot carry a byte-for-byte conformance golden, so it rides the provider-agnostic overlay adapter and lands in the SEPARATE downstream #overlay node. A goal phrased "image+OCR" therefore spans two task-graph nodes: the image reader here, the OCR adapter in #overlay. The attested image and av readers declare <code>ocr: false</code>, their honest capability. Do not force OCR into the deterministic-reader plugin pattern; "act within scope of previous plugins" means the deterministic readers.</p> <h3 id="2026-06-28--static-committed-binary-fixtures-give-a-build-config-independent-golden"><a class="header" href="#2026-06-28--static-committed-binary-fixtures-give-a-build-config-independent-golden">2026-06-28 — static committed binary fixtures give a build-config-independent golden</a></h3> <p>The geometry, eda, image, and av readers take inputs that are committed as STATIC fixture files (a real PNG, a JPEG with EXIF, a WAV, an H.264 MP4, an STL, a Gerber, and the rest), generated once with PIL, ffmpeg, and python's <code>wave</code> module. Because the content id is the sha of the committed bytes and the reader only DECODES the fixed file with no re-encoding, the golden is independent of build configuration. This sidesteps the in-test-generated-zip determinism trap (the earlier office Deflated-versus-Stored bug) entirely, since there is no test-time generation to vary under feature unification. Prefer a static committed fixture over in-test generation whenever the input is binary. Two as-built pin notes: KiCad reads through the generic <code>lexpr</code> S-expression form tally because <code>kiutils_kicad</code> reads from a path, not the bytes a <code>Source</code> carries; audio-visual reads through <code>symphonia</code> (audio) and the <code>mp4</code> crate (video) rather than the pre-build research's <code>mp4parse</code> and <code>lofty</code>, which were not needed.</p> <h3 id="2026-06-28--ocr-ships-out-of-process-the-operator-overruled-the-overlay-deferral"><a class="header" href="#2026-06-28--ocr-ships-out-of-process-the-operator-overruled-the-overlay-deferral">2026-06-28 — OCR ships out-of-process: the operator overruled the overlay deferral</a></h3> <p>I scoped OCR out of the recognition wave (deferred to <code>#overlay</code>) as non-deterministic ML. The operator overruled that through the <code>/goal</code> Stop hook, requiring image and OCR delivered together, then named the route: "this is what our out-of-process API is for." The resolution is <code>call/0034</code>. The only pure-Rust OCR engine, <code>ocrs</code> over <code>rten</code>, carries CC-BY-SA-4.0 model weights, a content-copyleft licence the permissive component must not absorb, so the <code>call/0033</code> arms-length rule built for GPL applies. A separate <code>host-reference-ocr-helper</code> binary embeds the engine and the CC-BY-SA models; the permissive <code>host-reference-ocr</code> plugin writes the image to a temp file, runs the helper as a separate process, and reads the recognised text from stdout. That is an aggregation rather than a linkage, so the plugin and its dependents stay permissive. The plugin implements the same <code>Normalizer</code> interface as the in-process readers, the <code>call/0033</code> interface test, reached before OpenSCAD. Determinism: <code>ocrs</code> is run-to-run identical on a host with the pinned vendored models, enough for a byte-for-byte golden; cross-host bit-determinism is unproven and is the open edge for the release task.</p> <p>Two lessons. Methodology: a "deferred to a later node" descope can be overruled toward fold-in (see [[review-findings-are-requirements-not-descope]]), and the out-of-process boundary serves content-copyleft (CC-BY-SA), not only code-copyleft (GPL). Technical: <code>ocrs</code> <code>ImageSource</code> needs the explicit <code>from_tensor</code> with an <code>NdTensor</code> of shape <code>[H, W, C]</code> and <code>DimOrder::Hwc</code> plus <code>rten_tensor::prelude::*</code> for <code>.view()</code>; the <code>from_bytes((h, w))</code> path misread the channels and returned garbage. The conformance test builds the helper on demand via <code>env!("CARGO")</code> and points the plugin at it through <code>HOST_REFERENCE_OCR_HELPER</code>.</p> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><blockquote> <p><strong>Superseded.</strong> Retained as immutable history, not current guidance.</p> </blockquote> <h1 id="use-markdown-decision-records-madr"><a class="header" href="#use-markdown-decision-records-madr">Use Markdown decision records (MADR)</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: superseded by the methodology spine (host-template @ 94a1ac7)</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-14</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-19"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-19">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>Architectural decisions for the host methodology need a durable, citable home, separate from MEMORY.md (lessons learned, backward-looking) and PLAN.md (the milestone index). Without one, the reasoning behind a choice evaporates and gets re-litigated.</p> <h2 id="decision-17"><a class="header" href="#decision-17">Decision</a></h2> <p>Adopt MADR (Markdown Architectural Decision Records) for this log:</p> <ul> <li>Files are named <code>NNNN-topic-slug.md</code>, zero-padded to width four, numbered monotonically from this <code>0000</code> bootstrap, lowercase-and-dashes, no <code>ADR-</code> prefix.</li> <li>A record's number is its identity, not a sort key; this log is the ordering authority.</li> <li>Numbers are assigned at merge, so two branches do not collide on the same <code>NNNN</code>.</li> <li>Disambiguation by home: a bare number at the plan root names a milestone; a numbered file under <code>call/</code> names a decision.</li> </ul> <h2 id="consequences-19"><a class="header" href="#consequences-19">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: decisions carry structured status and supersession and a stable, citable identity; this log documents its own format as record zero.</li> <li>Neutral: this is one of two numbered registers (milestones and decisions), told apart by home, kept explicit to avoid confusion.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><blockquote> <p><strong>Superseded.</strong> Retained as immutable history, not current guidance.</p> </blockquote> <h1 id="tool-outputs-are-project-owned-not-under-allium-or-specula-licenses"><a class="header" href="#tool-outputs-are-project-owned-not-under-allium-or-specula-licenses">Tool outputs are project-owned, not under allium or Specula licenses</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: superseded by the methodology spine (host-template @ 94a1ac7)</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-14</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-20"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-20">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>The host methodology composes three referenced tools as submodules: host-lint (Unlicense, ours), allium (MIT, JUXT Ltd), and Specula (Apache-2.0, specula-org). Running them produces artifacts inside our project: allium's <code>propagate</code> emits obligations; Specula emits a modeling brief, <code>base.tla</code> / <code>MC.tla</code> specs, traces, and bug reports into <code>.specula-output/</code>. We need a recorded position on whether those generated artifacts carry the tools' licenses, so our project content stays license-clean and we avoid accidental contamination.</p> <h2 id="considered-options-13"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-13">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li>Treat generated artifacts as project-owned, outside the tools' license scope.</li> <li>Treat them as derivative works of the tools (carrying MIT / Apache-2.0).</li> <li>Never generate into our tree (run the tools out-of-tree only).</li> </ol> <h2 id="decision-outcome-3"><a class="header" href="#decision-outcome-3">Decision Outcome</a></h2> <p>Chosen option 1: <strong>artifacts the tools generate from our own input are our project's work product, outside allium's and Specula's license scope</strong>, the same way a compiled binary is not a derivative of the compiler that produced it.</p> <h3 id="basis-verified-against-the-source-repos"><a class="header" href="#basis-verified-against-the-source-repos">Basis (verified against the source repos)</a></h3> <ul> <li>Both licenses govern only "the Work" / "the Software" (the tools' own files), not artifacts produced by running them. allium ships standard MIT (© JUXT Ltd) with no output clause. Specula ships standard Apache-2.0 whose "Derivative Works" definition refers to works based on Specula's code, not specs that model our system. Specula's DCO governs inbound contributions only.</li> <li>Both tools frame outputs as the user's: allium "generates tests from the formal behaviours of your system"; Specula writes outputs to <code>.specula-output/</code> under the project root.</li> <li>Both authors chose permissive licenses; a verification tool that claimed the specs you write about your own system would be self-defeating. So this reading is consistent with (not contrary to) the authors' intent.</li> </ul> <h3 id="carve-out"><a class="header" href="#carve-out">Carve-out</a></h3> <p>Verbatim boilerplate a tool injects (a Specula trace-module or test-rig skeleton, an allium obligation template) remains part of that tool's Work and keeps its license. We reference such boilerplate (for example <code>EXTENDS</code> or an include from the submodule), never vendor it into our tree.</p> <h2 id="consequences-20"><a class="header" href="#consequences-20">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: generated specs and bug reports under a milestone's <code>spec/</code> are project-owned and Unlicense-clean, with no attribution obligation downstream.</li> <li>Good: the tools stay in their submodules under their own licenses; the separation is clean by construction.</li> <li>Risk: the cleanliness depends on referencing tool boilerplate rather than copying it; a generated artifact that embeds tool source verbatim carries that fragment's license.</li> <li>Note: this is practical reasoning, not legal advice; revisit before any commercially load-bearing use.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><blockquote> <p><strong>Superseded.</strong> Retained as immutable history, not current guidance.</p> </blockquote> <h1 id="split-verification-into-lanes-by-property-type"><a class="header" href="#split-verification-into-lanes-by-property-type">Split verification into lanes by property type</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: superseded by the methodology spine (host-template @ 94a1ac7)</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-14</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-21"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-21">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>Correctness has distinct property classes, and no single tool covers them all well. Functional and data invariants suit property-based testing; temporal, ordering, and concurrency invariants need full state-space exploration; and neither catches naming or process slop. We need a clear division so each concern is routed to the tool that actually covers it.</p> <h2 id="considered-options-14"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-14">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li>One tool for everything (forces a single tool to cover properties it cannot).</li> <li>Lanes by property type: route each concern to the tool suited to it.</li> </ol> <h2 id="decision-outcome-4"><a class="header" href="#decision-outcome-4">Decision Outcome</a></h2> <p>Chosen option 2: <strong>three lanes, one per property class.</strong></p> <div class="table-wrapper"><table><thead><tr><th>Lane</th><th>Property class</th><th>Tool</th><th>License</th></tr></thead><tbody> <tr><td>Hygiene</td><td>naming and process slop (agentic tells)</td><td>host-lint</td><td>Unlicense</td></tr> <tr><td>Requirements + property-based testing</td><td>functional and data invariants ("for all inputs")</td><td>allium</td><td>MIT</td></tr> <tr><td>Timing + concurrency</td><td>temporal, ordering, interleaving invariants ("for all interleavings"), via model checking</td><td>Specula / TLA+</td><td>Apache-2.0</td></tr> </tbody></table> </div> <p>TLA+ is reserved strictly for its home ground: timing and concurrency. It is not a general requirements tool; that is allium's lane.</p> <h3 id="escalation-bridge"><a class="header" href="#escalation-bridge">Escalation bridge</a></h3> <p>When an allium property depends on interleaving or timing that property-based testing cannot adequately explore, it escalates out of the property-based lane into the timing-and-concurrency lane. allium identifies such a property; Specula proves the temporal ones.</p> <h2 id="consequences-21"><a class="header" href="#consequences-21">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: every concern has a clear owner; the lanes are complementary, not overlapping.</li> <li>Good: a milestone may legitimately carry obligations in more than one lane.</li> <li>Neutral: contributors must learn which lane a property belongs to; the escalation bridge is the rule that resolves the boundary cases.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><blockquote> <p><strong>Superseded.</strong> Retained as immutable history, not current guidance.</p> </blockquote> <h1 id="compose-verification-tools-as-referenced-submodules"><a class="header" href="#compose-verification-tools-as-referenced-submodules">Compose verification tools as referenced submodules</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: superseded by the methodology spine (host-template @ 94a1ac7)</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-14</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-22"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-22">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>The three lanes (see decision 0002) are owned by tools under three different licenses: host-lint (Unlicense, ours), allium (MIT), and Specula (Apache-2.0). They must fit our milestone (decision-record) lifecycle and obligations model without contaminating our license posture, forcing us to maintain a fork, or turning a tool's internal workflow into a competing project axis.</p> <h2 id="considered-options-15"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-15">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li>Vendor each tool's files into our tree and edit them to fit.</li> <li>Reference each tool as a submodule and steer it from our own layer.</li> </ol> <h2 id="decision-outcome-5"><a class="header" href="#decision-outcome-5">Decision Outcome</a></h2> <p>Chosen option 2: <strong>each tool is a referenced submodule; we orchestrate, never edit.</strong></p> <ul> <li><strong>Reference, never vendor.</strong> Each tool is a git submodule; our methodology (CLAUDE.md) refers to its skills. Its files are never copied or edited; behaviour is steered by instruction plus thin wrappers in our own tree. This keeps us clean of Apache-2.0 obligations and keeps submodule updates trivial.</li> <li><strong>Specs are tagged by lane and co-located with tests.</strong> A spec is <code>.allium</code> (allium) or <code>.tla</code> (Specula); both live in the milestone's <code>spec/</code> area. A tool's generated artifacts (allium's behavioural spec and generated tests, Specula's modeling brief, TLA+ specs, traces, confirmed bugs) are the discharge evidence, and are project-owned per decision 0001.</li> <li><strong>A tool's workflow discharges, it does not milestone.</strong> A tool's internal pipeline runs inside one milestone to discharge that milestone's obligations; it is not a parallel milestone axis. Specula's steps are referred to by name (Code Analysis, Spec Generation, and so on), never as ordinal phases; this keeps our own host-lint convention.</li> <li><strong>Lane choice is a decision.</strong> Choosing a lane for a concern, or classifying a Specula target category, is itself an architectural decision and gets its own record.</li> <li><strong>Boilerplate is referenced, not vendored.</strong> Verbatim tool boilerplate (a Specula trace-module, an allium template) is pulled in by reference, never copied; this is the carve-out from decision 0001.</li> </ul> <h2 id="consequences-22"><a class="header" href="#consequences-22">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: clean license separation by construction; our content stays Unlicense; submodule bumps are mechanical.</li> <li>Good: one numbered milestone register, undisturbed by the tools' internal step naming.</li> <li>Risk: correctness depends on the reference-not-vendor and instruct-not-patch discipline; patching a submodule is a designed-around failure mode, not a routine tool.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><blockquote> <p><strong>Superseded.</strong> Retained as immutable history, not current guidance.</p> </blockquote> <h1 id="the-template-is-the-canonical-versioned-source-of-the-methodology"><a class="header" href="#the-template-is-the-canonical-versioned-source-of-the-methodology">The template is the canonical, versioned source of the methodology</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: superseded by the methodology spine (host-template @ 94a1ac7)</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-14</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-23"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-23">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>The methodology is stated in two places: the host's own <code>CLAUDE.md</code> (which governs this meta-repo) and the template's <code>CLAUDE.md</code> (the canonical manual shipped to projects built from the template). They share a spine (the four working principles, audited plans, append-only memory) and will drift as the methodology moves. The host also <em>builds</em> the template whose methodology it follows, which makes "where is the single source?" awkward: a template's <code>CLAUDE.md</code> is addressed to an agent working in an <em>instantiated</em> project, not in this host, and an existing exemption already forbids treating the nested template <code>CLAUDE.md</code> as live governance here.</p> <h2 id="considered-options-16"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-16">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>Diverge</strong>: declare them not-duplicates; sync the shared spine by a documented checklist when it changes.</li> <li><strong>Template canonical, host live-references it</strong>: shrink the host <code>CLAUDE.md</code> to a pointer at the template's.</li> <li><strong>Extract a shared spine</strong> both files import.</li> <li><strong>Copy-at-version</strong>: the template is the source; every project holds a copy of the spine at the revision it adopted, recorded in a stamp; upgrading re-runs a migration.</li> </ol> <h2 id="decision-outcome-6"><a class="header" href="#decision-outcome-6">Decision Outcome</a></h2> <p>Chosen option 4: <strong>the template is the canonical, versioned source; each project (the host included) holds a copy of the spine at the revision it adopted.</strong></p> <ul> <li><strong>The template authors the spine.</strong> Changes to the methodology land in the template <code>CLAUDE.md</code> first.</li> <li><strong>Every project carries a copy, pinned to a revision.</strong> A <code>.host</code> stamp at the repo root records the template revision adopted. The copy is inline and acted-on (not a fragile pointer), so governance stays strong locally.</li> <li><strong>The host is adopter zero, not a special case.</strong> It is migrated by the same protocol any project uses (decision 0005); the self-referential "compiler compiling itself" loop dissolves into ordinary adoption.</li> <li><strong>The exemption stays true.</strong> A project never <em>obeys</em> the nested template <code>CLAUDE.md</code> as live governance; it <em>copies from it</em> during a migration event. Copy-at-version is single-sourced (the template authors the revision) yet drift-managed (re-run the migration to upgrade).</li> </ul> <h2 id="consequences-23"><a class="header" href="#consequences-23">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: one source of truth for the spine; upgrades are a defined, mechanical diff between two template revisions, not a hand merge.</li> <li>Good: the host's existing template-CLAUDE.md exemption is now principled and permanent, not a stopgap.</li> <li>Cost: the spine text is duplicated into each adopter at adoption time; staleness is bounded by whether a project re-runs the upgrade migration, and made visible by the recorded revision.</li> <li>Deferred: fully reconciling the host's own <code>CLAUDE.md</code> wording with the template beyond the spine refresh (the broader duplication) is out of scope here.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><blockquote> <p><strong>Superseded.</strong> Retained as immutable history, not current guidance.</p> </blockquote> <h1 id="adopt-and-upgrade-existing-repos-by-a-cased-moded-migration-protocol"><a class="header" href="#adopt-and-upgrade-existing-repos-by-a-cased-moded-migration-protocol">Adopt and upgrade existing repos by a cased, moded migration protocol</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: superseded by the methodology spine (host-template @ 94a1ac7)</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-14</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-24"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-24">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>Decision 0004 makes the template a versioned source and says projects adopt a revision, but nothing defined <em>how</em> an existing repo becomes a host, or how one that adopted an earlier revision upgrades. Real targets show the shapes vary: a repo with no <code>CLAUDE.md</code>, a repo with a foreign <code>CLAUDE.md</code> predating the methodology (with deep ordinal-naming debt in files and history), and our own repos at an earlier revision. They also vary in appetite for disruption: some can absorb a single forward-only change; rewriting history is sometimes warranted and sometimes forbidden (when the history carries provenance others depend on).</p> <h2 id="considered-options-17"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-17">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>Ad hoc</strong>: migrate each repo by hand, deciding everything case by case.</li> <li><strong>A defined protocol</strong>: one skeleton with a small set of explicit choices.</li> </ol> <h2 id="decision-outcome-7"><a class="header" href="#decision-outcome-7">Decision Outcome</a></h2> <p>Chosen option 2: <strong>a single protocol, parameterised by two orthogonal axes, shipped as the template's <code>MIGRATION.md</code> and driven by <code>host-lifecycle</code>.</strong></p> <ul> <li><strong>Case = starting state</strong> (decides how governance is established): <strong>(a)</strong> no <code>CLAUDE.md</code>: drop in the manual and elicit the repo's own conventions; <strong>(b)</strong> foreign <code>CLAUDE.md</code>: merge each rule (subsumed / project-specific / conflict to human); <strong>(c)</strong> carries a <code>.host</code> stamp: upgrade by diffing the recorded revision against the current one. <code>host-lifecycle classify</code> reports the case.</li> <li><strong>Mode = blast radius</strong> (decides how the audit is applied): <strong>Preview</strong> (report only, always first), <strong>Shallow PR</strong> (default; live files only; history untouched), <strong>Staged</strong> (Shallow across several PRs, for volume), <strong>Deep rewrite</strong> (opt-in; archive-first, then rewrite history).</li> <li><strong>Selection rule.</strong> Default Shallow; escalate to Staged on volume; choose Deep only when history-coherence value exceeds provenance/disruption cost, and never on history carrying provenance you do not control. <strong>History is immutable by default</strong>: outside Deep, legacy tells are acknowledged, not rewritten.</li> <li><strong>The stamp keys upgrades.</strong> <code>host-lifecycle adopt</code> scaffolds the rooms and writes <code>.host</code> (template + revision + date); <code>version</code> reads it. The recorded revision is what a case-(c) upgrade diffs from.</li> <li><strong>Mechanical work is token-free.</strong> <code>host-lifecycle</code> does the classify / scaffold / stamp; <code>host-lint --all</code>/<code>--log</code> does the audit. Model effort is spent only on the case-(b) merge, case-(a) elicitation, and audit triage.</li> </ul> <h2 id="consequences-24"><a class="header" href="#consequences-24">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: adoption and upgrade are repeatable and auditable; a fresh session can read <code>MIGRATION.md</code> and the stamp and continue.</li> <li>Good: the dangerous operation (history rewrite) is gated behind an explicit mode with an archive-first rule, not reached by default.</li> <li>Cost: the protocol adds two new artifacts to maintain (the <code>MIGRATION.md</code> payload and the <code>host-lifecycle</code> verbs) and a stamp file per repo.</li> <li>Validated by dogfood: the host migrates itself (case c) under this decision; external repos follow as their own milestones.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><blockquote> <p><strong>Superseded.</strong> Retained as immutable history, not current guidance.</p> </blockquote> <h1 id="host-lint-exempts-sanctioned-vocabulary-via-a-repo-root-allow-file"><a class="header" href="#host-lint-exempts-sanctioned-vocabulary-via-a-repo-root-allow-file">host-lint exempts sanctioned vocabulary via a repo-root allow file</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: superseded by the methodology spine (host-template @ 94a1ac7)</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-14</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-25"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-25">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>The migration protocol (<code>call/0005</code>) says history is immutable by default and legacy tells are <em>acknowledged, not rewritten</em>. But the audit engine had no way to <em>express</em> an acknowledgement: <code>host-lint</code> knew only "is this a tell?", with repo policy hardcoded in a shell wrapper. So a token that is legitimate vocabulary in a given repo, a version string (<code>NT 3.1</code>, <code>DOS 6.22</code>), a release identity, a cross-repo filename one is forbidden to rename, flagged with no sanctioned way to clear it short of editing the file.</p> <p>This bit hard on the first external dogfood. The Agentic-MCP-Win32s migration (PR #1) chased "zero tells" the only way the tool allowed: by rewriting protected content (append-only <code>MEMORY.md</code> and closed, immutable milestone bodies) which violated two standing rules and dissolved precise historical identifiers (review codes, work-item codes) into vague prose. The missing capability, not the agent's judgement alone, forced the over-reach.</p> <h2 id="considered-options-18"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-18">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>An acknowledged baseline</strong>: record the existing flagged <em>occurrences</em> (by file + text), exempt exactly those, keep flagging anything new. Solves "acknowledge this historical body" precisely; carries a baseline file that must be regenerated as files move.</li> <li><strong>A sanctioned-token allow-list</strong>: list <em>phrases</em> that are legitimate vocabulary anywhere in the repo; never flag those tokens. Location-independent; simple; does not by itself protect an arbitrary historical line that happens to contain a real tell.</li> <li><strong>Both.</strong></li> </ol> <h2 id="decision-outcome-8"><a class="header" href="#decision-outcome-8">Decision Outcome</a></h2> <p>Chosen option 2: <strong>a sanctioned-token allow-list, and only that, for now.</strong> A repo-root <code>.host-lint-allow</code> file lists one phrase per line (<code>#</code> comments, blank lines ignored). Each phrase is masked out of every line before classification (case-insensitively, at word boundaries) so the occurrence never flags in any mode (<code>--stdin</code>, files, <code>--all</code>, <code>--log</code>); a missing file means no allow-list, so the feature is opt-in and behaviour is unchanged.</p> <p>The word-boundary rule keeps an entry honest: an entry masks only its exact phrase, not a longer tell that merely shares its prefix, and a <em>different</em> tell on the same line still flags (the concrete demonstration lives in host-lint's README). Masking reuses the existing classifier untouched rather than adding a parallel suppress path.</p> <p>Baseline (option 1) was <strong>deliberately deferred</strong>, not rejected on the merits. The Win32s lesson is that the right migration move is to <em>rename</em> ordinal milestone docs to content names, not to baseline their old bodies, so the acute need is exempting genuine vocabulary, which the token list covers. A per-site baseline is the correct tool for "this real tell is acknowledged legacy we will not touch" (the pgs-release history class); add it when a target concretely needs it, against a real case, rather than speculatively.</p> <p>This does <strong>not</strong> relax the methodology: the allow-list is for tokens that are not slop. The standing preference is still to name work after its content (<code>host-lint</code>'s <code>VOCABULARY.md</code>); the allow file is the escape hatch for genuine vocabulary, sized so it cannot quietly silence a real tell.</p> <h2 id="consequences-25"><a class="header" href="#consequences-25">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: a migration can acknowledge legitimate numbered vocabulary instead of rewriting protected, append-only, or immutable content, the failure mode that produced Win32s PR #1.</li> <li>Good: the rule is small and predictable (literal phrase, word-boundaried, location-independent) and adds no dependency to a deliberately dependency-free binary.</li> <li>Cost: an allow file can be abused to hide real tells; mitigated by the boundary rule, by keeping it opt-in, and by the documented "not for silencing slop" guidance. It remains a trust surface, reviewed like any config.</li> <li>Deferred: the per-site acknowledged baseline (option 1) remains unbuilt; the pgs-release dogfood is the likely forcing case.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><blockquote> <p><strong>Superseded.</strong> Retained as immutable history, not current guidance.</p> </blockquote> <h1 id="adoption-is-a-clean-break-to-a-pristine-state-upgrade-is-a-shallow-delta"><a class="header" href="#adoption-is-a-clean-break-to-a-pristine-state-upgrade-is-a-shallow-delta">Adoption is a clean break to a pristine state; upgrade is a Shallow delta</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: superseded by the methodology spine (host-template @ 94a1ac7)</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-14</li> <li>Refines: <code>call/0005</code> (does not supersede it: the cased/moded protocol stands; this corrects how mode is chosen and fixes a factual error in a target audit).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-26"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-26">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p><code>call/0005</code> made <strong>Shallow the universal default</strong> and treated history as immutable-by-default, gating any rewrite behind a provenance prohibition. Two problems surfaced while applying it.</p> <ol> <li><strong>The token ledger.</strong> A clean break costs <em>more</em> at migration time but <em>less</em> per future session: one naming system, <code>host-lint --all</code> exiting 0 outright, no dictionary to consult, no "clean-modulo-acknowledged-legacy" subtraction to re-derive each session. For an <em>active</em> repo the recurring saving dominates. Shallow's real home is the small, frequent case (a stamped repo bumping the template revision), exactly like <code>copier update</code>. Treating Shallow as the universal default mis-priced first-time adoption.</li> <li><strong>A wrong audit premise.</strong> <code>call/0005</code> and the pgs-release brief recorded that pgs-release carries <em>external</em> FFmpeg patch-series provenance forbidding a rewrite. Examination (2026-06-14) showed otherwise: pgs-release is <strong>not a fork</strong> (567 KB, no parent): it is planning docs (<code>PHASE1–14</code> + variants) plus four vendor submodules; the FFmpeg fork (<code>connollydavid/FFmpeg</code>) and its patch series are <strong>ours</strong>; the host repo's own history is ~199 commits, <strong>100% authored by us</strong>, with tells in only a minority of <code>docs:</code> subjects. The prohibition rested on a misattribution.</li> </ol> <h2 id="decision-outcome-9"><a class="header" href="#decision-outcome-9">Decision Outcome</a></h2> <p><strong>Correlate mode with intent, and define a clean break as three risk tiers.</strong></p> <ul> <li><strong>Adoption</strong> (first-time: case (a)/(b), or a first-stamping case (c)): a <em>transformation</em> of what the repo is, so a <strong>clean break to a pristine live state.</strong></li> <li><strong>Upgrade</strong> (a stamped case (c) bumping template revision X to Y): a <em>delta</em>, so <strong>Shallow.</strong> Small, frequent, non-disruptive.</li> </ul> <p>A <strong>clean break</strong> is unbundled by risk, because the tiers are not equally safe:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Live files</strong> (structure, governance, plan docs, skills): rewrite freely to content names. No ceremony. This is where most of the recurring saving lives.</li> <li><strong>Owned append-only record</strong> (<code>MEMORY.md</code>, closed milestone bodies): may be rewritten at adoption <strong>only archive-first + map-only + recorded</strong>, the document analog of a Deep history rewrite, governed by <code>CLAUDE.md</code> §6. Original archived verbatim; only mapped rename tokens substituted; unmapped identifiers (review codes, version strings, work-item codes) left byte-for-byte; the diff shows nothing but substitutions. Never free-form, never self-authorized.</li> <li><strong>Git commit history</strong>: <strong>acknowledged, not rewritten, by default</strong>, even in a clean break. Re-shaing every commit (re-pointing tags, diverging branches, stale refs) rarely beats the value of de-numbering old subjects. Reserve Deep for the exceptional case where history coherence genuinely matters <em>and</em> the history is ours.</li> </ol> <p><strong>Provenance test, corrected.</strong> The bar is "history whose shas external consumers depend on," <strong>not</strong> "touches a patch series." A patch series we author (e.g. <code>connollydavid/FFmpeg</code>) is ours and rewrite-tolerant: rebasing is its native lifecycle, and a local rewrite produces a normal v2/v3; it cannot corrupt an already-submitted series. So no target is categorically Deep-forbidden merely for having patches; the decision is cost + ownership, per <code>call/0005</code>'s selection rule.</p> <h2 id="consequences-26"><a class="header" href="#consequences-26">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: the model matches how scaffolding tools actually work (clean <em>generate</em>, incremental <em>update</em>) and stops mis-pricing adoption as a minimal diff.</li> <li>Good: corrects the pgs-release provenance error before it hardened into doctrine.</li> <li>Good: the one dangerous operation (rewriting the owned record) is gated by an explicit, verifiable discipline (archive-first, map-only, recorded) rather than forbidden outright or (as in Agentic-MCP-Win32s PR #1) done free-form under a self-invented authorization.</li> <li>Cost: "adoption = clean break" raises the one-time spend and the tier-2 fabrication risk; mitigated by the map-only discipline and by preferring the deferred <code>host-lint</code> baseline (<code>call/0006</code>) to carry <code>--all</code>-cleanliness when a record rewrite costs more than it is worth.</li> </ul> <h3 id="per-target-consequences-correcting-the-call0005-audit"><a class="header" href="#per-target-consequences-correcting-the-call0005-audit">Per-target consequences (correcting the <code>call/0005</code> audit)</a></h3> <ul> <li><strong>Agentic-MCP-Win32s</strong> (adoption): clean break. Live PHASE docs to content homes freely; <code>MEMORY.md</code> + closed bodies rewritten <strong>map-only, original archived</strong>; host git history acknowledged; record the authorization in a <code>call/</code>.</li> <li><strong>pgs-release</strong> (adoption): <strong>Staged-Shallow for the host migration, by cost, not prohibition.</strong> ~199 owned commits, 11 release tags (<code>n*-pgsN.N</code>) and two branches make a host-history Deep rewrite not worth it. Clean-break the live PHASE docs into content homes (tier 1); acknowledge host git history (tier 3); keep the release tags as identities; submodules untouched. Cleaning the ffmpeg patch subjects for upstream is a separate, <strong>permitted</strong> rebase in our fork, out of scope for the host migration.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><blockquote> <p><strong>Superseded.</strong> Retained as immutable history, not current guidance.</p> </blockquote> <h1 id="adoption-remap-is-enforced-by-a-dictionary-applied-deterministically-then-removed"><a class="header" href="#adoption-remap-is-enforced-by-a-dictionary-applied-deterministically-then-removed">Adoption remap is enforced by a dictionary applied deterministically, then removed</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: superseded by the methodology spine (host-template @ 94a1ac7)</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-14</li> <li>Relates to: <code>call/0007</code> (clean-break tier 2 is "map-only"), <code>call/0006</code> (the allow-list), <code>call/0003</code> (tools composed as submodules), <code>CLAUDE.md</code> §6 (the append-only escape valve this enforces).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-27"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-27">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p><code>call/0007</code> says the owned record may be rewritten at adoption only "map-only": substitute the documented rename tokens, invent nothing. As <strong>prose discipline</strong> this failed in Agentic-MCP-Win32s PR #1: parallel sub-agents denumbered the record independently and <strong>drifted</strong>: the same concept surfaced under names that were in no map ("the network-and-transport milestone" was invented). Two needs follow: the map-only rule must be <strong>mechanically enforced</strong>, not promised; and the migration must not leave the old vocabulary behind as a permanent artifact.</p> <h2 id="decision-outcome-10"><a class="header" href="#decision-outcome-10">Decision Outcome</a></h2> <p><strong>An enforced mapping dictionary, applied token-free, used as removable scaffold.</strong></p> <ul> <li><strong>One declared dictionary</strong>: <code>old-concept to canonical-new-name</code>, human-approved. It covers milestone names and any sub-codes the human chooses to denumber; the <strong>new name is always supplied by the human</strong>; the tool never coins one.</li> <li><strong>Deterministic applier</strong> (in <code>host-lifecycle</code>, the token-free migrator): applies <em>only</em> declared substitutions, word-bounded, archives originals first, and <strong>refuses to touch any unmapped token</strong>. No LLM in the substitution loop, so fabrication and spatial drift (inconsistent names across files/agents) are <em>structurally impossible</em>, not merely discouraged.</li> <li><strong>Completeness check</strong>: enumerates every token <code>host-lint</code> flags; each must be dispositioned as <strong>map</strong> (rename it), <strong>allow</strong> (<code>.host-lint-allow</code> vocabulary), or <strong>acknowledge</strong> (legacy residue). It fails while any flagged token is undispositioned, so there is no silent residue and no guessed mapping.</li> <li><strong>No ongoing gate; fault, policy-free.</strong> Preventing <em>future</em> drift is already <code>host-lint</code>'s job; a new numbered concept is a tell the detector catches regardless of the dictionary. An ongoing gate that resolved every reference against the dictionary would also <strong>cost tokens on every commit, forever</strong>, for drift already prevented for free. So <code>host-lint</code> stays a <strong>pure detector that faults on any tell and never consults the dictionary</strong>; all dictionary logic is confined to the one-time <code>host-lifecycle</code> applier; the engine never learns policy. After a clean remap there are no tells, so a policy-free fault is exactly right: anything that faults later is a genuine new tell to fix, not something to resolve.</li> <li><strong>Removable scaffold, applied then cleaned.</strong> Applying the map runs with the dictionary committed (so the diff reviews as <em>only</em> mapped substitutions, beside the archive). A follow-up deletes the dictionary file: the repo must not permanently carry an artifact whose content is the old ordinal names. The durable copy lives in the migration <code>call/</code> decision (documenting old-to-new is appropriate <em>there</em>) and in the apply commit, recoverable via <code>git</code>; the Rosetta stone sits in the same stratum (history/decision) as the old names it decodes; the live tree stays clean.</li> <li><strong>Coupling.</strong> Removable-scaffold requires the <strong>full</strong> clean break: the live <em>record</em> is remapped too (archived), so nothing live still references an old name. The alternative, keep+dictionary (record left verbatim), makes the dictionary load-bearing and permanent; that is the non-clean-break mode and accepts the elephant by design.</li> </ul> <h2 id="consequences-27"><a class="header" href="#consequences-27">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: "map-only" is a mechanical guarantee, not a promise; PR #1's spatial drift and fabrication cannot recur under the applier.</li> <li>Good: the completeness check makes the residual explicit and human-dispositioned (the irreducible non-mappable codes, review codes, bare version-like numbers, are surfaced and consciously allow-listed or acknowledged, never silently left or invented away).</li> <li>Good: no permanent old-vocabulary artifact and no redundant ongoing gate.</li> <li>Cost: more human input up front: a canonical name for every flagged concept. That input <em>is</em> the anti-fabrication guarantee, not overhead to optimise away.</li> <li>Builds on the trio: <code>host-lint</code> enumerates the flags (and gates new tells forever), <code>.host-lint-allow</code> dispositions vocabulary, the dictionary dispositions renames.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><blockquote> <p><strong>Superseded.</strong> Retained as immutable history, not current guidance.</p> </blockquote> <h1 id="a-migrated-project-excludes-its-append-only-record-from-the-audit"><a class="header" href="#a-migrated-project-excludes-its-append-only-record-from-the-audit">A migrated project excludes its append-only record from the audit</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: superseded by the methodology spine (host-template @ 94a1ac7)</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-14</li> <li>Refines: <code>call/0007</code> (the record tier). Builds on <code>host-lint</code> <code>.host-lintignore</code>.</li> <li>Superseded in part by <code>call/0019</code>: the <code>plan/*/README.md</code> blanket exclusion in the Decision Outcome below is <strong>retired</strong>. Editable plan docs stay linted; an irreducible literal citation is boxed in a <code>host-lint:ignore</code> fence (the per-block mechanism), not path-excluded. Only the append-only record (<code>MEMORY.md</code>) remains path-excluded. The blanket exclusion existed only because path-exclusion was the sole mechanism in 2026-06.</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-28"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-28">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p><code>call/0007</code> left the record (append-only <code>MEMORY.md</code>, closed milestone bodies) either rewritten (governed, map-only) or kept. Applying it to a real target (Agentic-MCP-Win32s) showed rewriting the record is the wrong trade: the record is a dense lab-notebook where token-substitution produces awkward prose (<code>"the bridge core bridge first-green"</code>) and ~25 review/tracking codes (<code>weed #1</code>, <code>R1</code>/<code>F1</code>/<code>G1</code>, and the like) resist content-naming. So a "full clean break" of the record is expensive and degrades it.</p> <p>The decisive fact, <strong>verified on the target</strong>: nothing re-scans the historical tree. The commit hooks lint only the commit subject and the <em>staged diff</em>; CI runs no linter (<code>mdbook</code> only). <code>host-lint --all</code> is a manual one-shot for migration events. So old names sitting in the record cost <strong>zero</strong> on an ongoing basis; they are read by no automated gate, ever.</p> <h2 id="decision-outcome-11"><a class="header" href="#decision-outcome-11">Decision Outcome</a></h2> <p><strong>A migrated project excludes its append-only record from the <code>--all</code> audit via a <code>.host-lintignore</code>, rather than rewriting it or acknowledging-modulo.</strong> The migration writes, e.g.:</p> <pre><code>MEMORY.md plan/*/README.md </code></pre> <ul> <li>The <strong>live layer</strong> (indexes, governance, skills, cross-refs) is clean-broken to content names (via the <code>remap</code> dictionary, <code>call/0008</code>) and stays in audit scope.</li> <li>The <strong>record</strong> is left verbatim and excluded, so <code>--all</code> is genuinely <code>0</code> without a rewrite and without abusing the token allow-list. Old milestone names survive only where history lives; the renamed <code>plan/<NNNN-slug>/</code> folders are the implicit old-to-new map (a reader hitting an old ordinal name in the record finds <code>plan/0004-command-execution/</code>).</li> <li><code>host-lint</code> stays general/policy-free; it honours an ignore file the way <code>git</code>/<code>ripgrep</code> do; the <em>methodology</em> supplies the policy by writing the file.</li> </ul> <p><strong>Token rationale (the deciding lens):</strong> rewriting the record is one-time cost with <strong>no</strong> recurring payback (nothing re-scans it), and risks negative value (degraded prose costs future readers). Excluding it is near-zero one-time and zero recurring. So exclusion is the token-optimal record handling. The live-layer clean break keeps its recurring payback and stays.</p> <h2 id="consequences-28"><a class="header" href="#consequences-28">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: <code>--all</code> means "no live tells," cleanly, with no record rewrite, no lossy prose, no allow-list abuse.</li> <li>Good: this is the concrete resolution of the deferred baseline question (<code>call/0006</code>) for the record class, a path exclusion, not a per-finding baseline.</li> <li>Cost: a reader auditing <em>history</em> must run <code>host-lint</code> against the excluded paths deliberately; that is the rare, correct time to look. The exclusion is explicit and reviewable in <code>.host-lintignore</code>.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><blockquote> <p><strong>Superseded.</strong> Retained as immutable history, not current guidance.</p> </blockquote> <h1 id="embed-the-software-as-a-bare-store-with-named-worktrees"><a class="header" href="#embed-the-software-as-a-bare-store-with-named-worktrees">Embed the software as a bare store with named worktrees</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: superseded by the methodology spine (host-template @ 94a1ac7)</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-14</li> <li>Relates: <code>call/0003</code> (compose tools as referenced submodules; the software embedding is the counterpart question for the "Where" room).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-29"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-29">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>The "Where" room (the software under test) has been a single git <strong>submodule</strong>: one gitlink, one pinned SHA, one working tree. That gives audit/reproducibility but only one live checkout, which serializes work. Two realistic needs break that: <strong>parallel agents</strong> on one codebase (the prevailing idiom is a bare repo + worktrees), and <strong>multiple production release branches live simultaneously</strong> (a maintained project servicing several shipped versions cannot have its single tree flip between them). The established "many live branches, one object store" idiom is a <strong>bare clone + worktrees</strong>; but that is not a gitlink, so adopting it means deciding what the host pins and how audit survives.</p> <h2 id="decision-18"><a class="header" href="#decision-18">Decision</a></h2> <p>Embed each software component as a <strong>bare object store plus named worktrees</strong>, with the pin recorded in host config rather than as a gitlink:</p> <ul> <li><strong>Layout.</strong> <code><name>.git/</code> is the bare clone (shared object store, no working tree). <code><name>/</code> is the <strong>canonical worktree</strong>, the audited state, where CI and the verification lanes run. Parallel lines are sibling worktrees <code><name>.<line>/</code>, one per agent or release branch; worktrees are never nested inside another worktree (it breaks <code>.git</code> resolution). A host may embed <strong>several</strong> software components; each <code><name></code> is its own bare store plus worktrees.</li> <li><strong>Recipe, not trees.</strong> The bare store and all worktrees are local artifacts (gitignored). The host commits a recipe (<code>.host-software</code>): <strong>one <code>[software "<name>"]</code> stanza per component</strong>, mirroring <code>.gitmodules</code>, each recording <code>url</code>, the <strong>pinned canonical SHA</strong>, and the worktree set, that a setup step materializes: <code>clone --bare</code>, set the <code>+refs/heads/*:refs/remotes/origin/*</code> fetch refspec, <code>fetch</code>, <code>worktree add <name></code> at the pinned SHA, init nested submodules per worktree, then add the parallel worktrees.</li> <li><strong>Audit moves to our tooling.</strong> With no gitlink, <code>host-lifecycle</code> owns the "is <code><name>/</code> at its recorded SHA?" check; the recipe's pinned SHA is the reproducibility anchor the gitlink used to be.</li> <li><strong>Nested submodules stay native.</strong> If the software has submodules, each worktree initializes them with <code>git submodule update --init --recursive</code> (worktrees share superproject objects, not submodule checkouts).</li> </ul> <h3 id="migration-an-existing-submodule-to-a-bare-store"><a class="header" href="#migration-an-existing-submodule-to-a-bare-store">Migration (an existing submodule to a bare store)</a></h3> <p>A project already embedding its software as a gitlink converts mechanically, <strong>preserving the software exactly</strong>, with no software commit created, rewritten, or moved:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Preserve the pin.</strong> Record the current gitlink SHA as the recipe's pinned canonical SHA; the bare+worktree state begins at precisely the commit the submodule pinned.</li> <li><strong>De-register the gitlink.</strong> <code>git rm --cached <name></code>, delete the <code>[submodule "<name>"]</code> stanza from <code>.gitmodules</code>, remove <code>.git/modules/<name></code>. The path <code><name>/</code> is freed.</li> <li><strong>Write <code>.host-software</code></strong> with <code>url</code>, <code>pin</code> (the preserved SHA), and the worktree set (at minimum the canonical <code><name></code>).</li> <li><strong>Gitignore the layout:</strong> <code>/<name>/</code>, <code>/<name>.git/</code>, <code>/<name>.*/</code>, all rebuilt from the recipe.</li> <li><strong>Path continuity is the win.</strong> The canonical worktree keeps the path <code><name>/</code>. Only the embedding mechanism changed, so every reference that pointed at <code><name>/…</code> (build commands, routing tables, hook paths) still resolves.
