Audit references for the few that named the submodule <em>qua</em> submodule.</li> <li><strong>Pointer-bump becomes pin-update.</strong> "Merge in the software repo, then bump the submodule pointer in the host as a separate commit" becomes "…then update the recipe <code>pin</code> as a separate commit." Same audit trail (a tracked one-line commit recording the software's new SHA), different mechanism; update <code>CLAUDE.md</code> and the completion step accordingly.</li> </ol> <h2 id="consequences-29"><a class="header" href="#consequences-29">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: parallel agents and multiple live release branches coexist on one object store; the canonical worktree stays the single audited, CI-run state.</li> <li>Good: symmetric, idiomatic layout; the bare store is visibly infrastructure; the canonical worktree keeps the conventional path, so migration is reference-preserving.</li> <li>Neutral: audit shifts off native <code>git submodule status</code> onto a <code>host-lifecycle</code> check against the recorded SHA; a fresh host clone no longer auto-fetches the software; the setup step (recipe, then materialize) replaces <code>submodule update --init</code> and must be documented/scripted.</li> <li>Bad / accepted: worktrees share git objects while keeping separate environments; each carries its own build output and submodule checkouts; heavy-vendor repos pay real disk per live line. This cost is <strong>required</strong>, not incidental: it is the price of holding several production releases (or several agents' branches) materialized at once, which a single tree cannot do.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><blockquote> <p><strong>Superseded.</strong> Retained as immutable history, not current guidance.</p> </blockquote> <h1 id="references-to-the-software-worktree-must-survive-its-absence"><a class="header" href="#references-to-the-software-worktree-must-survive-its-absence">References to the software worktree must survive its absence</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: superseded by the methodology spine (host-template @ 94a1ac7)</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-15</li> <li>Refines: <code>call/0010</code> (the bare-store-with-worktrees embedding).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-30"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-30">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p><code>call/0010</code> moved the software off a gitlink submodule onto a bare store with worktrees. A submodule gave the software <strong>auto-presence</strong>: <code>git checkout</code> always left at least an empty directory, so symlinks into it resolved and tree-scans did not choke. The bare-store model <strong>removes that</strong>: the worktree is genuinely absent in any fresh clone or CI job until <code>host-lifecycle software --materialize</code> runs.</p> <p>This bit immediately: the self-migration turned the host's mdBook <strong>Site CI red</strong>. <code>.claude/skills/host-lint -> ../../host-lint</code> is a tracked symlink into the worktree; with <code>book.toml src = "."</code>, mdBook scanned the dangling link (the worktree is not materialized in CI) and failed. <code>call/0010</code>'s "path continuity" (references resolve to the same <em>path</em>) was not enough; it assumed the path was <em>populated</em>. The missing guarantee is about <strong>presence</strong>, not path. And: a feature was declared "complete" while this sibling CI was red. <strong>Complete must mean the whole suite is green</strong>, not one artifact built.</p> <h2 id="decision-19"><a class="header" href="#decision-19">Decision</a></h2> <p>Treat every host reference into a software worktree as valid only where the worktree is materialized, and harden against the gap three ways:</p> <ol> <li><strong>Prevention (don't track the hazard).</strong> Do not git-track an artifact that depends on the worktree existing. The skill symlink <code>.claude/skills/host-lint</code> is now <strong>gitignored and local</strong> (recreated after <code>software --materialize</code>) so a fresh clone has no dangling worktree symlink at all.</li> <li><strong>Detection (mechanical, bounded).</strong> <code>host-lifecycle software --check</code> flags a <strong><code>HAZARD</code></strong> for any host-tracked symlink whose target resolves into a worktree path; it would dangle wherever the software is not materialized. Bounded to symlinks deliberately: a path-<em>string</em> reference in a script or config cannot be told from prose statically, so widening the scan would only reproduce the <code>host-lint --all</code> noise. Those stay on the rule + an un-materialized CI job.</li> <li><strong>The un-materialized context must be exercised, and gate "done."</strong> CI's docs build <em>is</em> a fresh-clone-without-materialize context; it already catches this class; the failure was not looking at it. So: an un-materialized CI job must exist for each runtime-critical artifact, and "done" is gated on the <strong>whole</strong> CI sweep being green, not on the feature alone.</li> </ol> <p><strong>Docs-vs-skills semantics (resolved):</strong> the published site does <strong>not</strong> include the tool skills (they were never in <code>SUMMARY.md</code>). The Site build prunes dangling symlinks; with the skill symlink now untracked, there is nothing to prune for it.</p> <h2 id="consequences-30"><a class="header" href="#consequences-30">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: the exact break is now caught mechanically (<code>--check</code> reports the hazard, exit 1) and prevented (the symlink is untracked), and the CI is green again.</li> <li>Good: the rule generalises to the upcoming migrations (Win32s, pgs, greenfield), which all carry skill symlinks, hooks, and CI that assume the old auto-presence.</li> <li>Neutral: a fresh clone must run <code>software --materialize</code> and recreate the local skill symlink before host-lint's skill is discoverable; the honest cost of the software being absent until materialized.</li> <li>Limit: detection covers the symlink class only; path-string references (a hook's binary path, a build <code>--manifest-path</code>) remain a rule-and-CI concern, not a tool gate.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><blockquote> <p><strong>Superseded.</strong> Retained as immutable history, not current guidance.</p> </blockquote> <h1 id="a-single-file-named-host-is-the-front-door-that-owns-the-adoption-and-upgrade-process"><a class="header" href="#a-single-file-named-host-is-the-front-door-that-owns-the-adoption-and-upgrade-process">A single file named <code>host</code> is the front door that owns the adoption-and-upgrade process</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: superseded by the methodology spine (host-template @ 94a1ac7)</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-16</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-31"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-31">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p><code>call/0005</code> defined the migration protocol and shipped it <em>as the template's <code>MIGRATION.md</code></em>, driven by <code>host-lifecycle</code>. But adoption has no front door: there is no single thing an agent (or a patient human) can be pointed at to turn a repo into a host and keep it current. The protocol prose also sits inside the template, which overloads it: the template is pulled toward serving a capability rather than being the versioned source of the techniques (<code>call/0004</code>).</p> <p>We want: point an agent or a human at <strong>one URL</strong>, and out comes an agentic project with all the techniques present and <strong>upgradable</strong>. The open question was <em>what owns what</em>, because a self-contained instruction file and a revision-keyed upgrade ledger cannot both be canonical without forking the spine.</p> <h2 id="considered-options-19"><a class="header" href="#considered-options-19">Considered Options</a></h2> <ol> <li><strong>Template doubles as a plugin marketplace.</strong> One URL, but inverts the template's role, making a <em>source of techniques</em> into a <em>server of capabilities</em>, the overload <code>call/0004</code> exists to avoid.</li> <li><strong>Standalone repo, multi-file plugin/skill.</strong> Cleaner separation, but more to maintain and it buries the front door inside an engine.</li> <li><strong>Standalone repo, a single instruction file named <code>host</code> that owns the <em>process</em>; the template keeps the <em>techniques</em> and the <em>ledger</em>.</strong> One file, one URL, no overload.</li> </ol> <h2 id="decision-outcome-12"><a class="header" href="#decision-outcome-12">Decision Outcome</a></h2> <p>Chosen option 3.</p> <ul> <li><strong><code>host</code> is a single instruction file</strong> in its own repo (<code>https://github.com/connollydavid/host</code>). It is the front door: an agent or a human follows it to <strong>adopt</strong>, <strong>migrate</strong>, or <strong>upgrade</strong>, and ends with an agentic project carrying the methodology's techniques.</li> <li><strong><code>host</code> is developed here as software, not as a tool submodule of this repo.</strong> It gets a <code>[software "host"]</code> stanza in <code>.host-software</code>: a bare store with a worktree, pinned by SHA, gitignored, exactly like <code>host-lint</code> (<code>call/0010</code>). It is <em>consumed</em> downstream as a submodule; it is <em>developed</em> here as a worktree.</li> <li><strong><code>host</code> owns the process; the template owns the techniques and the ledger.</strong> This is the split that keeps "upgradable" true with no fork: <ul> <li><strong><code>host</code></strong> = the adopt/migrate/upgrade <strong>procedure</strong> (what <code>MIGRATION.md</code>'s steps were). Stable; rarely edited.</li> <li><strong>template</strong> = the <strong>techniques</strong> (the <code>CLAUDE.md</code> spine, the room scaffold, the tool pins) <em>and</em> the revision-keyed <strong>upgrade ledger</strong> (<code>UPGRADING.md</code>, one <code>[upgrade "<revision>"]</code> stanza per structural migration). The ledger is data the template's own changes generate, so it lives with them.</li> </ul> </li> <li><strong>Upgradability is automatic and lockstep-free.</strong> A produced folder records its adopted template revision in <code>.host</code>. To upgrade, <code>host</code> tells the operator to run <code>host-lifecycle upgrade <dir></code>, which prints every template ledger entry newer than the stamp; apply them; re-stamp. The procedure (<code>host</code>) does not change when the template adds a ledger entry; only the template does.</li> <li><strong>Copy-at-version, not embed.</strong> A produced folder copies the spine in at the adopted revision and stamps it (<code>call/0004</code>); it does not submodule the template. (This forge embeds the template as a submodule for development convenience only; it is adopter-zero, not a produced folder.)</li> </ul> <p>This <strong>amends <code>call/0005</code></strong>: the protocol is no longer shipped <em>as the template's <code>MIGRATION.md</code></em>. Its procedural content moves to <code>host</code>; the template's <code>MIGRATION.md</code> is reduced to a pointer at <code>host</code>. <code>UPGRADING.md</code> stays in the template. The cased/moded protocol itself (<code>call/0005</code>) and the clean-break / delta distinction (<code>call/0007</code>) are unchanged; only their home moves.</p> <h2 id="consequences-31"><a class="header" href="#consequences-31">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: one URL, one file, dual-audience (agent or human). The template stops drifting toward being a capability server; <code>call/0004</code> holds.</li> <li>Good: <code>host</code> stays a stable single file because the per-revision ledger (the part that churns) remains in the template with the techniques that cause it. No lockstep between <code>host</code> and template revisions.</li> <li>Cost: the procedure and the ledger now live in two repos, so a reader must follow one reference (<code>host</code> to the template's ledger) instead of reading one document. The single-file ideal is "self-contained <em>procedure</em>", not "everything inline".</li> <li>Cost: a new repo to maintain and a new <code>.host-software</code> stanza in this forge.</li> <li>Migration of this decision itself: move <code>MIGRATION.md</code>'s steps into <code>host</code>, leave <code>UPGRADING.md</code> in the template, and replace the template <code>MIGRATION.md</code> with a pointer, tracked as follow-on work, not done by accepting this record.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><blockquote> <p><strong>Superseded.</strong> Retained as immutable history, not current guidance.</p> </blockquote> <h1 id="the-canonical-doc-site-publisher-is-host-lifecycle-book"><a class="header" href="#the-canonical-doc-site-publisher-is-host-lifecycle-book">The canonical doc-site publisher is <code>host-lifecycle book</code></a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: superseded by the methodology spine (host-template @ 94a1ac7)</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-17</li> <li>Refines: <code>call/0011</code> (worktree-absence coherence; the <code>src = "."</code> hazard).</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-32"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-32">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>The methodology mandates five rooms (Who/What/When/Where/Why/How) and two spec formats, but shipped <strong>no canonical way to publish them</strong>. <code>host-template</code> carried only a one-line note ("scope <code>src</code> to <code>docs/</code>"); every adopter therefore hand-rolled an mdBook generator, and each got the same things wrong. A complete case-(b) adoption reported it (the <code>agentic-host</code> repo issue on doc-site gaps), and four of its findings reproduce on <strong>this repository's own published site</strong>:</p> <ul> <li>The Software/Where room (the action the project exists to produce) has no page, though <code>.host-software</code> carries the data to render a stub.</li> <li>Spec bodies (<code>.allium</code>/<code>.tla</code>) are never published.</li> <li>The sidebar is in source-call order, not the five-rooms lifecycle order (Cast, the Who, sits near the bottom).</li> <li>Nothing asserts a page per room, so a half-room site ships green.</li> </ul> <p>The root cause is singular: a missing, maintained publisher. Worse, this repository's own <code>book.toml</code> set <code>src = "."</code> (the exact hazard <code>call/0011</code> warned against) and survived only because CI prunes dangling symlinks and the worktrees are absent in checkout.</p> <h2 id="decision-20"><a class="header" href="#decision-20">Decision</a></h2> <p>Ship one canonical publisher, <strong><code>host-lifecycle book</code></strong>, and route every adopter (this repository included) through it instead of a hand-rolled generator.</p> <ol> <li><strong><code>src = "docs"</code>, never <code>"."</code>.</strong> <code>book</code> writes <code>book.toml</code> scoped to a generated <code>docs/</code>, encapsulating the <code>call/0011</code> rule once, centrally, so no adopter re-derives it wrong.</li> <li><strong>Lifecycle order.</strong> <code>SUMMARY.md</code> is emitted Cast (Who), then Plan + specs (What/When), then Software/Where, then Call (Why), then Reference/CLAUDE (How), then Memory.</li> <li><strong>The Where stub is read from <code>.host-software</code>.</strong> Component, url, pin, worktrees, and the materialize command, from the committed recipe only, so it is safe in an un-materialized checkout (the worktrees themselves are never walked; <code>call/0011</code>).</li> <li><strong>Specs render.</strong> Each spec becomes a fenced code page, navigable from its milestone; the What contract is published, not listed as bare filenames.</li> <li><strong>A coverage gate.</strong> <code>host-lifecycle book --check</code> fails the build naming any room that has source material but renders no page with content; a room with no source (a fresh <code>call/</code>, a project with no software yet) is legitimately empty and skipped.</li> </ol> <p><code>book.toml</code> and <code>docs/</code> are generated output (gitignored), regenerated in CI before <code>mdbook build</code>. The publisher lives in <code>host-lifecycle</code> (the token-free generator), the reference Site workflow and the canonical-order documentation live in <code>host-template</code>, and the upgrade is recorded in the template's <code>UPGRADING.md</code> ledger (requires <code>host-lifecycle v0.6.1</code>).</p> <h2 id="consequences-32"><a class="header" href="#consequences-32">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>Good: the four findings stop reproducing on this repository's site, and every future adopter inherits one maintained, tested artifact instead of a footgun.</li> <li>Good: the <code>src = "."</code> contradiction with the template's own guidance is resolved; this repository now obeys the rule it published.</li> <li>Neutral: the doc site is now a build-time generation step (run the publisher, then mdBook) rather than a hand-maintained <code>SUMMARY.md</code>.</li> <li>Limit: prose pulled in by copy keeps its own relative links; cross-room links resolve because <code>docs/</code> mirrors the room layout, but a generator change that moves a room would need the links revisited. The coverage gate guards room presence, not link correctness.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><blockquote> <p><strong>Superseded.</strong> Retained as immutable history, not current guidance.</p> </blockquote> <h1 id="rung-discharge-requires-a-signed-verifier-pass-attestation-not-name-presence"><a class="header" href="#rung-discharge-requires-a-signed-verifier-pass-attestation-not-name-presence">Rung discharge requires a signed verifier-pass attestation, not name-presence</a></h1> <ul> <li> <p>Status: superseded by <code>call/0018</code>. The CI-signed ed25519 token requires per-adopter key management (a deal-breaker: operator burden + a fragile, human-rooted trust anchor) and complicates the bare-store/parallel-worktree model (per-project secrets + <code>.att</code> files in every worktree). <code>call/0018</code> keeps this decision's principle (<code>AVAILABLE ≠ DISCHARGED</code>, discharge needs a real PASS) but replaces the mechanism with <strong>re-derivation in a pinned toolchain</strong> (no keys, no CI overfit). The body below is the historical record of the rejected approach.</p> </li> <li> <p>Date: 2026-06-20</p> </li> <li> <p>Scope: host-prove, host-lifecycle, host-lint (the verification toolchain)</p> </li> <li> <p>Relates: <code>call/0002</code> (verification lanes by property type, the rungs extend it); <code>plan/0023</code> (the verification ladder this hardens); <code>plan/0024</code> (the milestone that implements this); connollydavid/host #8 (discharge is name-presence), #9 (bound dropped), #10-#12, #13 (LEXICON provenance). Generalizes the reproducible-build attestation already shipping in <code>.host-software</code> (<code>artifact = … <sha256></code> + <code>toolchain = …@sha256</code>, recomputed offline by <code>software --verify-build</code>).</p> </li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-33"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-33">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p><code>plan/0023</code> added deep-verification rungs (Apalache, TLAPS, Kani) discharging obligations dispositioned <code>apalache:</code>/<code>tlaps:</code>/<code>kani:</code>. The tool validated a disposition with <code>obligation_gaps</code> calling <code>src.contains(name)</code>: it checked the proof was <strong>present by name</strong>, never that it <strong>passed</strong>. So a stub, a failing check, or a vacuous <code>THEOREM X == TRUE</code> satisfied <code>obligations</code> and <code>software --check</code>; the rung sold as "stronger than a test" became weaker than one (#8). Compounding it, the soundness <strong>bound</strong> of the bounded tools (Apalache <code>--length=N</code>, Kani <code>--unwind=K</code>) was unrepresentable, dropped from <code>verdict.py</code>, and a bounded check was presented as a complete proof (#9).</p> <p><code>AVAILABLE ≠ DISCHARGED</code>: availability of a proof is not discharge of an obligation. The audience includes a weak (4B) agent who will forge or shortcut anything not mechanically enforced, and the hook path must stay network-free and fast. A committed plaintext attestation is forgeable (the 4B hand-types <code>{PASS, bound, sha}</code>); running the verifier in the hook breaks network-free.</p> <h2 id="decision-21"><a class="header" href="#decision-21">Decision</a></h2> <p><strong>An obligation discharges only against a CI-signed attestation token that binds the verifier's PASS verdict, its bound, and the digests of the proven inputs, verified offline at check time.</strong></p> <ul> <li><strong>Token:</strong> one <code>.att</code> per obligation / per cited reference, co-located with the spec in the software repo (<code>call/0010</code>, plan/0012). Fields: <code>claim</code> (the disposition verbatim), <code>verdict</code> (<code>verdict.py</code>'s exact PASS line), <code>bound</code> (required for bounded tools; <code>unbounded</code> only for TLAPS; absent ⇒ HAZARD), <code>inputs</code> (<code>git hash-object</code> of each consumed source; for LEXICON, <code>content-sha</code> of the fetched URL body), <code>tool</code> (from <code>tools.lock</code>), <code>sig</code> (ed25519 over the canonical concatenation).</li> <li><strong>Minter:</strong> the CI verification lane (the one <code>call/0002</code>/plan/0023 already require) signs with an ed25519 private key held only in CI secrets, after the verifier exits 0. The agent commits the <code>.att</code>; CI fails if it does not match a fresh run (no write-back).</li> <li><strong>Consumer:</strong> <code>host-lifecycle obligations</code>/<code>software --check</code> verify the signature against a public key shipped in the binary, recompute the input digests (mismatch ⇒ stale ⇒ HAZARD), and require <code>verdict</code> be a PASS word and <code>bound</code> ≥ the declared bound. This replaces <code>src.contains</code>.</li> </ul> <p>The same token grounds LEXICON (#13) reference provenance: a network-having lane binds and signs the cited URL's <code>content-sha</code>, which the offline gate verifies; the weak agent cannot fabricate a URL whose CI-fetched body-hash matches a token it never obtained.</p> <h2 id="consequences-33"><a class="header" href="#consequences-33">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li><strong>Weak agents are hard-gated; strong agents keep authoring latitude.</strong> No shortcut mints a token without a real CI pass; a strong agent authors the proof/bound freely, but the output is verified, not trusted, and a later silent weakening re-stales the token via the input digest.</li> <li><strong>Bound becomes first-class:</strong> <code>verdict.py</code> must emit it from the real invocation before it can be signed (the #9 fix is a prerequisite, not an add-on).</li> <li><strong>Property triviality is explicitly NOT closed.</strong> The token attests that the verifier ran and passed at bound N, not that the property is meaningful; a vacuous proof still passes. Whether a proof proves the <em>right</em> thing stays a human/strong-agent review concern; it is not mechanizable, and this decision does not claim to gate it.</li> <li><strong>Key management is now soundness-critical</strong> (a leaked CI key forges tokens); key rotation is a future <code>call/</code> decision. A new crypto dependency (ed25519 verify) enters host-lifecycle.</li> </ul> <h2 id="alternatives-considered-2"><a class="header" href="#alternatives-considered-2">Alternatives considered</a></h2> <ul> <li><strong>Plaintext committed attestation:</strong> forgeable by a 4B (hand-typed); relocates #8, does not fix it.</li> <li><strong>Run the verifier in the hook / on <code>--check</code>:</strong> sound but breaks the network-free, fast hook constraint (Kani/Apalache/TLAPS are heavy); CI-only, it is just "the lanes already run it."</li> <li><strong>Query live CI status:</strong> network + platform coupling, unavailable offline / fresh-clone / non-GitHub, carries no bound, forgeable via workflow YAML.</li> </ul> <div style="break-before: page; page-break-before: always;"></div><blockquote> <p><strong>Superseded.</strong> Retained as immutable history, not current guidance.</p> </blockquote> <h1 id="every-lifecycle-phase-discharges-to-a-tool-written-receipt-release-is-a-strict-tool-carried-phase"><a class="header" href="#every-lifecycle-phase-discharges-to-a-tool-written-receipt-release-is-a-strict-tool-carried-phase">Every lifecycle phase discharges to a tool-written receipt; release is a strict, tool-carried phase</a></h1> <ul> <li>Status: superseded by the methodology spine (host-template @ 617e420, where the lifecycle manifest makes every phase emit a receipt)</li> <li>Date: 2026-06-20</li> <li>Scope: host-lifecycle, host-template (the lifecycle tool and the spine it reads)</li> <li>Relates: <code>call/0016</code> (the attestation primitive, same idea, here applied to phases not proofs); <code>plan/0022</code> (the <code>.host</code> applied-set this generalizes from upgrades to all phases); <code>plan/0005</code> / <code>call/0010</code> (<code>repro-exempt</code>, the migrated-software escape pattern reused for release); <code>plan/0024</code> (the attestation infra the strict release consumes); connollydavid/host #14 (verify-build in the pinned toolchain, a strict-release prerequisite). Implemented by <code>plan/0025</code>.</li> </ul> <h2 id="context-and-problem-statement-34"><a class="header" href="#context-and-problem-statement-34">Context and Problem Statement</a></h2> <p>The lifecycle (classify / adopt / embed / remap / verify / publish / upgrade) is realised as scattered skills + <code>host-lifecycle</code> subcommands. The phase <strong>order</strong> lives only in spine prose, re-typed in three places (CLAUDE.md, STRUCTURE.md, UPGRADING.md); there is no tool-readable manifest, and no <code>release</code> phase at all, so every software release is hand-orchestrated with raw git/cargo/docker.</p> <p>This session demonstrated the failure mode directly. A <strong>strong</strong> agent (the author of this decision), with the rules in hand, skipped the <code>verify</code> gate and hand-rolled a release, nearly mis-recording the artifact hash (the byte-identity assumption). A <strong>weak</strong> agent (Fen, the real 4B) skips structurally; it cannot assemble multi-step orchestration. Instructions are advisory; they fail for <strong>both</strong> classes, for different reasons. The methodology already has nine receipt-like mechanisms (obligation <code>waived:</code>, the <code>.host</code> applied-set, <code>upgrade --unverified call/NNNN</code>, <code>repro-exempt = call/NNNN</code>, the <code>software --verify-build</code> artifact re-derivation, <code>.host-lintignore</code>, <code>call/</code> <code>Status:</code>, <code>.host-software</code> artifact hashes, append-only MEMORY/PLAN descopes), and most already record skips. But no single format binds them, so a skip recorded in one mechanism is invisible to the gate of another.</p> <h2 id="decision-22"><a class="header" href="#decision-22">Decision</a></h2> <ol> <li> <p><strong>Universal receipts.</strong> Every lifecycle phase, on every project, discharges to an append-only, <strong>tool-written</strong> receipt: <code>done</code> (with machine-checkable evidence) or <code>skip</code>/<code>n-a</code> (with a recorded reason: free text for the trivial, a <code>call/NNNN</code> citation for the substantive). A phase named in the manifest with <strong>no</strong> receipt is the <em>sole</em> defect <code>software --check</code> gates. Silent skipping becomes mechanically impossible, equally for weak and strong agents: a weak agent cannot forge <code>done</code> evidence, a strong agent cannot omit the receipt. The receipt unifies the nine existing mechanisms under one format and one gate.</p> </li> <li> <p><strong>A tool-readable lifecycle manifest</strong> in the spine (host-template), single-source, replaces the three prose copies. Each phase carries its <strong>modality</strong>: <code>once</code> (classify/adopt/remap), <code>continuous</code> (verify/publish), <code>per-revision</code> (upgrade), <code>conditional-on-Where</code> + <code>recurring-per-component</code> (embed, release), plus its command/skill and the shape of its evidence. <strong>release</strong> joins as a first-class phase.</p> </li> <li> <p><strong>Strict, tool-carried release.</strong> <code>host-lifecycle release</code> orchestrates the release as single commands (<code>--next</code> / <code>--record</code>): run <code>verify</code> (refuse on red), then build <strong>in the recorded toolchain</strong> (host#14), then require a CI attestation (plan/0024) so <strong>BUILT ≠ RELEASED</strong>, then re-pin <code>.host-software</code>, then tag, then write the release receipt; <code>software --check</code> re-verifies every receipt. Strict means <em>maximum mechanical enforcement, minimum agent steps</em>; the tool carries it so Fen can execute it (the resolution of the Fen ↔ Orin/Bly tension the persona review surfaced: soundness is non-negotiable for Orin/Bly, un-orchestratable by Fen; the tool, not the agent, holds the sequence).</p> </li> <li> <p><strong>Migrated escape.</strong> A case-(b) component may discharge <code>release</code> as <code>skip: call/NNNN</code>, which mirrors <code>repro-exempt</code> (it keeps its own foreign release process). The escape is itself a receipt: recorded, not silent.</p> </li> </ol> <h2 id="consequences-34"><a class="header" href="#consequences-34">Consequences</a></h2> <ul> <li>The spine's "all lifecycle phases are <strong>unconditional</strong>, no opt-out" rule is <strong>revised</strong>: the invariant becomes "every phase emits a <strong>receipt</strong>," not "every phase <strong>runs</strong>." Conditionality and recurrence are expressed as receipt <em>types</em>, never as silent absence. This is a real spine semantics change, delivered through an UPGRADING entry (honest, not free).</li> <li>The strict release <strong>depends on</strong> host#14 and plan/0024; a shallow interim is explicitly rejected (the operator's "build the strictest possible"). Build order is software-first: host#14, then attestation, then host-lifecycle (manifest parse + receipts + release), then spine manifest, then dogfood.</li> <li>The receipts ledger is <strong>copy-at-version sensitive</strong>: a project behind on upgrades carries an older manifest, so enforcement is only as current as its last upgrade. Mitigation: the manifest ships via UPGRADING; <code>software --check</code> degrades (warns) on a missing/old manifest rather than crashing. This is in tension with plan/0022's honest-partial-upgrades and is recorded, not resolved here.</li> <li>Attestation key-management risk is inherited from <code>call/0016</code>.</li> </ul> <h2 id="alternatives-considered-3"><a class="header" href="#alternatives-considered-3">Alternatives considered</a></h2> <ul> <li><strong>Spec-only modality typing, no receipts:</strong> encodes conditionality yet still permits the <em>silent</em> skip of a phase that does apply; it does not gate the strong-agent failure this session exhibited.</li> <li><strong>Shallow release orchestration</strong>: rejected by the operator ("strictest possible") and by Orin/Bly: a shallow release lets a stale or unattested artifact ship, and a later cold read cannot catch it.</li> <li><strong>A new orchestrator tool</strong>: rejected: it fragments lifecycle ownership across two binaries; host-lifecycle already owns every phase. (The operator's earlier skepticism, "are you correct in suggesting a new one?", was right.)</li> </ul> <h2 id="review-hardening-post-adversarial-review"><a class="header" href="#review-hardening-post-adversarial-review">Review hardening (post-adversarial-review)</a></h2> <p>An adversarial review of the implementing milestone (<code>plan/0025/design-review.md</code>, 39 findings, proceed-with-changes) confirmed the decision but forced the mechanism to be strictly mechanical; the first cut's receipts were too self-asserted. The decision holds as written, refined by:</p> <ul> <li><strong>A receipt is never a plaintext self-assertion.</strong> <code>done</code> requires a re-runnable <code>recheck =</code> post-condition or signed/digest evidence; a phase whose evidence cannot be re-derived offline may not be <code>done</code>. (Inherits, not weakens, <code>call/0016</code>'s anti-forgery standard.)</li> <li><strong><code>n-a</code> is tool-computed from project state</strong>, not agent-asserted; a protected core (<code>verify</code>) is un-skippable; phase modality constrains the legal dispositions, gate-enforced.</li> <li><strong>The skip escape is content-validated</strong>: the cited decision is parsed (accepted Status, a Scope/<code>authorizes-skip:</code> header naming this phase+component, content-hashed), allowed only for recipe-proven migrated components, never greenfield. Mere file-existence is insufficient.</li> <li><strong>The manifest is read at the adopted revision; a missing one HAZARDs</strong> (never a silent pass).</li> <li><strong>Release has no degraded mode</strong>: plan/0024 is a hard prerequisite; absent it, an artifact-bearing release blocks. The tool computes the version and the hash; irreversible mutations (tag, re-pin) run last and resumably, after attestation.</li> </ul> </main> <nav class="nav-wrapper" aria-label="Page navigation"> <!-- Mobile navigation buttons --> <div style="clear: both"></div> </nav> </div> </div> <nav class="nav-wide-wrapper" aria-label="Page navigation"> </nav> </div> <script> window.playground_copyable = true; </script> <script src="elasticlunr.min.js"></script> <script src="mark.min.js"></script> <script src="searcher.js"></script> <script src="clipboard.min.js"></script> <script src="highlight.js"></script> <script src="book.js"></script> <!-- Custom JS scripts --> <script> window.addEventListener('load', function() { window.setTimeout(window.print, 100); }); </script> </div> </body> </html>
